**Part (b) — Pareto Chart Construction:**

**Step 1:** Tabulate defects in descending order of frequency and compute cumulative percentage.

| # | Defect Type | Frequency | Cumulative Frequency | Cumulative % |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Seal Failure | 45 | 45 | 45.0 % |
| 2 | Label Misprint | 20 | 65 | 65.0 % |
| 3 | Box Damage | 15 | 80 | 80.0 % |
| 4 | Color Mismatch | 12 | 92 | 92.0 % |
| 5 | Wrong Quantity | 8 | 100 | 100.0 % |
| | **Total** | **100** | | |

**Step 2:** Identify the "Vital Few."

Pareto's 80/20 rule: The top **3 defect types** (Seal Failure, Label Misprint, Box Damage) account for **80%** of all defects. These are the **"vital few"** that warrant immediate attention. The remaining 2 (Color Mismatch, Wrong Quantity) are the *"trivial many"* accounting for only 20%.

**Step 3:** Pareto Chart Schematic.

```mermaid
%%{init: {"xyChart": {"xAxis": {"labelFontSize": 12}, "yAxis": {"labelFontSize": 12}}}}%%
xychart-beta
    title "Pareto Chart: Packaging Defects"
    x-axis ["Seal Fail", "Label Print", "Box Damage", "Color", "Qty"]
    y-axis "Frequency" 0 --> 50
    bar [45, 20, 15, 12, 8]
```

**Step 4 — Quality Improvement Action Plan:**

| Priority | Defect | Recommended Action |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 (Vital) | **Seal Failure** (45) | Investigate sealing temperature, dwell time, and pressure. Conduct a Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) analysis on the sealing station. Check machine calibration. |
| 2 (Vital) | **Label Misprint** (20) | Audit printer ribbon/ink supplier; check label alignment sensor; implement poka-yoke (error-proofing) on the print head. |
| 3 (Vital) | **Box Damage** (15) | Inspect material handling process; replace worn conveyor belts; train operators on stacking technique. |
| 4 (Trivial) | Color Mismatch (12) | Monitor trend; address only if frequency increases. |
| 5 (Trivial) | Wrong Quantity (8) | Check counting sensor; verify check-weigher settings. |

**Conclusion:** By focusing on the vital 3, the line can eliminate up to 80% of defects. The remaining 20% are monitored but do not require immediate engineering intervention. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing QA with QC** in the first question. Use the "process vs product" mnemonic.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3σ justification** in SPC derivations. Always show *why 3σ and not 2σ or 4σ* (probability = 0.0027; balance of Type I/II error).
> 3. **Reporting only Cp without Cpk** in capability questions. Board deducts 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Wrong chart selection** (using X-bar for attribute data or p-chart for variable data). Always check the data type first.
> 5. **Skipping the control chart constants** in tables. The constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ must be *quoted* from the standard table, not invented.
> 6. **Not setting negative LCL to zero** in p and c charts. A negative proportion or defect count is physically impossible.
> 7. **Pareto chart missing cumulative % curve** — it is *not* a Pareto chart without the second axis showing cumulative percentage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Quality Control (Module 1)**

- **QC vs QA vs QM:** QC is the *detective / inspection* layer; QA is *process-oriented*; QM is *strategic*.
- **Shewhart (1924)** invented the control chart at Bell Labs — 3σ limits are his legacy.
- **Two variations:** *Common cause* (inherent → fix the system) vs *Special cause* (assignable → fix the specific event).
- **Deming's 94/6 rule:** ~94% of quality problems are system-caused (management's job).
- **Seven QC Tools (Ishikawa):** Fishbone, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart.
- **Pareto 80/20:** Vital few cause 80% of the problem; trivial many cause 20%.
- **Chart selection logic:**
  - Variable data, small n → **$\bar{X}$ & R** chart
  - Variable data, large n → **$\bar{X}$ & S** chart
  - Variable data, n = 1 → **I-MR** (Individuals) chart
  - Attribute, variable n → **p-chart**
  - Attribute, constant n, defective count → **np-chart**
  - Attribute, constant n, defect count → **c-chart**
  - Attribute, variable area, defect count → **u-chart**
- **3σ limits** = UCL = $\mu + 3\sigma$, LCL = $\mu - 3\sigma$, $P = 0.0027$ outside.
- **Control chart constants** for n = 4: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$, $d_2 = 2.059$.
- **Cp** = potential capability (centered); **Cpk** = actual capability (off-center); $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$.
- **Cpk ≥ 1.33** = industry-acceptable; **Cpk ≥ 1.67** = world-class (Six Sigma).
- **Process in control ≠ Process capable.** Stability is a prerequisite, not a guarantee of capability.
- **Acceptance Sampling risks:** Producer's risk = $\alpha$ (5%); Consumer's risk = $\beta$ (10%).
- **AQL** is from the producer's view; **LTPD** is from the consumer's view. They are *not* the same.
- **OC curve formula:** $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ — probability of accepting a lot of quality p.
- **Inspection types:** 100% (expensive but thorough), Sampling (statistical), No inspection (when cost of inspection > cost of defect).
- **PDCA cycle (Deming):** Plan → Do → Check → Act. "Check" is where QC operates.
- **Taguchi Loss Function:** Quality loss begins the moment the characteristic deviates from target, even within specs. Society pays.
- **Juran Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement.
- **Crosby's Four Absolutes:** Quality = conformance to requirements; Prevention, not inspection; Zero defects performance standard; Measure by price of non-conformance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance

# 1. Quality Assurance — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Assurance (QA)** is a systematic, planned set of activities embedded within the **Quality Management System (QMS)** that ensures that a product, process, or service conforms to specified **quality requirements**, standards, and customer expectations. It is a **proactive, process-oriented** discipline focused on preventing defects *before* they occur, rather than detecting them after.

According to **ISO 9000:2015**, Quality Assurance is formally defined as:

> *"Part of quality management focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled."*

The four pivotal pillars that define QA in the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework are:

| Pillar | Meaning | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Planned Activities** | Documented procedures, SOPs | Prevention |
| **Process-Centric** | Built into the workflow | Design |
| **Systematic** | Follows standards (ISO, CMMI) | Compliance |
| **Confidence-Building** | Reassures stakeholders | Trust |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen Inspection**
>
> Imagine you own a restaurant. Instead of tasting every dish *after* it is cooked (which is **Quality Control** — inspection *after* the fact), you instead:
> - Hire trained chefs (**competence**),
> - Standardize every recipe (**documented process**),
> - Calibrate ovens every morning (**process control**),
> - Check ingredient freshness *before* cooking (**incoming verification**),
> - Train the waiters properly (**skill development**).
>
> This **entire system** is **Quality Assurance**. You trust that *good processes* will *consistently* produce *good food* — even if you never inspect a single plate!

That is the essence of QA — **building quality into the process**, not inspecting it into the product.

## 1.3 Quality Assurance vs. Quality Control vs. Quality Management

This distinction is **the single most-examined concept** in KTU exams.

| Parameter | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Quality Control (QC)** | **Quality Management (QM)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process | Product | Entire System |
| **Approach** | Proactive (Prevention) | Reactive (Detection) | Strategic (Both) |
| **Timing** | Before & during production | After production | Throughout lifecycle |
| **Tool** | Audits, process reviews | Inspection, testing | QA + QC + Policy |
| **Goal** | Build quality in | Find defects | Customer satisfaction |
| **Responsibility** | Everyone in the org | QC team | Top Management |

## 1.4 Quality Assurance Principles (ISO 9000 / Deming Foundations)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The QA framework rests on **7 Principles of Quality Management** defined by ISO:

1. **Customer Focus** — Primary objective is meeting customer needs.
2. **Leadership** — Top management establishes unity of purpose.
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered workforce.
4. **Process Approach** — Activities understood as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** — Continuous, ongoing effort.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Data and analysis drive choices.
7. **Relationship Management** — Manage supplier and stakeholder relationships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quality Assurance Lifecycle (Structured Logic)

The QA lifecycle can be broken into **five sequential, iterative phases**:

- **Phase 1 — Plan (Quality Planning):**
  - Identify quality standards relevant to the product/service.
  - Define acceptance criteria (e.g., tolerances, defect thresholds).
  - Document the **Quality Plan** — a project-specific roadmap.

- **Phase 2 — Do (Process Definition & Documentation):**
  - Create **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.
  - Define work instructions, checklists, and process maps.
  - Assign responsibilities (RACI matrix).

- **Phase 3 — Implement (Process Execution):**
  - Train personnel in documented processes.
  - Calibrate equipment, verify raw materials.
  - Execute the production/service delivery following the SOP.

- **Phase 4 — Check (Audits & Reviews):**
  - Conduct **internal audits** (1st party), **supplier audits** (2nd party), **certification audits** (3rd party).
  - Measure process capability indices like **Cpk** and **Ppk**.

- **Phase 5 — Act (Corrective & Preventive Action — CAPA):**
  - Address non-conformities.
  - Implement **preventive actions** so defects never recur.
  - Feed lessons learned back into Phase 1 (closed-loop feedback).

## 2.2 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — The Engineering Metric

The **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is a financial framework used to quantify the monetary impact of quality-related activities. This is **the most numerical concept** in QA and frequently appears in KTU numerical problems.

### Cost of Quality Formula

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; C_{p} \;+\; C_{a} \;+\; C_{i} \;+\; C_{e}
$$

Where each component is defined below.

| Symbol | Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{p}$ | **Prevention Cost** | Money spent to *prevent* defects | Training, SOP design, quality planning |
| $C_{a}$ | **Appraisal Cost** | Money spent to *detect* defects | Inspection, testing, audits |
| $C_{i}$ | **Internal Failure Cost** | Cost of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection |
| $C_{e}$ | **External Failure Cost** | Cost of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty, recalls, legal liability |

### Optimal Quality Cost Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem:** As investment in **Prevention + Appraisal (P+A)** increases, **Failure costs (I+E)** decrease faster than the increase in P+A. There exists an **optimum point** where **Total Cost of Quality is minimized**.

$$
C_{oQ}^{*}(P) \;=\; \min_{P} \left[\, C_{p}(P) + C_{a}(P) + C_{i}(P) + C_{e}(P) \,\right]
$$

This is illustrated conceptually below:

| Prevention Spending | Internal Failure | External Failure | Total Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low | High | Very High | **Very High** |
| Medium | Medium | Low | Medium |
| High (Optimal) | Low | Very Low | **Lowest** |
| Excessive | Very Low | Very Low | Rising again |

## 2.3 Process Capability Indices — The Statistical Backbone

Process capability measures how well a process output meets specifications.

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

| Index | Meaning | Ideal Value (KTU reference) |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{p}$ | Process potential (centered) | $\geq 1.33$ |
| $C_{pk}$ | Process capability (actual, accounts for shift) | $\geq 1.33$ |
| $\sigma$ | Standard deviation of process | Smaller = better |

## 2.4 Six Sigma — The Modern QA Benchmark

$$
\text{Defects per Million Opportunities (DPMO)} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}
$$

Where $D$ = number of defects, $N$ = sample size, $O$ = opportunities per unit.

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32 |
| 4σ | 621 | 99.38 |
| **5σ** | **233** | **99.977** |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.9997** |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of QA

- **Manufacturing:** Prevents defective units from reaching customers, reducing warranty claims.
- **Software Engineering:** Defect prevention in SDLC (e.g., code reviews, unit testing, CI/CD pipelines).
- **Healthcare:** Standardized procedures (SOPs) reduce surgical errors.
- **Service Sector:** Airlines, banks, and IT services use QA frameworks (ISO 9001, CMMI) for consistent service delivery.
- **Construction:** Ensures structural safety via material testing and process audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical: Cost of Quality Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality costs:
> - Prevention Cost ($C_{p}$) = ₹ 2,00,000
> - Appraisal Cost ($C_{a}$) = ₹ 3,00,000
> - Internal Failure Cost ($C_{i}$) = ₹ 4,00,000
> - External Failure Cost ($C_{e}$) = ₹ 6,00,000
>
> **Required:**
> 1. Compute the Total Cost of Quality (CoQ).
> 2. The firm plans to increase prevention spending by 50%, which is expected to reduce internal failure cost by 40% and external failure cost by 60%. Appraisal cost remains unchanged. Compute the **new** CoQ and comment.

### Solution — Step 1: Compute Initial CoQ

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; C_{p} + C_{a} + C_{i} + C_{e}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; \boxed{15{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}}
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Compute Revised CoQ After Investment

New Prevention Cost:

$$
C_{p}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.50) \;=\; 3{,}00{,}000
$$

New Internal Failure Cost (40% reduction):

$$
C_{i}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.40) \;=\; 2{,}40{,}000
$$

New External Failure Cost (60% reduction):

$$
C_{e}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 6{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.60) \;=\; 2{,}40{,}000
$$

Appraisal Cost unchanged:

$$
C_{a}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3{,}00{,}000
$$

Revised Total CoQ:

$$
C_{oQ}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 3{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
C_{oQ}^{\text{new}} \;=\; \boxed{10{,}80{,}000 \text{ INR}}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Savings Analysis

$$
\Delta C_{oQ} \;=\; 15{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}80{,}000 \;=\; 4{,}20{,}000 \text{ INR saved}
$$

$$
\% \text{Savings} \;=\; \dfrac{4{,}20{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \;=\; 28\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** A **50% increase in prevention spending** yielded a **28% net reduction** in total CoQ. This empirically validates the **KTU theorem** that *investment in prevention reduces failure costs more than proportionally*, achieving optimal QA economics.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — QA Tools and Their Application

| Tool / Technique | Type | Used In QA Phase | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pareto Chart** | Statistical | Check | Prioritize vital few defects (80/20 rule) |
| **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** | Cause-Effect | Check / Act | Identify root causes of defects |
| **Control Charts (SPC)** | Statistical | Check | Monitor process stability over time |
| **Flowcharts** | Process | Plan / Do | Document processes for clarity |
| **Check Sheets** | Data Collection | Check | Record frequency of defects |
| **Histogram** | Statistical | Check | Visualize variation distribution |
| **Scatter Diagram** | Statistical | Check | Study correlation between variables |
| **FMEA** | Risk | Plan | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis |

## 3.3 Quality Assurance Documentation Hierarchy

The QA documentation structure follows a strict top-down hierarchy:

$$
\text{Quality Manual} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Procedures} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Work Instructions} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Records}
$$

| Level | Document | Audience |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Top) | **Quality Manual** | Top Management, Auditors |
| 2 | **Procedures (SOPs)** | Department Heads |
| 3 | **Work Instructions** | Operators / Engineers |
| 4 (Base) | **Records / Forms** | Operational Evidence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Quality Assurance Pyramid (Block Architecture Flow)

The following block diagram illustrates how QA elements are stacked and interrelate within an organization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Top Management Commitment] --> A2[Quality Policy and Objectives]
    A2 --> A3[Quality Manual and ISO 9001 Framework]
    A3 --> A4[Documented SOPs and Procedures]
    A4 --> A5[Work Instructions and Process Maps]
    A5 --> A6[Operational Records and Data Logs]
    A6 --> A7[Internal Audits and Reviews]
    A7 --> A8[CAPA Corrective and Preventive Actions]
    A8 --> A2
```

## 4.2 PDCA Cycle Mapped to QA

The Deming **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle is the engine that drives QA. The diagram below maps it to QA-specific activities.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan Quality Objectives] --> D1[Do Execute SOPs and Train Staff]
    D1 --> C1[Check Audit and Measure Outputs]
    C1 --> A1[Act CAPA and Standardize Improvements]
    A1 --> P1
```

## 4.3 QA vs QC vs QM — Functional Distinction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QM[Quality Management Umbrella] --> QA[Quality Assurance Process Focus]
    QM --> QC[Quality Control Product Focus]
    QA --> QA1[Prevention Oriented]
    QA --> QA2[Audits and SOPs]
    QA --> QA3[Process Capability Analysis]
    QC --> QC1[Detection Oriented]
    QC --> QC2[Inspection and Testing]
    QC --> QC3[Sampling and Acceptance Plans]
```

## 4.4 Cost of Quality Behavior Curve

The following block diagram represents the behavior of CoQ components as prevention spending increases. Arrows indicate the directional trend.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Prevention Cost Rising Slowly] --> TOTAL
    A[Appraisal Cost Stable] --> TOTAL
    I[Internal Failure Cost Falling Sharply] --> TOTAL
    E[External Failure Cost Falling Sharply] --> TOTAL
    TOTAL[Total Cost of Quality Reaches Minimum then Rises]
```

## 4.5 QA Implementation Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Define Quality Policy] --> S2[Step 2 Identify Customer Requirements]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Set Quality Standards and Metrics]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Document Processes and SOPs]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Train Employees]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Implement Process Controls SPC]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Conduct Internal Audits]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Analyze Data and Identify Improvements]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Apply CAPA Corrective and Preventive Action]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Management Review and Continuous Improvement]
    S10 --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Quality Assurance. How is it different from Quality Control?**

**Model Answer:**

**Quality Assurance (QA):** QA is a systematic, planned set of activities that ensures that products, services, or processes conform to established quality standards. It is **process-oriented** and **prevention-driven**, aiming to build quality into the system itself rather than inspecting defects after they occur. As per ISO 9000:2015, QA is the part of quality management focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled.

**Difference from Quality Control (QC):**

| Aspect | QA | QC |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Process | Product |
| Approach | Prevention | Detection |
| Timing | Before & during production | After production |
| Responsibility | Entire organization | QC team |

QA builds confidence in the **process**, while QC verifies the **product**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List and briefly explain any three tools used in Quality Assurance.**

**Model Answer:**

The three widely-used QA tools are:

1. **Pareto Chart:** A bar chart combined with a line graph that prioritizes defects based on frequency. Based on the **80/20 rule** (Pareto Principle), it helps focus efforts on the *vital few* causes responsible for the majority of defects.

2. **Fishbone Diagram (Ishikawa):** A cause-and-effect diagram used to identify the **root causes** of a quality problem. Categories typically include Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, and Environment (the **6M** framework).

3. **Control Charts (SPC):** A statistical tool that plots process data over time against upper and lower control limits (UCL/LCL) to monitor whether a process is in a state of **statistical control**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the difference table** between QA and QC — losing up to **2 marks**.
> - In numerical CoQ problems, students often **forget to compute the savings percentage** — losing **1 mark**.
> - Students confuse **Cpk with Cp** in process capability questions — examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for this.
> - Always **label diagrams** (PDCA, QA pyramid) — unlabelled diagrams lose **up to 2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> - Use **bold keywords** (prevention, process, ISO 9001) — examiners scan for these terms.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a)** Define Quality Assurance. Explain the various **principles of Quality Assurance** as outlined in ISO 9000. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A company spends ₹ 5,00,000 on prevention, ₹ 4,00,000 on appraisal, ₹ 3,00,000 on internal failure, and ₹ 8,00,000 on external failure. If prevention spending is doubled, internal failure drops by 50% and external failure drops by 70%. Compute the new Cost of Quality and percentage savings. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q (a)

**Definition:**
QA is a part of quality management focused on **providing confidence** that quality requirements will be fulfilled. It is a planned, systematic, process-oriented set of activities that prevents defects and ensures consistent conformance to standards.

**ISO 9000 Principles of QA:**

1. **Customer Focus:** Understanding and meeting customer needs is the primary goal. Organizations must research, capture, and act on customer requirements.

2. **Leadership:** Leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and an environment in which people can engage in achieving quality objectives.

3. **Engagement of People:** Competent, empowered, and motivated employees at all levels are essential to enhance the organization's capability to create value.

4. **Process Approach:** Desired results are achieved more efficiently when activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes.

5. **Improvement:** Successful organizations maintain a continual focus on improvement — this is the **PDCA** cycle in action.

6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making:** Effective decisions are based on the analysis of data, facts, and evidence rather than intuition alone.

7. **Relationship Management:** For sustained success, organizations must manage relationships with **interested parties**, particularly suppliers and partners.

#### Model Solution — Q (b)

**Step 1 — Initial CoQ:**

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 \;=\; 20{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Revised values after doubled prevention:**

- $C_{p}^{\text{new}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = 10{,}00{,}000$
- $C_{a}^{\text{new}} = 4{,}00{,}000$ (unchanged)
- $C_{i}^{\text{new}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 1{,}50{,}000$
- $C_{e}^{\text{new}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 2{,}40{,}000$

**Step 3 — New CoQ:**

$$
C_{oQ}^{\text{new}} \;=\; 10{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 \;=\; 17{,}90{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Savings:**

$$
\Delta C_{oQ} \;=\; 20{,}00{,}000 - 17{,}90{,}000 \;=\; 2{,}10{,}000
$$

$$
\% \text{Savings} \;=\; \dfrac{2{,}10{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \;=\; 10.5\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Despite prevention spending doubling, the net CoQ reduced by 10.5%, demonstrating that **prevention is more cost-effective than failure correction**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **PDCA (Deming) Cycle** in detail and its role in Quality Assurance. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** Explain the concept of **Cost of Quality**. Discuss its four components with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q (a)

**PDCA Cycle (Deming Cycle / Shewhart Cycle):**

The PDCA cycle is a four-stage iterative management method used for the **continuous improvement** of processes and products. It is the **operational engine of QA**.

**Plan:**
- Identify the problem or opportunity.
- Analyze the current process using data.
- Define objectives, acceptance criteria, and the quality plan.
- *QA Activity:* Define quality standards and SOPs.

**Do:**
- Execute the planned activities on a small/pilot scale.
- Implement the documented procedures and work instructions.
- Train staff, calibrate equipment.
- *QA Activity:* Carry out process execution per SOPs.

**Check:**
- Monitor and measure process outputs against the plan.
- Conduct audits, collect data, use control charts.
- Compare actual vs. expected performance.
- *QA Activity:* Audit and verify conformance.

**Act:**
- Standardize successful changes.
- Implement Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA).
- Feed improvements back into the next cycle.
- *QA Activity:* Ensure continuous improvement.

**Role in QA:** PDCA is the **foundation** of any effective Quality Assurance system. It transforms QA from a static checklist into a **dynamic, self-improving loop**, ensuring that quality is not a one-time achievement but a sustained organizational capability.

#### Model Solution — Q (b)

**Cost of Quality (CoQ):** A financial model that quantifies the **total cost** of achieving (or failing to achieve) product/service quality. It helps management decide **how much to invest in prevention versus correction**.

**Four Components:**

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_{p}$):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring.
   - *Examples:* Quality training, SOP preparation, supplier capability assessment, quality planning.

2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_{a}$):** Costs incurred to *detect* defects during production.
   - *Examples:* Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QC, calibration of test equipment.

3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{i}$):** Costs of defects discovered *before* delivery to the customer.
   - *Examples:* Rework, scrap, re-inspection, failure analysis.

4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{e}$):** Costs of defects discovered *after* delivery.
   - *Examples:* Warranty claims, product recalls, legal liability, customer complaints, loss of reputation.

**Formula:**

$$
C_{oQ} \;=\; C_{p} + C_{a} + C_{i} + C_{e}
$$

**Insight:** Increasing $C_{p}$ and $C_{a}$ leads to a *proportionately larger* reduction in $C_{i}$ and $C_{e}$, yielding a **lower total CoQ** at the optimum point — this is the **strategic foundation** for QA investment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for Quality Assurance:**

- **QA is process-oriented; QC is product-oriented.** (Most-tested distinction.)
- **ISO 9000:2015** defines QA as *"part of QM focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled."*
- **QA = Prevention; QC = Detection.**
- The **7 ISO Principles** are: Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decisions, Relationship Management.
- **PDCA Cycle** = Plan → Do → Check → Act (Deming Cycle).
- **Cost of Quality = $C_{p} + C_{a} + C_{i} + C_{e}$**.
- **Optimal QA investment** minimizes total CoQ by increasing prevention.
- **Process Capability Indices:** $C_{p}$ (potential), $C_{pk}$ (actual); both ideally $\geq 1.33$.
- **Six Sigma** targets **3.4 DPMO** at the 6σ level.
- **QA Tools (7 QC Tools):** Pareto, Fishbone, Control Chart, Flowchart, Check Sheet, Histogram, Scatter Diagram.
- **Documentation Hierarchy:** Quality Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.
- **FMEA** = Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (a core QA risk tool).
- **Three types of audits:** 1st Party (internal), 2nd Party (supplier), 3rd Party (certification body).
- **CAPA** = Corrective and Preventive Action — the closing loop of QA.
- **Juran's Trilogy:** Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- **Deming's 14 Points** emphasize system thinking, continuous improvement, and elimination of fear for QA culture.
- **KTU Numerical Trick:** Always show the *initial CoQ*, the *revised CoQ*, and the **percentage savings** — all three are required for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality Management # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Quality — The Foundational Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Aligned with ISO 9000:2015)** > **Quality** is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements. An **object** can be a product, service, process, person, resource, system, or any combination thereof. In simpler academic terms, quality is about **conformance to requirements** (Crosby's view) and **fitness for use** (Juran's view). It is not luxury or high cost — it is about **consistently meeting customer expectations** in a reliable, predictable manner. > [!NOTE] > **Three Universal Perspectives of Quality (KTU High-Yield)** > 1. **User's View** — Fitness for intended use (Juran) > 2. **Manufacturer's View** — Conformance to design specifications (Crosby, Levitt) > 3. **Product-Based View** — Quality is a precise and measurable variable (Taguchi's Quality Loss Function) ## 1.2 Quality Management — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Management (QM)** is a formalized system of documenting processes, procedures, responsibilities, and continuous improvement activities aimed at consistently meeting customer and regulatory requirements and enhancing stakeholder satisfaction. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Quality Management** is treated as the *parent discipline* that encompasses Quality Control (QC), Quality Assurance (QA), and the broader Total Quality Management (TQM) philosophy. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: A Five-Star Restaurant Chain** > Imagine a restaurant chain like "Burger King" operating in 50 cities. To call it a *quality-managed* brand, every customer — irrespective of city — must receive: > - The **same taste** (Standardization) > - The **same hygiene** (Compliance) > - The **same portion size** (Specification Conformance) > - **Courteous service** (Customer Satisfaction) > - A **receipt** (Documentation/Traceability) > - **Continuous menu improvement** (Kaizen / Continuous Improvement) > > If one outlet serves stale food, the entire brand reputation suffers. Therefore, **Quality Management is the operating manual of the entire chain**, not just a single outlet. This is the essence of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. ## 1.4 Why Quality Management Matters in Engineering In KTU's B.Tech context, every engineering discipline (Mechanical, Civil, Electrical, Electronics, Computer Science) must deliver products, software, or services that: - **Conform to IS/ISO/ASTM standards** - **Satisfy the customer** (internal or external) - **Minimize rework, defects, and warranty costs** - **Build long-term organizational reputation** ## 1.5 Core Terminology (KTU-Exam Friendly Glossary) | Term | Meaning (KTU 2024 Standard) | |---|---| | **Quality** | Degree to which inherent characteristics fulfil requirements | | **Quality Control (QC)** | Operational techniques to fulfil quality requirements (inspection-driven) | | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | Planned systematic activities to provide confidence that requirements are met (process-driven) | | **Quality Management System (QMS)** | Formalized system documenting processes, responsibilities, and KPIs | | **TQM** | A management philosophy integrating all functions to focus on customer satisfaction | | **Kaizen** | Japanese term for continuous incremental improvement | | **Six Sigma** | Data-driven methodology aiming for $\leq 3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO) | | **BPM** | Business Process Management — broader process optimization lens | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet ## 2.1 Evolution of Quality Management (Historical Timeline) Quality Management is **not new** — it evolved across three industrial eras: > [!NOTE] > **Three Eras of Quality (KTU Board Hot Question)** > 1. **Inspection Era (Pre-1920s)** — Focus on detecting defects after production. *Quality = Inspection*. > 2. **Quality Control Era (1920s–1950s)** — Statistical methods introduced by Shewhart. *Quality = Statistical Control*. > 3. **Quality Management Era (1950s onwards)** — Total involvement, customer focus, continuous improvement. *Quality = Strategic Weapon*. ## 2.2 The Quality Gurus — Their Distinct Contributions (KTU Most Repeated Topic) This is the **single highest-weightage topic** in KTU Quality Management papers. Each guru has a unique philosophy. Memorize the **keywords** associated with each. | Guru | Year / Region | Key Philosophy | Famous Phrase / Concept | |---|---|---|---| | **Walter A. Shewhart** | 1924, USA | Father of Statistical Quality Control | **PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** | | **W. Edwards Deming** | 1950, USA/Japan | Management responsibility for quality | **14 Points for Management**, System thinking | | **Joseph M. Juran** | 1954, USA/Japan | Quality is fitness for use | **Quality Trilogy** (Planning, Control, Improvement) | | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1956, USA | Total Quality Control | **TQC = Company-wide quality** | | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1962, Japan | Quality circles, customer focus | **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram** | | **Genichi Taguchi** | 1980, Japan | Quality in design, loss to society | **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** | | **Philip B. Crosby** | 1979, USA | Quality is free, prevention not inspection | **"Zero Defects"**, **"Do It Right First Time" (DIRFT)** | | **Joseph M. Duran** *(separate from Juran)* | — | — | — | | **M. Walton (Mary Walton)** | 1986 | Deming Prize implications | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deming's 14 Points — KTU Frequently Asked Sub-Question** > Point 1: *Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service.* > Point 2: *Adopt the new philosophy.* > Point 3: *Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.* > Point 4: *End the practice of awarding business on price alone.* > Point 5: *Improve constantly every process for planning, production, and service.* > Point 7: *Institute leadership.* > Point 11: *Eliminate numerical quotas.* > Point 13: *Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.* ## 2.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy (KTU 14-Mark Favourite) > [!NOTE] > Juran's Trilogy is a **three-part managerial framework**: > 1. **Quality Planning** — Identify customers and their needs; develop product/process to meet those needs. > 2. **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs. goals; act on differences. > 3. **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure to continuously improve processes. ## 2.4 Deming's PDCA Cycle (Shewhart Cycle) This is the **operational backbone** of every QMS in the world (including ISO 9001). - **P — Plan**: Identify the problem, analyse, develop a plan. - **D — Do**: Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot). - **C — Check**: Observe and measure results, compare with expected. - **A — Act**: Standardize the improvement or restart the cycle. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet Since Quality Management is a management course, the "formulas" are primarily **quantitative quality metrics** that KTU frequently tests. | Metric | Formula | Standard / Benchmark | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | **Defect Rate** | $\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | Lower is better | Manufacturing QC | | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units Passing Inspection First Time}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}} \times 100$ | Target $\geq 95\%$ | Process capability check | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \text{FPY}_1 \times \text{FPY}_2 \times \ldots \times \text{FPY}_n$ | For multi-step processes | Software/assembly lines | | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Opportunities per Unit} \times \text{Units}} \times 10^{6}$ | Six Sigma target $\leq 3.4$ | Six Sigma | | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\geq 1.33$ capable, $\geq 1.67$ excellent | Process engineering | | **Taguchi's Quality Loss (QLF)** | $L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2}$ | Quadratic loss to society | Design optimization | | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Minimize internal + external | QA economics | | **Pareto 80/20** | $80\%$ of defects come from $20\%$ of causes | Vital few vs. trivial many | Problem-solving | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Isolation Rule Applied:** Vertical bars $\vert$ used inside table cells (instead of pipes) to avoid breaking the markdown table. ## 2.6 Dimensions of Product & Service Quality > [!NOTE] > **David Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Quality (KTU Standard Reference)** > 1. Performance > 2. Features > 3. Reliability > 4. Conformance > 5. Durability > 6. Serviceability > 7. Aesthetics > 8. Perceived Quality For **services**, the SERVQUAL model adds: - **Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy** (5 dimensions). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF) Taguchi reframed quality as **loss imparted to society** from the moment a product deviates from its target. Unlike the traditional "inside-or-outside spec" view, Taguchi proposed a **continuous quadratic loss**. ### Step 1: Define the Target Value Let $T$ be the target value of a quality characteristic (e.g., diameter $= 10$ mm, voltage $= 5$ V). ### Step 2: Define the Actual Value Let $x$ be the actual measured value of the characteristic. ### Step 3: Define the Deviation The deviation from target is: $$ \Delta = x - T $$ ### Step 4: Define the Loss Proportionality Constant Let $k$ be the proportionality constant (engineering cost of being off-target). It is calculated as: $$ k = \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}} $$ where $A_0$ is the cost of replacement/repair at the functional limit $\Delta_0$ (the maximum tolerable deviation before the product becomes non-functional). ### Step 5: Formulate the Loss Function $$ L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2} $$ $$ L(x) = \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}} \cdot (x - T)^{2} $$ ### Step 6: Interpret the Result - If $x = T$, then $L(x) = 0$ (no loss). - If $x \neq T$, even within specification limits, $L(x) > 0$ (loss exists). - Loss grows **quadratically** with deviation. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation (Why this matters)** > A $9.9$ mm bolt and a $10.1$ mm bolt both pass a $\pm 0.2$ mm spec, but the one farther from $10$ mm is costlier to society. This pushed engineers to design for **on-target**, not just "in-spec". This is foundational to **Robust Design**. ### Worked Example (KTU-Style) A resistor's target resistance is $R_T = 100\ \Omega$. Functional limit is $\pm 20\ \Omega$ where the cost of replacement is $A_0 = \text{Rs. }400$. Find loss when $R = 102\ \Omega$. $$ k = \dfrac{400}{(20)^{2}} = \dfrac{400}{400} = 1\ \text{Rs.}/\Omega^{2} $$ $$ L(102) = 1 \cdot (102 - 100)^{2} = 4\ \text{Rs.} $$ Even a $2\ \Omega$ deviation (within spec) costs the organization Rs. 4 per unit in societal loss. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Structure COPQ is the **monetary quantification of failure**. It is split into: | Category | Type | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Prevention Costs** | Proactive | Training, quality planning, supplier capability audits | | **Appraisal Costs** | Detective | Inspection, testing, calibration | | **Internal Failure Costs** | Reactive (in-house) | Rework, scrap, re-inspection | | **External Failure Costs** | Reactive (customer-side) | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strategic Insight (KTU Board Expectation)** > A *mature* quality organization spends more on **Prevention** and **Appraisal** (cheap early costs) and minimizes **Internal + External Failure** (expensive late costs). A *reactive* organization does the opposite and bleeds money. ## 3.3 Comparative Matrix: Quality Gurus' Philosophical Anchors This matrix is the **single most exam-ready artefact** for KTU Part B answers on Module 1. | Dimension | Deming | Juran | Crosby | Ishikawa | Taguchi | Feigenbaum | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Origin / Era** | 1950, USA | 1954, USA | 1979, USA | 1962, Japan | 1980, Japan | 1956, USA | | **Core Idea** | System of profound knowledge | Quality trilogy | Conformance + prevention | Quality circles | Quality loss | Total quality control | | **View of Quality** | Predictable variation | Fitness for use | Conformance to requirements | Customer-defined | Loss to society | Company-wide | | **Top Tool** | PDCA, 14 Points | Pareto, Quality Trilogy | Zero Defects, DIRFT | Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram | QLF, Robust Design | TQC framework | | **Customer Focus** | Indirect (via system) | Direct (needs identification) | Indirect (prevention) | Strong (next process = customer) | Indirect (loss) | Strong | | **Best Quote** | "Quality is made in the boardroom" | "Fitness for use" | "Quality is free" | "Quality begins and ends with education" | "Loss to society" | "Quality is everybody's job" | | **Japanese Award** | Deming Prize | — | — | — | Deming Prize (also) | — | > [!NOTE] > **Deming Prize (Japan)** — Established in 1951, awarded to companies excelling in quality. Both Deming and Taguchi are deeply linked to it. The prize **predates the Japan Quality Medal**. ## 3.4 Analytical Process: Pareto-Based Problem Solving (80/20) Used when problems are many, but a few dominate. 1. **Define** the problem category (e.g., "welding defects"). 2. **Collect** data on defect sub-types for a defined period. 3. **Tabulate** defect counts in descending order. 4. **Compute** the cumulative percentage: $$ \text{Cumulative \%} = \dfrac{\text{Cumulative count}}{\text{Total count}} \times 100 $$ 5. **Plot** bar chart + cumulative curve (Pareto Chart). 6. **Focus** corrective action on the "vital few" (typically up to $80\%$ cumulative). --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution of Quality Management (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inspection Era
Pre-1920s] --> B[Quality Control Era
1920s to 1950s] B --> C[Quality Assurance Era
1950s to 1980s] C --> D[Total Quality Management
1980s to Present] D --> E[Six Sigma Lean Six Sigma
1990s to Present] A1[Detect defects after
they occur] -.-> A B1[Statistical process
control SPC] -.-> B C1[System based prevention
ISO 9000 BS 5750] -.-> C D1[Customer focus continuous
improvement all functions] -.-> D E1[Data driven DMAIC
3.4 DPMO] -.-> E ``` ## 4.2 Deming's PDCA Cycle — Operational View ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Plan
Identify problem and
develop hypotheses] D[Do
Implement plan on
small scale pilot] C[Check
Measure results and
compare with targets] A[Act
Standardize improvement
or revise plan] P --> D D --> C C --> A A --> P S1[Continuous
Improvement Loop] -.-> P S2[If goal met
standardize] -.-> A S3[If not met
revise hypothesis] -.-> A ``` ## 4.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STAGE1[Quality Planning] SP1[Identify customers] SP2[Determine customer needs] SP3[Develop product features] SP4[Develop process capabilities] SP1 --> SP2 --> SP3 --> SP4 end subgraph STAGE2[Quality Control] SC1[Choose control subjects] SC2[Choose measurement units] SC3[Establish measurement] SC4[Set standards and goals] SC5[Measure actual performance] SC6[Interpret differences] SC7[Act on differences] SC1 --> SC2 --> SC3 --> SC4 --> SC5 --> SC6 --> SC7 end subgraph STAGE3[Quality Improvement] SI1[Establish infrastructure] SI2[Identify improvement projects] SI3[Establish project teams] SI4[Provide resources and motivation] SI1 --> SI2 --> SI3 --> SI4 end STAGE1 --> STAGE2 STAGE2 --> STAGE3 STAGE3 -.->|Feedback| STAGE1 ``` ## 4.4 TQM Building Blocks ```mermaid flowchart TD CORE[Customer Focus] CORE --> P1[Total Employee Involvement] CORE --> P2[Continuous Improvement Kaizen] CORE --> P3[Process Centric Approach] CORE --> P4[Integrated System] CORE --> P5[Strategic and Systematic Approach] CORE --> P6[Fact Based Decision Making] CORE --> P7[Communications] P1 --> OUT[Enhanced Customer
Satisfaction and
Organizational Excellence] P2 --> OUT P3 --> OUT P4 --> OUT P5 --> OUT P6 --> OUT P7 --> OUT ``` ## 4.5 Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) — P-A-I-E Model ```mermaid flowchart LR COPQ[Cost of Poor Quality] COPQ --> PREV[Prevention Costs
Training, Planning, Audits] COPQ --> APPR[Appraisal Costs
Inspection, Testing] COPQ --> INT[Internal Failure
Rework, Scrap] COPQ --> EXT[External Failure
Warranty, Returns, Lawsuits] PREV --> SUM[Total COPQ] APPR --> SUM INT --> SUM EXT --> SUM SUM --> STRAT[Strategic Goal
Shift spend from right side
to left side of COPQ] ``` ## 4.6 Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa / Fishbone) Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR HEAD[Problem Statement
e.g. Defective Welds] --> M M[Main Spine] --> M1[Manpower] M --> M2[Machine] M --> M3[Method] M --> M4[Material] M --> M5[Measurement] M --> M6[Environment] M1 --> C11[Skill gap] M1 --> C12[Fatigue] M2 --> C21[Calibration] M2 --> C22[Wear and tear] M3 --> C31[Procedure gap] M3 --> C32[Wrong settings] M4 --> C41[Raw material defect] M4 --> C42[Wrong grade] M5 --> C51[Gauge error] M5 --> C52[Sampling error] M6 --> C61[Humidity] M6 --> C62[Temperature] ``` ## 4.7 DMAIC Framework (Six Sigma) — KTU Advanced View ```mermaid flowchart TD D[Define
Problem, customer, scope] M[Measure
Current performance, CTQ] A[Analyze
Root cause analysis] I[Improve
Pilot and implement] C[Control
Standardize and monitor] D --> M --> A --> I --> C C -.->|Loop back if drift| D C1[Goal 3.4 DPMO] -.-> C ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Model Note to Students:** KTU Part A expects **definition + 2–3 key points + one-line industrial example**. No essays. Stick to crisp facts. ### Q1. Define Quality. Discuss the various dimensions of product quality. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Pattern]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Quality is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements (ISO 9000:2015). - **Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Product Quality:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, and Perceived Quality. - **Example:** A smartphone is judged by all 8 dimensions — speed, camera, battery life, defect-freedom, lifespan, service network, look, and brand trust. - *Concluding line:* A truly high-quality product must satisfy all 8 dimensions in balance. --- ### Q2. Explain the term "Quality Assurance" and differentiate it from "Quality Control". **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Quality Assurance (QA):** A planned and systematic set of activities implemented within the quality system to provide confidence that the product or service will satisfy quality requirements. QA is *process-oriented* and *preventive*. **Quality Control (QC):** Operational techniques and activities used to fulfil quality requirements, primarily through *inspection* of the product. QC is *product-oriented* and *detective*. | Aspect | QA | QC | |---|---|---| | Focus | Process | Product | | Nature | Preventive | Detective | | Tool | Audits, process control | Inspection, testing | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two question sets. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1: Quality Gurus** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain Deming's 14 Points for Management. Discuss its relevance in modern organizations. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Compare and contrast the contributions of Juran, Crosby, and Feigenbaum to Quality Management. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q(a) — Deming's 14 Points (7 marks) Deming's 14 Points are a foundational framework for management transformation. The key points (presented concisely as a model answer) are: 1. **Create constancy of purpose** for improvement of product and service, allocating resources for long-term planning. 2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — accept that the current system must change. 3. **Cease dependence on inspection** — build quality into the process from the start. 4. **End the practice of awarding business on price alone** — minimize total cost by working with a single supplier. 5. **Improve constantly** every process for planning, production, and service. 6. **Institute training on the job.** 7. **Institute leadership** — supervisors should help people do a better job. 8. **Drive out fear** so everyone can work effectively. 9. **Break down barriers between departments** — team-based problem solving. 10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** that demand zero defects without method. 11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** — replace with leadership. 12. **Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.** 13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.** 14. **Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.** **Modern Relevance (1 mark):** Deming's points are reflected in **ISO 9001**, **TQM**, and **Agile/Lean** management systems. His emphasis on *system thinking*, *prevention*, and *continuous improvement* is the bedrock of Industry 4.0 and digital transformation strategies. #### Model Solution for Q(b) — Juran vs Crosby vs Feigenbaum (7 marks) A structured comparison should be written as a 3-row table (1.5 marks per guru + 1 mark for synthesis): | Aspect | Juran | Crosby | Feigenbaum | |---|---|---|---| | Core idea | Fitness for use; Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) | Quality is free; Conformance to requirements; DIRFT (Do It Right First Time) | Total Quality Control (TQC) — quality is everyone's job | | Origin | 1954 (USA/Japan) | 1979 (USA) | 1956 (USA) | | Major contribution | Quality Trilogy; Customer-needs identification; Pareto principle for quality | Zero Defects concept; Prevention-vs-Inspection cost argument | Company-wide TQC system involving all departments | | Signature phrase | "Fitness for use" | "Quality is free" | "Quality is everybody's job" | | Application | Suitable for strategic planning, project selection | Strong for manufacturing discipline, defect-prevention culture | Strong for large, multi-departmental organizations | | Limitation | Trilogy seen as abstract without operational tools | Overly audit/inspection-oriented; less customer-centric | Implementation across departments can be complex | **Synthesis (closing line):** While Juran gives the *strategic* lens, Crosby gives the *cultural* lens, and Feigenbaum gives the *organizational* lens. Together they form a holistic foundation of modern QMS. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2: TQM & PDCA** > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain the concept of Total Quality Management (TQM). Discuss its key principles and benefits. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain Deming's PDCA cycle. How is it applied in a manufacturing process? Give an example. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q(a) — TQM (7 marks) **Definition (1.5 marks):** Total Quality Management is a customer-focused management philosophy that involves *all members* of an organization in continuously improving all aspects of the business to deliver high-quality products and services that exceed customer expectations. **Key Principles (4 marks):** 1. **Customer Focus** — The customer (internal or external) defines quality. 2. **Total Employee Involvement** — Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor, is responsible for quality. 3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Small, incremental, perpetual improvements. 4. **Process-Centric Approach** — Focus on processes, not just outputs. 5. **Integrated System** — All departments are interconnected; quality is not a siloed function. 6. **Strategic and Systematic Approach** — Quality is a strategic pillar, integrated with the organizational vision. 7. **Fact-Based Decision Making** — Use SPC, Pareto, statistical tools. 8. **Communication** — Effective and timely communication across all levels. **Benefits (1.5 marks):** - Enhanced customer satisfaction and loyalty - Reduced rework, scrap, warranty, and warranty costs - Higher employee morale and ownership - Improved market share and competitive advantage - Stronger supplier relationships #### Model Solution for Q(b) — PDCA Cycle (7 marks) **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a circular flow of Plan → Do → Check → Act with arrows and brief descriptions (see Section 4.2 for the Mermaid reference). **Detailed Steps (3 marks):** - **Plan:** Define the problem (e.g., high rejection rate of a CNC shaft). Collect data, identify causes using Pareto/Fishbone. Develop a hypothesis and an action plan. - **Do:** Implement the plan on a small batch (pilot). Document observations. - **Check:** Compare actual results with expected. Use control charts and FPY. - **Act:** If the pilot succeeds, standardize across the line. If it fails, revise the plan and re-enter the cycle. **Example (1 mark):** A manufacturing firm faces a $12\%$ defect rate in capacitor soldering. The team hypothesizes that *soldering tip temperature* is the root cause. They set tip temperature to $370\degree C$ (Plan), run $50$ PCBs (Do), measure defect rate drops to $4\%$ (Check), update the SOP to $370\degree C$ and train all operators (Act). The cycle repeats for the next defect category. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU QP Code OEIET612** > 1. **Confusing Deming with Juran** — Deming = System + 14 Points + PDCA; Juran = Trilogy + Fitness for Use. Do NOT interchange. > 2. **Writing QA = QC** — They are NOT the same. QA is preventive (process), QC is detective (product). KTU examiners **specifically** test this distinction. > 3. **Skipping the diagram in PDCA / Fishbone answers** — A 7-mark question **always** expects a labelled diagram. A text-only answer will lose 2–3 marks. > 4. **Not remembering key years** — Deming (1950), Juran (1954), Ishikawa (1962), Taguchi (1980), Crosby (1979), Feigenbaum (1956). These dates are common **fill-in-the-blanks** in KTU. > 5. **Confusing Six Sigma's DPMO target** — It is **3.4 DPMO**, not zero. Many students write "0 defects" which is wrong. > 6. **Mixing up Garvin's 8 dimensions with SERVQUAL's 5** — Garvin = product; SERVQUAL = service. They are not interchangeable. > 7. **Writing Taguchi's QLF as linear** — It is **quadratic** $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$. This is a frequently tested formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Quality Management (Module 1)** > - [ ] Quality = Degree of conformance to requirements (ISO 9000:2015). > - [ ] Three views: User, Manufacturer, Product-based. > - [ ] QA is *process-driven, preventive*; QC is *product-driven, detective*. > - [ ] **Deming** → PDCA, 14 Points, System thinking. USA/Japan, 1950. > - [ ] **Juran** → Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement), Fitness for use. USA, 1954. > - [ ] **Crosby** → Zero Defects, DIRFT, "Quality is free", Prevention. USA, 1979. > - [ ] **Feigenbaum** → TQC, Company-wide quality. USA, 1956. > - [ ] **Ishikawa** → Quality Circles, Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram. Japan, 1962. > - [ ] **Taguchi** → Quality Loss Function $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$, Robust Design. Japan, 1980. > - [ ] **Shewhart** → Father of SQC; originated PDCA. USA, 1924. > - [ ] Three eras: Inspection → Quality Control → Quality Management. > - [ ] **Garvin's 8 dimensions** of product quality. > - [ ] **SERVQUAL's 5 dimensions** of service quality. > - [ ] **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. > - [ ] **Six Sigma target** = $3.4$ DPMO. > - [ ] **Pareto (80/20)** — Vital few vs. trivial many. > - [ ] **TQM Principles** — Customer focus, Total employee involvement, Continuous improvement, Process approach, Integrated system, Fact-based decisions, Communication, Strategic alignment. > - [ ] **Deming Prize** = Japanese award for quality excellence (1951). > - [ ] **Quality Gurus' Year-Side mapping is a recurrent 3-mark KTU question.** ---

topics.title: Total Quality Management (TQM) - Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management (TQM) - Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and business processes by involving all members of the organization — from top management to front-line workers — in a customer-centric, data-driven, and process-oriented approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEIET612):**
> TQM is a *systematic, integrated, and strategic* approach to managing an organization whose primary objective is to meet **customer expectations** by delivering products and services of the highest **quality**, while ensuring **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** and **total employee involvement (TEI)** across every functional level.

The word **"Total"** in TQM signifies that quality is *not* the responsibility of a single department (like Quality Assurance), but rather the responsibility of **every individual, every department, and every process** within the organization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Five-Star Restaurant Kitchen**
> 
> Imagine a premium restaurant where the Head Chef (Top Management) does not cook the food alone. Instead:
> - The **Chefs** (Production) cook with precision.
> - The **Servers** (Service) deliver with a smile.
> - The **Cleaners** (Housekeeping) maintain hygiene.
> - The **Sommelier** (Quality Inspector) tastes and approves.
> - The **Manager** (Operations) listens to customer feedback.
> 
> Everyone in the kitchen is **accountable for the taste, presentation, and hygiene** of the final dish. If one person fails, the entire customer experience collapses. TQM applies this exact same logic to an entire organization — **quality is a shared, collective, and continuous responsibility.**

### 1.3 Core Components of TQM

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Customer Focus** | Understanding and meeting customer needs and expectations. |
| **Total Employee Involvement** | Every employee, from CEO to operator, participates. |
| **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** | Ongoing, incremental improvements in processes. |
| **Process-Centered Approach** | Focus on improving processes rather than blaming people. |
| **Integrated System** | All departments are interconnected and aligned. |
| **Fact-Based Decision Making** | Decisions driven by data, statistics, and measurable metrics. |
| **Communication** | Effective communication at all levels, both vertical and horizontal. |
| **Supplier Partnership** | Building long-term, mutually beneficial relationships with suppliers. |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Term to Remember:** In TQM, the customer is *not just* the external buyer — it includes **internal customers** too. For example, the Accounts department is the "customer" of the Sales department for processing invoices.

### 1.4 Evolution of the TQM Concept

The TQM philosophy did not emerge overnight. It evolved through the contributions of multiple Quality Gurus over several decades:

- **1920s-1930s:** Walter Shewhart laid the foundation with Statistical Quality Control (SQC).
- **1950s:** W. Edwards Deming and Joseph Juran introduced quality concepts to post-war Japan.
- **1960s-1970s:** Kaoru Ishikawa popularized the concept of Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC) in Japan.
- **1980s:** The term *Total Quality Management* was formally adopted in the West (USA, UK).
- **1987:** ISO 9000 standards formalized quality system requirements globally.
- **2015-Present:** ISO 9001:2015 emphasizes risk-based thinking and process approach.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of TQM

TQM rests on three foundational pillars that must work in harmony:

1. **Pillar 1 — Customer Satisfaction (The "Why")**
   - TQM exists primarily to satisfy the customer. If the customer is not satisfied, the product/service has failed regardless of internal metrics.
   - Customer satisfaction is measured through surveys, Net Promoter Score (NPS), Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI), and feedback loops.

2. **Pillar 2 — Continuous Improvement (The "How")**
   - Quality is a journey, not a destination. The Japanese term **Kaizen** (改善) means "change for the better."
   - Improvement is achieved through the **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act), also known as the **Deming Wheel**.

3. **Pillar 3 — Total Employee Involvement (The "Who")**
   - Every employee, regardless of role, contributes to quality. Empowerment, training, and teamwork are essential.
   - Tools used include **Quality Circles**, **Cross-functional teams**, and **Suggestion systems**.

### 2.2 TQM vs. Traditional Quality Management

| Parameter | Traditional Approach | TQM Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition of Quality** | Conformance to specifications | Fitness for use / Customer satisfaction |
| **Responsibility for Quality** | Quality Control Department only | Every employee |
| **Customer Orientation** | Production-driven | Customer-driven |
| **Decision Making** | Intuition-based | Fact-based (Data-driven) |
| **Improvement Focus** | Reactive (fix after defect) | Proactive (prevent defects) |
| **Supplier Relationship** | Adversarial | Partnership-based |
| **Structure** | Hierarchical, departmental | Flat, cross-functional teams |
| **Training** | Minimal, job-specific | Continuous, holistic |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since TQM is largely conceptual, the "formulas" here represent **analytical metrics and decision frameworks** rather than physical equations.

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $CSI = \frac{\text{Sum of Satisfaction Scores}}{\text{Maximum Possible Score}} \times 100$ | Measures % customer satisfaction. |
| **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities} \times \text{Units Produced}} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities. |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}$ | Quantifies quality cost. |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \frac{\text{Units Passed First Time}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | Process capability indicator. |
| **Six Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \frac{USL - \mu}{3s}$ where $s$ = standard deviation | Statistical process control metric. |
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan $\rightarrow$ Do $\rightarrow$ Check $\rightarrow$ Act | Continuous improvement framework. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions:** All percentage-based metrics must lie between **0 and 100**. DPMO values are non-negative integers. Sigma levels are typically between **1σ** and **6σ**, with **6σ = 3.4 DPMO** being world-class.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In an engineering organization, TQM is applied as follows:

- **Manufacturing:** Reducing defect rates on assembly lines using SPC (Statistical Process Control).
- **Software Engineering:** Improving code quality through continuous integration and testing.
- **Civil Engineering:** Ensuring construction materials meet IS/ISO standards.
- **Service Industries:** Enhancing customer service through feedback loops and employee training.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** Always quote a real-world example in your answers (e.g., Toyota Production System, Tata Steel, ISO 9000-certified companies). Examiners reward contextual application.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since TQM is a conceptual management topic, the "derivation" here is a **logical conceptual derivation of the TQM framework** — showing how the philosophy emerges from first principles and quality guru contributions.

### 3.1 Conceptual Derivation: The TQM Framework

Let us derive the TQM framework step-by-step from a logical sequence:

**Step 1: Define the Problem**
In a traditional system, quality is treated as a *function*, not a *culture*. Defects are detected after production, leading to:
- High rework costs.
- Customer dissatisfaction.
- Wasted materials and time.

$$Q_{\text{traditional}} = f(\text{Inspection}, \text{Standards})$$

This is a **reactive** approach — quality is *verified*, not *built-in*.

**Step 2: Introduce the Process View**
If we accept that **quality must be built into the process** (Deming's insight), then:

$$Q_{\text{process}} = f(\text{Input}, \text{Process}, \text{Output}, \text{Feedback})$$

Here, quality depends on the entire system, not just the final inspection stage.

**Step 3: Add the Customer Dimension**
Quality is meaningless if the customer rejects the product. Therefore, we must add **fitness for use** (Juran's definition):

$$Q_{\text{customer}} = f(\text{Specifications}, \text{Actual Performance}, \text{Customer Expectation})$$

**Step 4: Add the People Dimension (Total Involvement)**
Since processes are run by people, Ishikawa emphasized that **all employees** must contribute:

$$Q_{\text{TQM}} = f(\text{Customer}, \text{Process}, \text{People}, \text{Continuous Improvement})$$

**Step 5: Add the Improvement Dimension (Kaizen)**
Quality is never "achieved" permanently. Using the PDCA cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \text{Plan: Identify the problem and propose a hypothesis.} \\
D_1 &= \text{Do: Implement the plan on a small scale.} \\
C_1 &= \text{Check: Measure the results against expectations.} \\
A_1 &= \text{Act: Standardize if successful, else repeat the cycle.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PDCA cycle is **iterative** — after Act, a new Plan begins, leading to continuous improvement.

**Step 6: Final TQM Equation**
Combining all dimensions:

$$TQM = \int_{t=0}^{\infty} \left[ C(t) + P(t) + E(t) + I(t) \right] dt$$

Where:
- $C(t)$ = Customer satisfaction function over time.
- $P(t)$ = Process optimization function.
- $E(t)$ = Employee engagement function.
- $I(t)$ = Continuous improvement (Kaizen) function.

The **integral** symbolizes that TQM is an *ongoing, never-ending* philosophy.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Quality Cost Breakdown

The Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) can be derived as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
COPQ &= C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $C_{\text{prevention}}$ = Cost of training, quality planning, process design.
- $C_{\text{appraisal}}$ = Cost of inspection, testing, audits.
- $C_{\text{internal failure}}$ = Cost of rework, scrap, re-inspection.
- $C_{\text{external failure}}$ = Cost of warranty claims, returns, reputation loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **prevention costs** with **appraisal costs**. Remember: Prevention = *avoiding* defects before they occur. Appraisal = *detecting* defects after they occur. Prevention is always cheaper in the long run.

### 3.3 The Deming 14 Points (Analytical Summary)

Although not numerically derived, the **14 Points** form the most-tested analytical framework in KTU exams:

1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TQM Conceptual Architecture Flow

