<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four of its key elements.*

**Model Answer:**
TQM is a customer-focused, process-centered, and data-driven management philosophy aimed at continuously improving the quality of products and services by involving all members of the organization.

Four key elements:
1. Customer Focus
2. Total Employee Involvement
3. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
4. Fact-Based Decision Making

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
*Distinguish between Traditional Quality Management and Total Quality Management in any four points.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Traditional QM | TQM |
|---|---|---|
| Responsibility | Quality Department only | Every employee |
| Orientation | Production-driven | Customer-driven |
| Improvement | Reactive (after defect) | Proactive (prevention) |
| Decision Making | Intuition-based | Data-driven |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the evolution of the Total Quality Management (TQM) concept. Discuss the contributions of any two Quality Gurus in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the key principles of TQM with suitable examples. Explain the PDCA cycle as a tool for continuous improvement. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Evolution of TQM:**

The concept of TQM evolved gradually through the contributions of several quality pioneers:

**Stage 1 — Inspection Era (Pre-1920s):** Quality was ensured by inspecting finished products. Defective items were rejected. This was a corrective approach.

**Stage 2 — Quality Control Era (1920s-1940s):** Walter Shewhart introduced Statistical Quality Control (SQC), using control charts to monitor process variation. This shifted focus from inspection to process control.

**Stage 3 — Quality Assurance Era (1950s-1970s):** Deming and Juran taught Japanese industries the importance of systemic quality. The concept of quality assurance across the entire system emerged.

**Stage 4 — TQM Era (1980s-Present):** The term TQM was formalized in the 1980s in the USA, with emphasis on total organizational involvement, customer focus, and continuous improvement.

**Contributions of Two Quality Gurus:**

**1. W. Edwards Deming (1900-1993):**
- Deming introduced the **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act), also called the Deming Wheel.
- He proposed **14 Points for Management** to transform organizational culture.
- Emphasized that **94% of quality problems** are attributable to the system, not workers.
- Introduced the concept of **statistical process control** and **common cause vs. special cause variation**.

**2. Joseph M. Juran (1904-2008):**
- Defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"** — a customer-oriented definition.
- Proposed the **Juran Trilogy**: Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement.
- Stressed the importance of **breakthrough projects** and **quality by design**.

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Key Principles of TQM with Examples:**

1. **Customer Focus:** Every process is designed to satisfy customer needs. *Example:* Amazon's customer-centric return policy.
2. **Total Employee Involvement:** Every employee contributes to quality. *Example:* Toyota's "Andon Cord" system allows any worker to stop the production line.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Small, incremental improvements daily. *Example:* Honda's assembly line improvements over decades.
4. **Process-Centered Approach:** Focus on improving processes, not blaming people. *Example:* Six Sigma projects at General Electric.
5. **Fact-Based Decision Making:** Use of data and statistical analysis. *Example:* SPC charts in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

**PDCA Cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &: \text{Plan — Identify a problem, analyze it, and propose a solution.} \\
D &: \text{Do — Implement the proposed solution on a small, controlled scale.} \\
C &: \text{Check — Measure the actual results and compare with expected outcomes.} \\
A &: \text{Act — If successful, standardize; if not, repeat the cycle with a new plan.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PDCA cycle is iterative — after Act, a new Plan begins, ensuring **continuous improvement**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2022]**

**(a)** Define TQM. Explain the key elements of TQM in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the barriers to TQM implementation. How can these barriers be overcome? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for (a):**

*(Same TQM definition as in Part A Q1.)*

**Key Elements of TQM:**

1. **Customer Focus:** Identify and meet customer needs. Includes *internal customers* (other departments) and *external customers* (buyers).
2. **Total Employee Involvement:** All employees, from CEO to operator, are responsible for quality. Achieved through empowerment, training, and recognition.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Ongoing, incremental improvements. Achieved through PDCA, Six Sigma, 5S, etc.
4. **Process-Centered Approach:** Focus on improving processes. Reduce variation and increase capability.
5. **Integrated System:** All departments work together as one unified system, breaking down silos.
6. **Fact-Based Decision Making:** Use statistical data, not assumptions.
7. **Communication:** Effective, timely, and transparent communication at all levels.
8. **Supplier Partnership:** Long-term, mutually beneficial relationships with suppliers.

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Barriers to TQM Implementation:**

1. **Lack of Top Management Commitment:** Without leadership support, TQM initiatives fail.
2. **Resistance to Change:** Employees fear job loss or increased workload.
3. **Inadequate Training:** Workers lack the skills to use quality tools.
4. **Poor Communication:** Misalignment between departments.
5. **Short-Term Focus:** Management seeks quick profits over long-term quality.
6. **Insufficient Resources:** Budget and time constraints.
7. **Cultural Barriers:** Hierarchical organizational cultures resist employee empowerment.

**Overcoming the Barriers:**

1. **Leadership Commitment:** Top management must actively champion TQM.
2. **Change Management:** Communicate the "why" of change, involve employees in planning.
3. **Comprehensive Training:** Invest in continuous education on quality tools.
4. **Transparent Communication:** Regular meetings, suggestion systems, open-door policies.
5. **Long-Term Vision:** Set multi-year quality goals aligned with strategy.
6. **Adequate Resource Allocation:** Budget for tools, training, and audits.
7. **Cultural Transformation:** Encourage experimentation, reward innovation, eliminate fear of failure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse TQM with ISO 9000.** ISO 9000 is a *certification standard*; TQM is a *management philosophy*. ISO 9000 can be part of TQM, but TQM is broader.
> 2. **Always attribute ideas correctly.** Deming = PDCA + 14 Points. Juran = Quality Trilogy + Fitness for Use. Ishikawa = Quality Circles + Fishbone. Crosby = Zero Defects + Quality is Free.
> 3. **Avoid one-line answers in Part B.** A 7-mark question requires at least **300-400 words** with diagrams, examples, and structured points.
> 4. **Draw a diagram whenever possible.** A PDCA diagram or TQM element circle adds 1-2 free marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TQM = Total + Quality + Management** — quality is everyone's responsibility, not a department's.
- **Five key thinkers:** Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum — know each one's main contribution.
- **PDCA Cycle** = Deming's contribution; iterative and continuous.
- **Juran's Trilogy** = Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- **Crosby's "Quality is Free"** and **Zero Defects** concept.
- **Ishikawa's** Quality Circles and Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram.
- **Customer Focus** is the *primary* dimension — internal and external customers both matter.
- **Kaizen** = Japanese for "continuous improvement" — small, daily, incremental.
- **COPQ** has four components: Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.
- **Deming's 14 Points** are the most-tested framework in KTU exams.
- **TQM is not a tool or a certificate** — it is a *culture* and a *philosophy*.
- **Six Sigma (6σ) = 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** — world-class quality benchmark.
- **Barriers to TQM:** Lack of commitment, resistance to change, poor training, short-term focus, cultural inertia.
- **Implementation phases:** Awareness $\rightarrow$ Planning $\rightarrow$ Execution $\rightarrow$ Review (continuous loop).
- **In exam answers, always include a diagram and a real-world example** (Toyota, Tata, GE, Amazon, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TQM Axioms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** TQM Axioms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of TQM Axioms

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **OEIET612 – Quality Management**, **TQM Axioms** are defined as the **fundamental, self-evident, and universally accepted principles (or "truths")** that form the philosophical and operational bedrock of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. The term *axiom* is borrowed from classical logic and mathematics — it represents a statement so fundamentally true that it does not require proof, only acceptance and consistent application.

According to the universally cited TQM literature, an **axiom of TQM** is a foundational belief that governs the behaviour, decisions, and culture of an organisation pursuing quality excellence. These axioms are *not* techniques, tools, or statistical methods — they are the **mindset and value-system** upon which all TQM tools (such as the 7 QC Tools, FMEA, Six Sigma, or the PDCA cycle) are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TQM Axioms are treated as the *philosophical foundation* of Total Quality Management. A student must be able to **list, explain, and apply** these axioms in real industrial/business scenarios. Expect direct 3-mark definitions and 7-mark descriptive answers in the University ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of TQM Axioms as the **"10 Commandments of Quality"** for an organisation. Just as a building cannot stand without a solid foundation, an organisation cannot practise TQM without first accepting these universal truths.

**Real-World Analogy — The Human Body:**
Imagine a hospital. The axioms of TQM are like the **basic laws of medicine**:
- The axiom *"Customer Satisfaction is paramount"* is like a doctor's oath — *"First, do no harm / serve the patient first."*
- The axiom *"Prevention over Inspection"* is like the principle of *preventive healthcare* (vaccination, hygiene) rather than *reactive care* (treating disease after it strikes).
- The axiom *"Continuous Improvement"* is like a doctor constantly updating medical knowledge.
- The axiom *"Total Involvement"* is like recognising that health is the responsibility of *every* organ in the body, not just the heart.

Without these "axioms" (laws of medicine), the hospital collapses. Similarly, without TQM Axioms, any quality programme collapses into a short-lived, paperwork-driven ritual.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Axiom** = Philosophical principle / belief (the *WHY*)
> - **Tool / Technique** = Statistical or managerial method (the *HOW*)
> - **System** = The framework (e.g., ISO 9001) that operationalises axioms + tools (the *STRUCTURE*)

### 1.3 Standard Terminology and Metrics

The following terms and metrics are central to the discussion of TQM Axioms and will be tested in KTU evaluations:

- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** — A measured metric reflecting the degree to which a product/service meets customer expectations.
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — The financial penalty incurred due to defects, rework, warranty claims, and lost reputation.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — The percentage of units completed correctly without any rework.
- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — A core **Six Sigma** metric (**target: 3.4 DPMO**) that quantifies process capability.
- **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** — Deming's improvement cycle, an axiomatic operationalisation.
- **Kaizen** — Japanese term for *continuous, incremental improvement*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Philosophical Hierarchy of TQM

TQM Axioms can be organised into **three logical tiers**:

**Tier 1 — Foundational Axioms (The "WHY"):**
These define *what quality means* and *why it matters*.
- Quality is defined by the **Customer**.
- Quality is a **process**, not a programme.
- Quality requires **Prevention**, not inspection.
- Quality is the **responsibility of everyone**.

**Tier 2 — Operational Axioms (The "HOW"):**
These define *how quality is achieved daily*.
- **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** is non-negotiable.
- Decisions must be **fact-based**, not opinion-based.
- **System Thinking** — improve the process, not the worker.
- **Supplier Partnership** — vendors are stakeholders, not adversaries.

**Tier 3 — Cultural Axioms (The "WHO"):**
These define *who owns quality* and *what culture sustains it*.
- **Total Involvement** of all employees.
- **Leadership Commitment** from top management.
- **Education and Training** at every level.

### 2.2 Detailed Explanation of Each Axiom

Below is the exhaustive KTU-aligned explanation of every major TQM Axiom. Each axiom is broken into the **belief**, the **operational meaning**, and the **real-world implication**.

#### Axiom 1: Quality is Defined by the Customer
- **Belief:** The customer — internal or external — is the ultimate judge of quality.
- **Operational Meaning:** Specifications, tolerances, and features are derived from customer needs (the *Voice of Customer — VOC*), not from engineering convenience.
- **Implication:** Marketing, design, production, and service must all align with VOC.

#### Axiom 2: Quality is a Process, Not a Programme
- **Belief:** Quality cannot be "installed" via a short-term campaign. It is the *result* of well-designed, stable processes.
- **Operational Meaning:** Improve the *system*; do not blame the *individual* (Deming's 85/15 rule — 85% of problems are system-related, only 15% are worker-related).
- **Implication:** Process mapping (SIPOC, flowcharts) and statistical process control (SPC) become core competencies.

#### Axiom 3: Prevention over Inspection
- **Belief:** It is cheaper and smarter to **build quality in** than to **inspect quality out**.
- **Operational Meaning:** Use *Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)*, *poka-yoke* (mistake-proofing), and robust design to prevent defects at source.
- **Implication:** Reduces **COPQ** drastically. Inspection is acknowledged as *costly, late, and ineffective* as a primary strategy.

#### Axiom 4: Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
- **Belief:** Today's "good enough" is tomorrow's "unacceptable."
- **Operational Meaning:** Apply the **PDCA cycle** repeatedly. Even a 1% daily improvement compounds to ~37× improvement in a year.
- **Implication:** Every employee must be trained in **basic problem-solving** (the 7 QC Tools).

#### Axiom 5: Fact-Based Decision Making
- **Belief:** In God we trust; all others must bring **data** (W. Edwards Deming).
- **Operational Meaning:** Decisions are based on statistical evidence (control charts, Pareto analysis, hypothesis testing), not on intuition, hierarchy, or anecdote.
- **Implication:** Build a culture of measurement and statistical literacy.

#### Axiom 6: Total Involvement of People
- **Belief:** Quality is not the Quality Department's job. It is *every* employee's job.
- **Operational Meaning:** Cross-functional **Quality Circles**, **Self-Directed Work Teams**, and **Suggestion Systems** are deployed.
- **Implication:** Empowerment + Accountability at every level.

#### Axiom 7: Leadership and Top Management Commitment
- **Belief:** Without **visible, sustained** leadership commitment, TQM dies.
- **Operational Meaning:** Leaders set the quality vision, allocate resources, remove barriers, and personally champion quality initiatives.
- **Implication:** *Quality Policy*, *Quality Objectives*, and *Management Review* are leadership artefacts.

#### Axiom 8: System Thinking
- **Belief:** An organisation is an **interconnected system**, not a set of isolated departments.
- **Operational Meaning:** Optimise the *whole* system (e.g., the supply chain) rather than sub-optimising individual functions.
- **Implication:** Recognise that improving one department at the expense of another is a **zero-sum game**.

#### Axiom 9: Supplier Partnership
- **Belief:** Suppliers are **partners**, not adversaries.
- **Operational Meaning:** Long-term contracts, shared training, joint problem-solving, and reduced supplier base (strategic sourcing).
- **Implication:** Improves incoming quality and reduces inspection burden.

#### Axiom 10: Education and Training
- **Belief:** A trained mind is the most valuable quality asset.
- **Operational Meaning:** Continuous, structured training in statistical tools, problem-solving, customer relations, and domain skills.
- **Implication:** Training is an *investment*, not a *cost*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Metrics)

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Purpose / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units out of Process}}{\text{Total Units Entering Process}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Measures process capability; high FPY = stable process |
| Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Decimal / Percent | Cumulative yield across multiple process steps |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects per million | Six Sigma benchmark; **target 3.4 DPMO** = 6σ level |
| Sigma Level | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}\right)} + 1.5$ | Sigma (σ) | Higher σ = fewer defects |
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Prevention Cost}$ | Currency (₹, $, €) | Quantifies the *price* of non-conformance |
| Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Performance Rating} \times \text{Importance Weight})}{\sum \text{Importance Weights}}$ | Index (0–100) | Weighted satisfaction measurement |
| Kaizen Compound Improvement | $\text{Final Value} = \text{Initial Value} \times (1 + r)^{n}$ | Dimensionless | With $r = 0.01$ daily, $n = 365$ gives ~37.78× improvement |
| Deming 85/15 Rule | $85\% \text{ System} \quad : \quad 15\% \text{ Worker}$ | Percent | Locus of problem ownership |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorise the DPMO and FPY formulas letter-perfect. The 3.4 DPMO benchmark for 6-Sigma and the 85/15 Deming rule are *favourite* KTU Part A questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These axioms are not merely philosophical — they are operationalised daily in:

- **Automotive industry:** Toyota Production System (axioms: prevention, Kaizen, system thinking, respect for people).
- **Software industry:** CMMI and Agile methodologies embody prevention, continuous improvement, and total involvement.
- **Healthcare:** Joint Commission International (JCI) accreditation standards enforce fact-based decisions, customer (patient) focus, and continuous improvement.
- **Aerospace:** AS9100 standard mandates supplier partnership, prevention, and leadership commitment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: The Six Sigma Linkage

One of the most important *quantitative* outcomes of TQM Axioms is the **Six Sigma** level of quality. Let us derive the relationship between DPMO, the normal distribution, and the sigma level step-by-step.

**Step 1: Define Defect Rate (Yield)**

$$Y = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$$

**Step 2: Define Defect Probability**

$$p = 1 - Y$$

**Step 3: Convert to Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**

$$\text{DPMO} = p \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4: Convert DPMO back to a Z-score (long-term capability)**

The Z-score in a standard normal distribution is found using the inverse cumulative distribution function:

$$Z = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - p\right)$$

where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the *inverse standard normal cumulative distribution function*.

**Step 5: Apply the Industry 1.5-σ Shift (Motorola Convention)**

In real processes, the mean drifts by approximately **1.5σ** over the long term. Therefore, the *true* sigma level is:

$$\sigma_{\text{process}} = Z + 1.5$$

**Step 6: Example Calculation for 3.4 DPMO (the 6-Sigma benchmark)**

Given $\text{DPMO} = 3.4$, we compute $p = 3.4 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0000034$.

$$Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.0000034) = \Phi^{-1}(0.9999966) \approx 4.5$$

$$\sigma_{\text{process}} = 4.5 + 1.5 = 6.0 \; \sigma$$

**Interpretation:** A 6-σ process produces only **3.4 defects per million opportunities** — translating the TQM axiom of *prevention* and *process excellence* into a quantitative, auditable benchmark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The 1.5σ shift convention was introduced by **Motorola** in the 1980s (Bill Smith). It is an *engineering convention*, not a natural law. KTU exams often ask: *"Why 3.4 DPMO corresponds to 6-Sigma?"* — the answer hinges on this 1.5σ shift.

### 3.2 Derivation: Compounding Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)

The Kaizen axiom states that **small, continuous improvements compound exponentially**. Let us derive this mathematically.

**Step 1: Initial State**
Let the initial performance metric be $P_0$.

**Step 2: Daily Improvement Rate**
Let the daily fractional improvement rate be $r$. A modest $r = 0.01$ (1% per day) is achievable.

**Step 3: After $n$ Days**

$$P_n = P_0 \times (1 + r)^{n}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $r = 0.01$, $n = 365$**

$$P_{365} = P_0 \times (1.01)^{365}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate $(1.01)^{365}$ Using the Continuous Compounding Limit**

We know that $\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{x}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{x}$. Approximating with the identity $(1.01)^{365} \approx e^{0.01 \times 365} = e^{3.65}$:

$$P_{365} \approx P_0 \times e^{3.65} \approx P_0 \times 38.47$$

**Conclusion:** A mere 1% improvement every working day transforms performance by **~38.5 times** in one year. This mathematically justifies the Kaizen axiom as a **strategic imperative**, not a feel-good slogan.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Axiom-to-Tool Mapping

This is a high-yield KTU matrix linking each axiom to its operational tool and metric.

| Axiom | Operational Tool | Quantitative Metric | KTU Exam Favourite |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer is the judge | Voice of Customer (VOC), QFD | Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) | High |
| Quality is a process | SIPOC, Flowcharts, SPC | FPY, RTY | High |
| Prevention over inspection | FMEA, poka-yoke | COPQ, DPMO | Very High |
| Continuous improvement | PDCA, 7 QC Tools | Kaizen multiplier | Very High |
| Fact-based decisions | Control charts, Pareto, Hypothesis testing | p-value, Cp, Cpk | High |
| Total involvement | Quality Circles, Suggestion schemes | Suggestions per employee | Medium |
| Leadership commitment | Quality Policy, MBO | Resource allocation % | Medium |
| System thinking | Value stream mapping | Lead time, throughput | High |
| Supplier partnership | Strategic sourcing, supplier audits | Supplier defect rate | Medium |
| Education & training | Training hours, certifications | Training ROI | Medium |

### 3.4 Deming's 14 Points vs TQM Axioms — Critical Distinction

A frequently confused topic in KTU exams is the difference between **TQM Axioms** and **Deming's 14 Points**. The mapping is as follows:

- **TQM Axioms** = *Universal, philosophical truths* about quality.
- **Deming's 14 Points** = *Prescriptive actions* (operational prescriptions) derived *from* those axioms.
- **Juran's Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement) = Another *operational framework* aligned with the axioms.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "Deming's 14 Points" as a *definition* of TQM Axioms. The 14 Points are *applications*, not the axioms themselves.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TQM Axioms — Master Architecture Flow

```mermaid

    U1 --> V1[Organisational Excellence]
```

### 4.2 TQM Axiom Hierarchy — Tier Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Tier1[Tier 1 Foundational Axioms - The WHY]
        T1A[Customer is the Judge]

    end
    
    subgraph Tier2[Tier 2 Operational Axioms - The HOW]

```

### 4.3 Axiom-to-Metric Linkage Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Axiom 1 Customer Focus] --> M1[Customer Satisfaction Index]
    A2[Axiom 2 Process Quality] --> M2[First Pass Yield]
    A3[Axiom 3 Prevention] --> M3[Defects Per Million Opportunities]
    A4[Axiom 4 Kaizen] --> M4[Compound Improvement Factor]
    A5[Axiom 5 Fact Based] --> M5[Statistical Significance p value]
    A6[Axiom 6 Involvement] --> M6[Suggestions per Employee]
    A7[Axiom 7 Leadership] --> M7[Quality Budget Allocation]

    M1 --> OUT1[Business Excellence]
    M2 --> OUT1
    M3 --> OUT1
    M4 --> OUT1
    M5 --> OUT1
    M6 --> OUT1
    M7 --> OUT1
    M8 --> OUT1
    M9 --> OUT1
    M10 --> OUT1
```

### 4.4 Process Visualisation: Kaizen Compounding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Day 0 Performance P0] --> S1[Day 1 P0 x 1.01]
    S1 --> S2[Day 2 P0 x 1.01 squared]
    S2 --> S3[Day 7 P0 x 1.01 to power 7]
    S3 --> S30[Day 30 P0 x 1.01 to power 30 approx 1.35 x]
    S30 --> S180[Day 180 P0 x 1.01 to power 180 approx 6.0 x]
    S180 --> S365[Day 365 P0 x 1.01 to power 365 approx 38.5 x]
    
    S365 --> RESULT[Compounding Kaizen Effect]
    RESULT --> NOTE[Small daily improvements create massive annual transformation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define TQM Axioms. List any four major TQM Axioms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
TQM Axioms are the fundamental, self-evident, universally accepted philosophical principles that form the foundation of Total Quality Management. They represent the core beliefs upon which all quality tools, systems, and behaviours are built.

**List of Four Axioms (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Customer Satisfaction** is the ultimate goal of any organisation.
2. **Quality is a process**, not a short-term programme.
3. **Prevention** is preferable to inspection.
4. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** must be embedded in the organisational culture.

*(Alternatively, students may list: Total Involvement, Fact-Based Decisions, Leadership Commitment, System Thinking, Supplier Partnership, Education & Training.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Explain the TQM Axiom "Prevention over Inspection" with a suitable industrial example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Explanation (2 marks):**
The axiom states that it is **far more effective and economical to design quality into a product or process at the design stage** than to detect defects through inspection after production. Inspection only *identifies* defects; prevention *eliminates* them at the source. This reduces rework, warranty costs, and customer dissatisfaction.

**Industrial Example (1 mark):**
In an automobile assembly line, instead of inspecting every car for loose wheel nuts at the end of the line, a **torque-limiting wrench (poka-yoke)** is installed at the wheel-station itself. This *prevents* the defect of an under-tightened wheel from ever occurring — exemplifying prevention over inspection.

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**

**Q.A) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Explain the ten TQM Axioms in detail. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Critically analyse how the TQM Axiom *"Customer is the Judge of Quality"* is implemented in a software services company. *(7 marks)*

##### **Solution to Q.A(a) — The Ten TQM Axioms (7 marks):**

1. **Customer is the Judge (0.7 mark):** Quality is defined by the customer's needs, expectations, and perceptions — not by internal standards. The *Voice of Customer* (VOC) drives design.

2. **Quality is a Process, not a Programme (0.7 mark):** Quality is the *output* of stable, well-designed processes. Improve the system, not the worker (Deming's 85/15 rule).

3. **Prevention over Inspection (0.7 mark):** Build quality in at the design stage using FMEA and poka-yoke. Inspection is a costly, late, and reactive approach.

4. **Continuous Improvement — Kaizen (0.7 mark):** Apply the **PDCA cycle** repeatedly. A 1% daily improvement compounds to ~38× in one year.

5. **Fact-Based Decision Making (0.7 mark):** Use data and statistical tools (control charts, Pareto, hypothesis testing). Intuition and hierarchy are *not* substitutes for evidence.

6. **Total Involvement of People (0.7 mark):** Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor, is responsible for quality. Empowerment + accountability.

7. **Leadership & Top Management Commitment (0.7 mark):** Leaders set vision, allocate resources, and *personally* champion quality. Without this, TQM fails.

8. **System Thinking (0.7 mark):** Optimise the whole value chain, not isolated departments. Sub-optimisation creates silos.

9. **Supplier Partnership (0.7 mark):** Treat suppliers as long-term partners with shared goals, training, and joint problem-solving.

10. **Education and Training (0.7 mark):** Continuous, structured training in statistical tools, problem-solving, and soft skills. Training is an *investment*, not a cost.

##### **Solution to Q.A(b) — Customer as Judge in a Software Services Company (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — VOC Capture (1.5 marks):**
The software company uses **interviews, surveys, focus groups, and user-story workshops** to capture the *Voice of Customer* before writing a single line of code. Personas, empathy maps, and journey maps are constructed.

**Step 2 — Translating VOC into Specifications (1.5 marks):**
Customer needs are converted into **functional requirements, non-functional requirements (NFRs)** such as performance, security, and usability, and formal **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**.

**Step 3 — Agile & Iterative Validation (1.5 marks):**
The company adopts **Scrum / Agile** with **2-week sprints**. Each sprint ends with a *Sprint Review* where the customer (Product Owner) *accepts or rejects* user stories. This embodies the axiom — the customer is the *real* judge.

**Step 4 — Post-Delivery Measurement (1.5 marks):**
Tools such as **Net Promoter Score (NPS)**, **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)**, and **Customer Effort Score (CES)** are tracked continuously. Bug escape rates, churn, and renewal rates are correlated with quality perception.

**Step 5 — Critical Analysis (1 mark):**
*Strengths:* Rapid feedback, customer-validated features, lower rework.
*Limitations:* Customer expectations evolve (Kano model's "delighters" become "must-haves" over time); thus, the axiom requires *dynamic* interpretation — not a one-time VOC study but a *continuous dialogue*.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative:**

**Q.B) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** Explain Deming's 85/15 Rule and the PDCA cycle as manifestations of TQM Axioms. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** A manufacturing unit produces 50,000 units/day with 4 defect opportunities per unit. If the goal is to achieve 6-Sigma quality, calculate the maximum allowable DPMO and the corresponding defects/day. *(7 marks)*

##### **Solution to Q.B(a) — Deming's 85/15 Rule & PDCA (7 marks):**

**Deming's 85/15 Rule (3.5 marks):**
According to W. Edwards Deming, approximately **85% of quality problems in an organisation are caused by the system (processes, procedures, management systems)** and only **15% are attributable to the individual worker**. The TQM axiom *"Quality is a Process, not a Programme"* is operationalised by this rule. When a defect occurs, managers must *first* investigate the system — the training, the equipment, the procedure, the inputs — before blaming the worker. This shifts the organisation from a *blame culture* to a *learning culture*.

**PDCA Cycle — Deming's Improvement Wheel (3.5 marks):**

The **Plan–Do–Check–Act** cycle operationalises the *Continuous Improvement* axiom:
- **Plan:** Identify the problem, analyse root cause (using 5-Why / Fishbone), and devise a countermeasure.
- **Do:** Implement the countermeasure on a small scale.
- **Check:** Measure outcomes using data; compare actual vs. expected results.
- **Act:** If successful, *standardise*; if not, revise the plan and re-iterate.

The PDCA cycle is repeated *indefinitely*, embodying *Kaizen* — the axiom that improvement has no终点 (endpoint).

##### **Solution to Q.B(b) — 6-Sigma DPMO Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Daily production = 50,000 units
- Defect opportunities per unit = 4
- Target: 6-Sigma quality

**Step 1: Recall 6-Sigma Benchmark (1 mark):**
At 6-Sigma level, the industry standard is **DPMO = 3.4** (with the 1.5σ shift convention).

**Step 2: Calculate Total Opportunities per Day (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = \text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}$$

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = 50{,}000 \times 4 = 200{,}000$$

**Step 3: Apply the DPMO Formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Total Opportunities}} \times 10^{6}$$

$$3.4 = \dfrac{D}{200{,}000} \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4: Solve for Maximum Allowable Defects $D$ (1.5 marks):**

$$D = \dfrac{3.4 \times 200{,}000}{10^{6}} = \dfrac{680{,}000}{10^{6}} = 0.68$$

**Conclusion:**
- **Maximum allowable DPMO = 3.4 defects per million opportunities** *(0.5 mark)*
- **Maximum allowable defects per day ≈ 0.68**, i.e., fewer than 1 defect per day in 200,000 opportunities. *(0.5 mark)*

**Engineering Insight (Bonus):** This is a *near-zero-defect* benchmark. Achieving it requires **prevention-based design, statistical process control, and disciplined PDCA cycles** — directly invoking the TQM axioms of *Prevention*, *Process*, and *Continuous Improvement*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TQM Axioms with Deming's 14 Points.** The 14 Points are *prescriptions derived from* the axioms. Writing "create constancy of purpose" as an *axiom* will be marked wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift** in DPMO-to-Sigma conversions. Many students compute $Z = 4.5$ but write the answer as "5-sigma," losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Vague answers on "Prevention vs Inspection."** Always quantify with **COPQ** (cost of poor quality) to score full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "why" and jumping to the "what."** KTU examiners award marks for *reasoning*, not just bullet points. Always state *why* an axiom matters.
> 5. **Mixing up TQM Axioms with ISO 9001 Clauses.** ISO 9001 is a *certifiable standard*; TQM axioms are *philosophical foundations*. They are related but not identical.
> 6. **Calculation errors in DPMO** — forgetting to multiply opportunities by units, or misplacing the $10^6$ factor.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** TQM Axioms = fundamental, universal, self-evident beliefs forming the philosophical foundation of Total Quality Management.
- **Origin:** Rooted in the works of **W. Edwards Deming, Joseph Juran, Philip Crosby, and Kaoru Ishikawa**.
- **Ten Core Axioms** (must memorise in order):
  1. Customer is the Judge
  2. Quality is a Process (not a programme)
  3. Prevention over Inspection
  4. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen / PDCA)
  5. Fact-Based Decision Making
  6. Total Involvement of People
  7. Leadership & Top Management Commitment
  8. System Thinking
  9. Supplier Partnership
  10. Education and Training
- **Deming's 85/15 Rule:** 85% system causes, 15% worker causes — supports the *Process* axiom.
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act — operationalises *Kaizen*.
- **Key Quantitative Metrics:**
  - $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}$ where $D$ = defects, $N$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit.
  - **6-σ benchmark: DPMO = 3.4** (with 1.5σ shift).
  - **Kaizen compound factor:** $(1.01)^{365} \approx 38.5$.
- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI):** Weighted average of performance × importance.
- **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality):** Internal failure + External failure + Appraisal + Prevention costs.
- **TQM Axioms ≠ Deming's 14 Points ≠ ISO 9001 Clauses** — they are related but distinct concepts.
- **TQM Axioms ≠ Juran's Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) — the trilogy is an *operational model* derived from axioms.
- **Real-world exemplars:** Toyota Production System (axioms of Kaizen, prevention, respect for people); Motorola (origin of 6-Sigma).
- **Three-Tier Hierarchy:** Tier 1 (Why: Foundational) → Tier 2 (How: Operational) → Tier 3 (Who: Cultural).
- **Always answer in KTU exams:** Definition → List → Detailed Explanation → Industrial Example → Quantification (if applicable).
- **Common confusions to avoid:** Axioms vs Tools, Deming 14 Points vs TQM Axioms, Six Sigma vs TQM, ISO 9001 vs TQM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consequences of Total Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Consequences of Total Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Consequences of Total Quality – Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequences of Total Quality (TQM)** refer to the measurable outcomes, results, and transformative effects — both tangible and intangible — that an organization experiences as a direct result of the successful adoption, implementation, and cultural integration of Total Quality Management philosophy across all hierarchical levels and processes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terms, these consequences are classified into **four beneficiary domains** as per the David A. Garvin framework adapted for Quality Management curricula:

1. **Consequences to Customers**
2. **Consequences to Employees**
3. **Consequences to Suppliers / Partners**
4. **Consequences to the Society at Large**

Additionally, the syllabus emphasizes the **dual financial consequence**:
- **Costs of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — losses incurred due to defects, rework, and warranty.
- **Costs of Quality Assurance** — investments made to prevent defects and build quality systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Healthy Lifestyle" Metaphor**
>
> Think of TQM like a person adopting a strict, disciplined, healthy lifestyle — eating nutritious food, exercising daily, sleeping well. The *consequences* of this lifestyle are not just one thing; they ripple outward:
>
> - To the **person** (employee): better energy, fewer sick days, pride in fitness.
> - To the **family** (customers): less stress, happier relationships, better service.
> - To the **gym trainer/dietician** (suppliers): long-term partnership, trust.
> - To the **community** (society): less burden on hospitals, more productive citizen.
> - And finally, the **financial outcome** — you spend less on medicines (less COPQ) and more on gym memberships (prevention costs), but overall you are wealthier in the long run.
>
> Just like fitness, TQM is **not an event — it is a culture with consequences**.

## 1.3 Key Constants / Standard Metrics

- **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality): Typically ranges from **15% to 40%** of total operating costs in unrefined organizations.
- **Target Industry Benchmark**: World-class organizations target **COPQ < 5%** of sales revenue.
- **Customer Retention Rate**: Each **5% improvement** in retention can increase profits by **25% to 95%** (Bain & Co. research, widely cited in KTU modules).
- **Pareto's 80/20 Rule**: ~**80%** of quality problems arise from ~**20%** of causes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Consequences — Expanded Logic

### **Pillar 1: Consequences to Customers**

| Sub-Consequence | Operational Meaning |
|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Improved Product Reliability** | Fewer breakdowns, longer product life. |
| **Enhanced Customer Satisfaction** | Lower complaint rates, higher NPS (Net Promoter Score). |
| **Better Value for Money** | Optimal price-to-quality ratio. |
| **Reduced Total Cost of Ownership** | Lower maintenance, repair, and replacement costs over product lifecycle. |
| **Increased Trust & Loyalty** | Repeat purchase, brand advocacy. |

**The 'Why' Behind It:** When quality is built *in*, defects are prevented *upstream*, leading to superior products reaching the customer with **zero defects** culture alignment.

---

### **Pillar 2: Consequences to Employees**

- **Job Satisfaction** rises as workers are empowered through Quality Circles and Kaizen teams.
- **Pride of Workmanship** is cultivated — every employee becomes a "quality inspector of their own output".
- **Reduced Workplace Stress** because rework, firefighting, and blame-culture decrease.
- **Improved Morale & Retention** as employees feel their contributions matter.
- **Skill Enhancement** through continuous training (a pillar of TQM).

---

### **Pillar 3: Consequences to Suppliers**

- **Long-Term Partnership Relationships** replace transactional, adversarial dealings.
- **Mutual Trust & Open Communication** — suppliers are treated as extensions of the organization.
- **Stable Order Flow** — fewer suppliers, higher volumes per supplier.
- **Collaborative Problem Solving** — joint kaizen, shared technical knowledge.
- **Reduced Inspection Costs** — incoming inspection reduces as supplier quality improves.

---

### **Pillar 4: Consequences to Society**

- **Environmental Protection** — reduced waste, pollution, and resource consumption.
- **Resource Conservation** — efficient use of raw materials and energy.
- **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** uplift — ethical labor practices.
- **Economic Prosperity** — higher employment, GDP contribution through quality exports.
- **Safer Products** in the market (e.g., automobile recalls reduced).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any numerical problem on this topic.**

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|------------------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Prevention Cost}$ | Currency (₹, $, etc.) |
| **COPQ as % of Sales** | $COPQ\,\% = \dfrac{COPQ}{\text{Total Sales Revenue}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Quality Cost Ratio (P-A Ratio)** | $P/A = \dfrac{\text{Prevention Cost}}{\text{Appraisal Cost}}$ | Dimensionless; **Target > 1** |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passing without rework}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI = \dfrac{\text{No. of Satisfied Customers}}{\text{Total Respondents}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}}$ | Per Million Opportunities |
| **Return on Quality Investment** | $ROQI = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit from Quality}}{\text{Quality Investment Cost}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Pareto Threshold** | $80\%$ of problems from $20\%$ of causes | Empirical; not a strict formula |

> **Note on notation:** All table cells use `\vert` or descriptive words instead of the vertical bar symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Indian manufacturing contexts (KSRTC buses, Tata Steel plants, Maruti Suzuki assembly lines), TQM consequences translate into:
- Reduced warranty claims.
- ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 certification readiness.
- Export competitiveness under Make-in-India initiatives.
- Lower employee attrition in shop-floor roles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of COPQ Optimization Logic

The core philosophical derivation behind TQM consequences is that **prevention costs rise initially, but failure costs fall dramatically**, leading to a **net reduction in total quality cost**.

Let:

- $P$ = Prevention Cost
- $A$ = Appraisal Cost  
- $IFC$ = Internal Failure Cost
- $EFC$ = External Failure Cost

Total Cost of Quality (TCQ):

$$
TCQ = P + A + IFC + EFC
$$

As quality investment ($P$) increases:

$$
P \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad IFC \downarrow \downarrow \quad \text{and} \quad EFC \downarrow \downarrow
$$

The optimal quality level $Q^*$ occurs where:

$$
\dfrac{d(TCQ)}{dP} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{dP}{dP} + \dfrac{dA}{dP} + \dfrac{d(IFC)}{dP} + \dfrac{d(EFC)}{dP} = 0
$$

Since $\dfrac{dP}{dP} = 1$ (prevention cost rises 1:1 with investment) and both failure costs fall:

$$
1 + \dfrac{dA}{dP} + \dfrac{d(IFC)}{dP} + \dfrac{d(EFC)}{dP} = 0
$$

Because the failure costs drop faster than prevention rises, the net derivative becomes negative **beyond a threshold** — meaning TCQ first rises, peaks, then falls. The minimum point of TCQ is the economically optimal quality investment.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A manufacturing unit produces **50,000 units/month**. Data:

- Prevention cost = ₹2,00,000
- Appraisal cost = ₹1,50,000
- Internal failure cost = ₹3,00,000
- External failure cost = ₹1,50,000
- Sales revenue = ₹50,00,000

**Compute:**

**(a)** Total COPQ
**(b)** COPQ as % of Sales
**(c)** P/A ratio
**(d)** Comment on TQM readiness.

---

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Cost of Poor Quality:**

$$
COPQ = P + A + IFC + EFC
$$

$$
COPQ = 200000 + 150000 + 300000 + 150000
$$

$$
COPQ = 800000 \;\text{₹}
$$

**Step 2 — COPQ as % of Sales:**

$$
COPQ\,\% = \dfrac{800000}{5000000} \times 100
$$

$$
COPQ\,\% = 16\%
$$

**Step 3 — P/A Ratio:**

$$
P/A = \dfrac{200000}{150000} = 1.33
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- A **COPQ of 16%** is far above the world-class benchmark of **<5%**.
- A **P/A ratio of 1.33 (>1)** indicates the firm is **proactively investing in prevention**, which is a healthy sign.
- However, **IFC = ₹3,00,000** is still the largest cost bucket, signaling **shop-floor process issues** that need root-cause analysis (Ishikawa / 5-Why).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Always tie your numerical answer back to a **strategic recommendation** — KTU evaluators award marks for interpretation, not just arithmetic.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Severity vs. Frequency of Consequences

| Consequence Domain | Likelihood of Realization | Severity if Realized | KTU Exam Weightage |
|--------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Customer satisfaction | **High** | **High** | Very High (Part B standard) |
| Employee retention | Medium | High | Medium (Part A) |
| Supplier partnership | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Society impact | Low (long-term) | Very High | Low (conceptual) |
| COPQ reduction | **High** | **High** | Very High (numerical) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Four Pillars of TQM Consequences

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TQM Implementation]:::core --> B1[Consequences to Customers]
    A --> B2[Consequences to Employees]
    A --> B3[Consequences to Suppliers]
    A --> B4[Consequences to Society]

    B1 --> C1[Improved Satisfaction]
    B1 --> C2[Reduced TCO]

    B3 --> E1[Long-term Partnership]
    B3 --> E2[Mutual Trust]
    B3 --> E3[Stable Order Flow]

    B4 --> F1[Environmental Protection]
    B4 --> F2[CSR Uplift]

    A -.-> G[Financial Outcome]

```

```mermaid

    C -->|High| F[Low IFC and EFC]

    J --> L[Market Loss]

```

## 4.3 Cause-Effect Schematic — How TQM Ripples Outward

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Management Commitment]:::root --> A1[Quality Policy]
    A1 --> A2[Employee Training]
    A2 --> A3[Process Standardization]
    A3 --> A4[Supplier Integration]
```
