In the context of KTU Quality Management (OEIET612), a scatter diagram is officially classified as one of the **"Seven Tools of Quality Control"** (7 QC Tools), originally codified by **Kaoru Ishikawa** of Japan. It is specifically used in the *Analyze* phase of the DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology under Six Sigma, as well as throughout the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *A Scatter Diagram is a graphical technique used to study the possible relationship between two variables. The independent variable (cause) is plotted on the X-axis and the dependent variable (effect) is plotted on the Y-axis. By observing the pattern of plotted points, the analyst can determine whether the two variables are correlated, and if so, the nature and strength of that correlation.*

**Key Terminology:**
- **Independent Variable (X-axis):** The presumed *cause* or *input* variable that the analyst controls or observes (e.g., temperature, pressure, training hours).
- **Dependent Variable (Y-axis):** The *effect* or *output* variable that responds to changes in the independent variable (e.g., product yield, defect rate, sales).
- **Data Pair (x, y):** A single observation containing one measurement of X and its corresponding measurement of Y.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a baker trying to understand why some cakes turn out fluffy and others flat. You suspect that the **amount of baking powder** (a cause) influences the **height of the cake** (an effect).

You bake 20 cakes, recording how much baking powder you used in each, and measuring the final height. If you simply listed these numbers in a table, it would be hard to see a pattern. But if you plotted each cake as a single dot on a graph — with baking powder on the horizontal axis and cake height on the vertical axis — you would immediately *see* the story: maybe the dots slope upward (more powder = taller cakes), or maybe they scatter randomly (powder doesn't matter at all).

That visual story is what a **Scatter Diagram** provides. It is the **"lens"** through which two variables are observed together, allowing the human brain (which is exceptional at pattern recognition) to instantly detect relationships that raw numbers would hide.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:**
> Consider a heat treatment process. An engineer suspects that the **soaking temperature** (X) affects the **ultimate tensile strength** of a steel alloy (Y). By plotting each heat-treatment batch as a point on a scatter diagram, the engineer can visually confirm whether higher temperatures yield stronger steel, and how tight (predictable) that relationship is. This is the everyday reality of process optimization in manufacturing.

## 1.3 Standard Statistical Metrics Used

The following **bold** values are standard reference benchmarks used universally in interpreting scatter diagrams:

- **Correlation Coefficient Range:** $\mathbf{-1 \leq r \leq +1}$
- **Perfect Positive Correlation:** $\mathbf{r = +1}$
- **Perfect Negative Correlation:** $\mathbf{r = -1}$
- **No Correlation:** $\mathbf{r = 0}$
- **Strong Correlation Threshold:** $\mathbf{\vert r \vert \geq 0.7}$
- **Weak Correlation Threshold:** $\mathbf{\vert r \vert \leq 0.3}$
- **Minimum Recommended Sample Size (KTU standard):** $\mathbf{n \geq 30}$ paired observations for reliable inference.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose and Objectives of a Scatter Diagram

The scatter diagram serves **four core analytical purposes** within quality management:

1. **Relationship Detection:** To discover and confirm whether a statistically meaningful relationship exists between two variables.
2. **Pattern Classification:** To classify the nature of the relationship (positive, negative, linear, non-linear, or none).
3. **Strength Quantification:** To visually estimate the strength (tightness) of the association.
4. **Causality Exploration (Preliminary):** To provide *initial evidence* supporting or refuting a cause-and-effect hypothesis, although correlation does not strictly prove causation.

## 2.2 The Logic Step-by-Step (How a Scatter Diagram Works)

### Step 1 — Hypothesis Formation
Before plotting, the analyst must form a clear hypothesis: *"Does variable X influence variable Y?"* This guides data collection.

### Step 2 — Paired Data Collection
Collect **n pairs** of (xᵢ, yᵢ) observations where each pair represents simultaneous measurements under identical conditions. The KTU convention is **n ≥ 30**.

### Step 3 — Axis Assignment
- Place the **independent (cause)** variable on the **horizontal X-axis**.
- Place the **dependent (effect)** variable on the **vertical Y-axis**.

### Step 4 — Plotting
Mark each pair (xᵢ, yᵢ) as a single dot on the graph. **Do not connect the dots with lines** — the points are individual observations.

### Step 5 — Pattern Recognition
Examine the cloud of points to determine the dominant trend:
- Upward slope → positive correlation
- Downward slope → negative correlation
- Random cloud → no correlation
- Curved shape → non-linear correlation

### Step 6 — Quantification
Compute the **Pearson Correlation Coefficient (r)** to numerically confirm the visual impression.

## 2.3 Seven Recognizable Scatter Patterns

| # | Pattern Name | Visual Description | Correlation Coefficient (r) | KTU Interpretation |
|---|--------------|---------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Strong Positive | Tight cluster sloping upward (lower-left to upper-right) | $r \approx +1$ | Direct proportional cause-effect |
| 2 | Weak Positive | Loose cloud sloping gently upward | $0 < r < +0.5$ | Likely relationship, noisy |
| 3 | Strong Negative | Tight cluster sloping downward (upper-left to lower-right) | $r \approx -1$ | Inverse cause-effect |
| 4 | Weak Negative | Loose cloud sloping gently downward | $-0.5 < r < 0$ | Possible inverse influence |
| 5 | No Correlation | Random circular cloud, no slope | $r \approx 0$ | Variables are independent |
| 6 | Perfect Positive | All points lie exactly on a straight upward line | $r = +1$ | Mathematical certainty |
| 7 | Non-Linear (Curvilinear) | Points form a U-shape, parabola, or exponential curve | $r \approx 0$ but non-linear fit exists | Non-linear relationship |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU student must memorize for the Scatter Diagram topic. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Formula Symbol | Formula Expression | Meaning / Use |
|----------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Pearson Correlation Coefficient | $r = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right]\left[n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2\right]}}$ | Computes $r$ directly from raw data |
| Pearson Coefficient (Mean Form) | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Computes $r$ using deviations from means |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = r^2$ | Proportion of variance in Y explained by X |
| Mean of X | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Average of independent variable |
| Mean of Y | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum y_i}{n}$ | Average of dependent variable |
| Regression Slope | $b = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Slope of best-fit line on scatter |
| Regression Intercept | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ | Y-intercept of best-fit line |
| Regression Line Equation | $\hat{y} = a + bx$ | Best-fit line used for prediction |
| Strength Threshold (Strong) | $\vert r \vert \geq 0.7$ | Indicative of strong relationship |
| Strength Threshold (Moderate) | $0.3 < \vert r \vert < 0.7$ | Indicative of moderate relationship |
| Strength Threshold (Weak) | $\vert r \vert \leq 0.3$ | Indicative of weak or no relationship |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | X Variable (Cause) | Y Variable (Effect) | Practical Use |
|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Manufacturing | Cutting speed (rpm) | Surface roughness (µm) | Optimize machining parameters |
| Civil Engineering | Cement content (kg/m³) | Compressive strength (MPa) | Mix design optimization |
| Software Quality | Code review duration (hrs) | Defect density (per KLOC) | Process improvement |
| Chemical Engineering | Reaction temperature (°C) | Conversion yield (%) | Reactor optimization |
| Healthcare Quality | Handwashing frequency | Hospital infection rate | Hygiene protocol design |
| Operations Management | Employee training hours | Productivity index | HR policy validation |

## 2.6 Limitations and Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Frequently Tests These Limitations:**
> 1. **Correlation ≠ Causation:** A scatter diagram only shows *association*; it cannot prove that X *causes* Y. A hidden third variable (lurking variable) may be responsible.
> 2. **Outlier Sensitivity:** A single extreme point can dramatically distort the perceived pattern.
> 3. **Sample Size Dependency:** With small $n$ (< 30), patterns can appear by random chance.
> 4. **Linearity Assumption:** Pearson's $r$ only measures *linear* relationships; it will report $r \approx 0$ even for perfect parabolic data.
> 5. **Range Restriction:** If X is sampled over a narrow range, the true relationship may be hidden.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Pearson's Correlation Coefficient (r)

The Pearson Correlation Coefficient is the **single most important quantitative metric** associated with a scatter diagram. We derive it step-by-step below.

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

Pearson's $r$ is a **dimensionless, unit-free number** that quantifies two things simultaneously:
- The **direction** (sign) of the linear relationship: positive or negative.
- The **strength** (magnitude) of the linear relationship: how tightly the points cluster around a straight line.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Starting Intuition:** We want a measure that becomes large when high X corresponds to high Y, and large negative when high X corresponds to low Y.

**Step 1 — Define Covariance:**
The covariance measures the joint variability of X and Y around their means.

$$
\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})
$$

*Logic:* If when $x_i > \bar{x}$ we also have $y_i > \bar{y}$, the product $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ is positive, contributing to a positive covariance.

**Step 2 — Normalize by Standard Deviations:**
Covariance has units (the product of X-units and Y-units), so it cannot be universally compared. Divide by the product of standard deviations to remove units.

$$
r = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Definitions of Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma_X = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}}, \quad \sigma_Y = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine and Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}{n}}} \\[1em]
&= \frac{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\dfrac{1}{n}\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}} \\[1em]
&= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The $\frac{1}{n}$ cancels from numerator and denominator, giving the cleaner form. This is the **mean-deviation form** of $r$.

**Step 5 — Expand to Raw-Score (Computational) Form:**

Expanding $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = x_i y_i - \bar{x} y_i - \bar{y} x_i + \bar{x}\bar{y}$ and summing over all $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) &= \sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y} - n\bar{y}\bar{x} + n\bar{x}\bar{y} \\
&= \sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ and $\bar{y} = \frac{\sum y_i}{n}$:

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = \sum x_i y_i - \frac{(\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)}{n}
$$

Similarly for the variance terms:

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \sum x_i^2 - \frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n}
$$

$$
\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = \sum y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum y_i)^2}{n}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Raw-Score Formula:**

$$
\boxed{r = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)}{\sqrt{\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right]\left[n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2\right]}}}
$$

*Logic:* This form is preferred for hand calculation because all quantities can be computed directly from raw data without first calculating means.

## 3.2 Proof that $-1 \leq r \leq +1$

We prove the boundedness of $r$ using the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality**, which states that for any two real vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$:

$$
\left(\sum u_i v_i\right)^2 \leq \left(\sum u_i^2\right)\left(\sum v_i^2\right)
$$

Let $u_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ and $v_i = y_i - \bar{y}$. Applying Cauchy-Schwarz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})\right]^2 &\leq \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 \\[0.5em]
\frac{\left[\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})\right]^2}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2} &\leq 1 \\[0.5em]
\left[\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}\right]^2 &\leq 1 \\[0.5em]
r^2 &\leq 1 \\[0.5em]
|r| &\leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $-1 \leq r \leq +1$. **QED.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equality cases:**
> - $r = +1$ occurs when $(y_i - \bar{y}) = k(x_i - \bar{x})$ for some positive constant $k$ (perfect positive linear).
> - $r = -1$ occurs when $(y_i - \bar{y}) = k(x_i - \bar{x})$ for some negative constant $k$ (perfect negative linear).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A quality engineer studies the relationship between **Annealing Temperature (°C)** (X) and **Hardness (HRC)** (Y) for a steel alloy. Data from $n = 5$ batches:

| Batch | $x_i$ (°C) | $y_i$ (HRC) |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | 700 | 42 |
| 2 | 750 | 48 |
| 3 | 800 | 55 |
| 4 | 850 | 60 |
| 5 | 900 | 65 |

**Required:** Compute $r$ and interpret.

### Step 1 — Compute the Required Sums

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\sum x_i$ | $700+750+800+850+900 = 4000$ |
| $\sum y_i$ | $42+48+55+60+65 = 270$ |
| $\sum x_i^2$ | $490000+562500+640000+722500+810000 = 3\,225\,000$ |
| $\sum y_i^2$ | $1764+2304+3025+3600+4225 = 14\,918$ |
| $\sum x_i y_i$ | $(700)(42)+(750)(48)+(800)(55)+(850)(60)+(900)(65) = 29400+36000+44000+51000+58500 = 218\,900$ |

### Step 2 — Apply the Raw-Score Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\sum x_i y_i &= (5)(218\,900) = 1\,094\,500 \\
(\sum x_i)(\sum y_i) &= (4000)(270) = 1\,080\,000 \\
\text{Numerator} &= 1\,094\,500 - 1\,080\,000 = 14\,500
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\sum x_i^2 &= (5)(3\,225\,000) = 16\,125\,000 \\
(\sum x_i)^2 &= (4000)^2 = 16\,000\,000 \\
\text{Term A} &= 16\,125\,000 - 16\,000\,000 = 125\,000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n\sum y_i^2 &= (5)(14\,918) = 74\,590 \\
(\sum y_i)^2 &= (270)^2 = 72\,900 \\
\text{Term B} &= 74\,590 - 72\,900 = 1690
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{14\,500}{\sqrt{(125\,000)(1690)}} = \frac{14\,500}{\sqrt{211\,250\,000}} \\[0.5em]
&= \frac{14\,500}{14\,533.6} \approx 0.9977
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

Since $r \approx 0.998$, which is greater than **0.7**, we have a **strong positive linear correlation**. Hardness increases almost perfectly with annealing temperature in this range.

### Step 4 — Regression Line (Optional but High-Yield)

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{2900}{25\,000} = 0.116 \\[0.5em]
\bar{x} &= 800, \quad \bar{y} = 54 \\[0.5em]
a &= 54 - (0.116)(800) = 54 - 92.8 = -38.8 \\[0.5em]
\hat{y} &= -38.8 + 0.116x
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The regression line can be used to predict the hardness for any annealing temperature within the observed range.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — How a Scatter Diagram is Constructed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["STEP 1\nIDENTIFY PROBLEM"]:::startNode --> node2["STEP 2\nFORM HYPOTHESIS\nDoes X affect Y?"]
    node2 --> node3["STEP 3\nCOLLECT PAIRED DATA\nMinimum n = 30 pairs"]
    node3 --> node4["STEP 4\nASSIGN AXES\nX = Cause on Horizontal\nY = Effect on Vertical"]
    node4 --> node5["STEP 5\nPLOT POINTS\nEach pair is one dot"]
    node5 --> node6["STEP 6\nOBSERVE PATTERN\nShape of cloud"]
    node6 --> decision1{"Pattern Visible?"}
    decision1 -- YES --> node7["STEP 7A\nCOMPUTE r VALUE\nQuantify strength"]
    decision1 -- NO --> node8["STEP 7B\nNO CORRELATION\nr near 0"]
    node7 --> node9["STEP 8\nDRAW BEST FIT LINE\nRegression line"]
    node9 --> node10["STEP 9\nINTERPRET AND DECIDE\nUse for process control"]
    node8 --> node10

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef default fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Pattern Recognition Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["OBSERVE SCATTER CLOUD"] --> nodeB{"Direction of Cloud?"}
    nodeB -- "Slopes Upward" --> nodeC{"Tight or Loose?"}
    nodeB -- "Slopes Downward" --> nodeD{"Tight or Loose?"}
    nodeB -- "Random No Slope" --> nodeE["NO CORRELATION\nr approx 0"]
    nodeB -- "Curved Shape" --> nodeF["NON LINEAR\nUse polynomial fit"]

    nodeC -- "Tight Cluster" --> nodeG["STRONG POSITIVE\nr greater than 0.7"]
    nodeC -- "Loose Cloud" --> nodeH["WEAK POSITIVE\nr between 0 and 0.5"]

    nodeD -- "Tight Cluster" --> nodeI["STRONG NEGATIVE\nr less than minus 0.7"]
    nodeD -- "Loose Cloud" --> nodeJ["WEAK NEGATIVE\nr between minus 0.5 and 0"]

    nodeG --> nodeK["INFERENCE\nCause effect confirmed\nPredict and control"]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeL["INFERENCE\nNo linear relation\nInvestigate other causes"]
    nodeF --> nodeM["INFERENCE\nNon linear relation\nApply transformation"]

    classDef strongPos fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef strongNeg fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nonlinear fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Position of Scatter Diagram in the 7 QC Tools Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sevenQC["7 QC TOOLS KAORU ISHIKAWA"]
        direction TB
        tool1["1. Cause and Effect Diagram\nFishbone"]
        tool2["2. Check Sheet\nData Collection"]
        tool3["3. Control Chart\nProcess Stability"]
        tool4["4. Histogram\nDistribution"]
        tool5["5. Pareto Chart\nPrioritization"]
        tool6["6. SCATTER DIAGRAM\nRELATIONSHIP"]
        tool7["7. Flowchart\nProcess Mapping"]
    end

    phase1["IDENTIFY Problem"] --> tool1
    tool1 --> tool2
    tool2 --> tool3
    tool3 --> tool4
    tool4 --> tool5
    tool5 --> tool6
    tool6 --> tool7
    tool7 --> output["QUALITY DECISION"]

    classDef highlight fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF,font-weight:bold
    class tool6 highlight
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interpretation of r

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["COMPUTED r VALUE"] --> range1{"Range Check\nminus 1 to plus 1"}
    range1 -- "INVALID" --> errorA["ERROR\nRecalculate data"]
    range1 -- "VALID" --> rval{"Magnitude"}

    rval -- "abs r approx 1" --> outA["PERFECT CORRELATION\nDeterministic link\nPredict with certainty"]
    rval -- "0.7 to 1.0" --> outB["STRONG CORRELATION\nReliable prediction\nAct on relationship"]
    rval -- "0.3 to 0.7" --> outC["MODERATE CORRELATION\nLikely relation but noisy\nInvestigate further"]
    rval -- "0.0 to 0.3" --> outD["WEAK CORRELATION\nPoor predictive power\nReject hypothesis"]
    rval -- "r approx 0" --> outE["NO LINEAR CORRELATION\nCheck non linear patterns"]
    rval -- "negative values" --> outF["INVERSE RELATIONSHIP\nIncrease X decreases Y"]

    classDef perfect fill:#00C853,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef strong fill:#76FF03,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef moderate fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef weak fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef none fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class outA,outF perfect
    class outB strong
    class outC moderate
    class outD weak
    class outE none
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define a Scatter Diagram. State any three types of correlation patterns observable in a scatter diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Scatter Diagram** is a graphical quality tool that displays the relationship between two quantitative variables by plotting paired data points, with the independent variable (cause) on the X-axis and the dependent variable (effect) on the Y-axis.

**Three correlation patterns:**
1. **Positive Correlation:** As X increases, Y also increases. The cloud of points slopes upward from lower-left to upper-right.
2. **Negative Correlation:** As X increases, Y decreases. The cloud slopes downward from upper-left to lower-right.
3. **No Correlation:** The points form a random cloud with no discernible trend, indicating the variables are statistically independent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the student mentions *both* variables (X and Y), identifies the axis assignment, and lists at least 3 distinct patterns.

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Distinguish between correlation and causation. Why is this distinction important when interpreting a scatter diagram?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Correlation | Causation |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Definition | Statistical association between two variables | One variable directly produces a change in another |
| Proof Required | Pairwise data observation | Controlled experimentation with isolation of variables |
| Direction | Bidirectional observation | Unidirectional (cause → effect) |
| Proof Tool | Scatter Diagram, Correlation Coefficient | Randomized Controlled Trial, DOE |

**Importance in Scatter Diagram Interpretation:**
A scatter diagram may show a *strong* correlation ($r = 0.9$) between two variables, but this does **not** prove that X causes Y. A **lurking variable** (a hidden third factor) may be driving both. For example, ice-cream sales and drowning deaths are positively correlated, but eating ice-cream does not *cause* drowning — *summer heat* is the lurking variable causing both.

Hence, scatter diagrams should be used as a **preliminary indicator** of relationships, prompting further investigation via designed experiments before any causal claim is made.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for constructing and interpreting a scatter diagram. Discuss any four recognizable correlation patterns with suitable sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data shows the relationship between the **number of hours of quality training (X)** given to employees and the **number of defects per batch (Y)** observed in their work. Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient and interpret the result.

| Employee | Training Hours (X) | Defects (Y) |
|----------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | 10 | 50 |
| 2 | 15 | 45 |
| 3 | 20 | 40 |
| 4 | 25 | 30 |
| 5 | 30 | 25 |
| 6 | 35 | 20 |
| 7 | 40 | 15 |

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Procedure for Constructing a Scatter Diagram:**

1. **Define the Problem:** Clearly identify the two variables suspected of being related. Establish which is the cause (X) and which is the effect (Y).
2. **Collect Paired Data:** Gather at least 30 pairs (xᵢ, yᵢ) of observations under identical conditions to ensure statistical reliability.
3. **Draw the Axes:** Place the independent variable X on the horizontal axis and dependent variable Y on the vertical axis. Label both axes with units and provide equal-scale gridlines.
4. **Plot the Points:** Each pair (xᵢ, yᵢ) is marked as a single dot. Dots are not connected.
5. **Observe the Pattern:** Visually analyze the cloud to determine trend.
6. **Quantify:** Calculate Pearson's $r$ for confirmation.
7. **Draw the Best-Fit Line:** A regression line $\hat{y} = a + bx$ summarizes the trend.
8. **Interpret and Act:** Use findings for process control or further experimentation.

**Four Recognizable Correlation Patterns:**

| Pattern | Description | Correlation Value |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| Strong Positive | Tight upward-sloping cluster; as X rises, Y rises consistently | $r \approx +1$ |
| Strong Negative | Tight downward-sloping cluster; as X rises, Y falls | $r \approx -1$ |
| No Correlation | Random circular cloud; no slope or pattern | $r \approx 0$ |
| Non-Linear (Curvilinear) | Points trace a curve (parabola, exponential); linear fit is inappropriate | $r \approx 0$ but relationship exists |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the Required Sums (n = 7):**

| Quantity | Calculation | Value |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| $\sum x_i$ | $10+15+20+25+30+35+40$ | $175$ |
| $\sum y_i$ | $50+45+40+30+25+20+15$ | $225$ |
| $\sum x_i^2$ | $100+225+400+625+900+1225+1600$ | $5075$ |
| $\sum y_i^2$ | $2500+2025+1600+900+625+400+225$ | $8275$ |
| $\sum x_i y_i$ | $500+675+800+750+750+700+600$ | $4775$ |

**Step 2 — Apply the Pearson Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= n\sum x_i y_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i) \\
&= (7)(4775) - (175)(225) \\
&= 33\,425 - 39\,375 = -5950
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term A} &= n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2 = (7)(5075) - (175)^2 = 35\,525 - 30\,625 = 4900 \\
\text{Term B} &= n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2 = (7)(8275) - (225)^2 = 57\,925 - 50\,625 = 7300
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{-5950}{\sqrt{(4900)(7300)}} = \frac{-5950}{\sqrt{35\,770\,000}} \\[0.5em]
&= \frac{-5950}{5980.80} \approx -0.9949
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Since $r \approx -0.995$, this is a **very strong negative correlation**. As training hours (X) increase, defects per batch (Y) decrease in a near-perfectly linear fashion. The quality management implication is that **investing in employee training dramatically reduces defects**, and the relationship is highly predictable.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** What is a Scatter Diagram? Explain its role as one of the seven tools of quality control. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process engineer studies the effect of **Catalyst Concentration (%)** (X) on **Reaction Yield (%)** (Y). The data obtained is:

| Trial | X (%) | Y (%) |
|-------|--------|--------|
| 1 | 1.0 | 65 |
| 2 | 1.5 | 72 |
| 3 | 2.0 | 78 |
| 4 | 2.5 | 85 |
| 5 | 3.0 | 90 |
| 6 | 3.5 | 93 |
| 7 | 4.0 | 95 |

Compute the Pearson correlation coefficient, draw the regression line equation, and predict the yield at $X = 4.5\%$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** A scatter diagram is a two-dimensional graphical plot of paired (X, Y) data points used to visually and statistically identify the relationship between a cause variable (X) and an effect variable (Y).

**Role in 7 QC Tools:**
It belongs to the family of "**Seven Tools of Quality Control**" codified by Kaoru Ishikawa, alongside Cause-and-Effect Diagrams, Check Sheets, Control Charts, Histograms, Pareto Charts, and Flowcharts. Specifically, the scatter diagram is the **only tool among the seven that examines the relationship *between* two variables**. It is used in the *Analyze* phase of DMAIC and helps verify cause-effect hypotheses.

**Advantages:**
1. **Visual Clarity:** Instantly reveals relationship patterns through visual inspection.
2. **Quantification:** Allows computation of Pearson's $r$ for objective strength measurement.
3. **Simplicity:** Requires no advanced mathematical training to construct and interpret.
4. **Versatility:** Applicable to any two continuous variables across all engineering disciplines.
5. **Predictive Power:** Combined with regression, enables forecasting.

**Limitations:**
1. **Only Two Variables:** Cannot handle multi-variable relationships.
2. **Correlation ≠ Causation:** Strong correlation does not imply causal link.
3. **Outlier Sensitivity:** A few extreme points can distort the visual and statistical impression.
4. **Linearity Bias:** Pearson's $r$ misses non-linear (curvilinear) patterns.
5. **No Time Component:** Cannot show how relationships evolve over time.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute Sums (n = 7):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\sum x_i$ | $1.0+1.5+2.0+2.5+3.0+3.5+4.0 = 17.5$ |
| $\sum y_i$ | $65+72+78+85+90+93+95 = 578$ |
| $\sum x_i^2$ | $1.0+2.25+4.0+6.25+9.0+12.25+16.0 = 50.75$ |
| $\sum y_i^2$ | $4225+5184+6084+7225+8100+8649+9025 = 48\,492$ |
| $\sum x_i y_i$ | $65+108+156+212.5+270+325.5+380 = 1517$ |

**Step 2 — Compute Pearson's r:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (7)(1517) - (17.5)(578) \\
&= 10\,619 - 10\,115 = 504
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term A} &= (7)(50.75) - (17.5)^2 = 355.25 - 306.25 = 49.0 \\
\text{Term B} &= (7)(48\,492) - (578)^2 = 339\,444 - 334\,084 = 5360
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{504}{\sqrt{(49.0)(5360)}} = \frac{504}{\sqrt{262\,640}} \\[0.5em]
&= \frac{504}{512.49} \approx 0.9834
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Regression Line:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= 17.5/7 = 2.5, \quad \bar{y} = 578/7 \approx 82.571 \\
b &= \frac{504}{49} = 10.286 \\
a &= 82.571 - (10.286)(2.5) = 82.571 - 25.714 = 56.857 \\
\hat{y} &= 56.857 + 10.286x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Prediction at X = 4.5%:**

$$
\hat{y} = 56.857 + 10.286(4.5) = 56.857 + 46.286 = 103.14\%
$$

*Note:* A predicted yield exceeding 100% indicates the model is being **extrapolated** beyond the observed data range ($X_{max} = 4.0\%$). The prediction should be treated with caution, and further experimental data at higher concentrations is recommended to validate the model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Axis Inversion:** Students often place the dependent variable on the X-axis. Always: Cause → X-axis, Effect → Y-axis.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** When interpreting, mention the units of both variables (e.g., "training hours" and "defects per batch").
> 3. **Confusing $r$ and $r^2$:** $r^2$ is the *coefficient of determination* (proportion of variance explained), not the correlation strength.
> 4. **Conflating Tools:** A scatter diagram does *not* control a process (that's a control chart) or rank causes (that's a Pareto chart).
> 5. **Missing the n = 30 caveat:** State that conclusions from small samples are unreliable.
> 6. **Ignoring Lurking Variables:** Always caution against inferring causation from correlation alone.
> 7. **Skipping the Equation on the Graph:** When asked to draw, students often forget to write the regression equation $\hat{y} = a + bx$ on the chart — this loses 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Scatter Diagram**

**Core Definition:**
- Graphical tool to study relationship between two variables.
- Part of the **7 QC Tools** (Kaoru Ishikawa).
- X-axis = independent (cause); Y-axis = dependent (effect).

**Key Patterns (must know all 7):**
- Strong positive, weak positive, strong negative, weak negative, no correlation, perfect positive/negative, non-linear (curvilinear).

**Mandatory Formulas:**
- $r = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - (\sum x_i)(\sum y_i)}{\sqrt{\left[n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2\right]\left[n\sum y_i^2 - (\sum y_i)^2\right]}}$
- $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$
- $R^2 = r^2$
- Regression: $\hat{y} = a + bx$ where $b = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$, $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$.

**Critical Numerical Boundaries:**
- $-1 \leq r \leq +1$
- $\vert r \vert \geq 0.7$ → strong; $0.3 < \vert r \vert < 0.7$ → moderate; $\vert r \vert \leq 0.3$ → weak.

**Universal Limitations (always state in answers):**
- Correlation ≠ Causation.
- Outlier sensitivity.
- Only handles two variables.
- Pearson's $r$ only measures linear association.
- Sample size $n \geq 30$ preferred.

**Steps to Draw (memorize the 8-step sequence):**
1. Identify problem → 2. Form hypothesis → 3. Collect pairs (n ≥ 30) → 4. Draw axes → 5. Plot points → 6. Observe pattern → 7. Compute r → 8. Draw regression line & interpret.

**Common Engineering Applications:**
- Cutting speed vs surface roughness (manufacturing).
- Cement content vs compressive strength (civil).
- Temperature vs yield (chemical).
- Training hours vs defects (HR quality).
- Code reviews vs defect density (software quality).

**Last-Minute Exam Pearls:**
- Always state units in axis labels.
- Always mention "lurking variable" caveat when interpreting.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, you must present the *formula table*, the *computed sums table*, the *substituted numerical equation*, and the *interpretation sentence*. Missing any one of these costs marks.
- For non-linear patterns, mention that Pearson's $r$ would falsely indicate "no correlation"; polynomial regression or transformation is required.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a stable (in-control) process to produce output that conforms to the customer's specification limits. It quantifies the relationship between the **natural process spread** (defined by the process standard deviation $\sigma$) and the **engineering specification spread** (defined by the Upper Specification Limit $USL$ and Lower Specification Limit $LSL$).

Mathematically, the most fundamental representation is the **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)**:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

Where the term $6\sigma$ represents the natural process spread (assuming a normal distribution where $99.73\%$ of output falls within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$). The index is dimensionless and unit-free, allowing direct comparison across different processes, products, or industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Process Capability belongs to Module 4 (Statistical Quality Control) of OEIET612. Mastery of $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, and $P_{pk}$ is mandatory for Part A and Part B examination questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** *Capability* refers to the inherent design of the process — it addresses the question *"Can this process ever meet the specifications?"*. *Performance*, in contrast, refers to what the process actually delivered over a specific time window.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **bread baker** in a bakery. The customer's specification is that every bread loaf must weigh between **490 g and 510 g** (a 20 g tolerance window). Your baking process, however, naturally produces loaves whose weights scatter around a mean of 500 g with a standard deviation of 3 g.

- The **specification spread** ($USL - LSL$) = 20 g (what the customer demands).
- The **natural process spread** ($6\sigma$) = $6 \times 3 = 18$ g (what your oven naturally produces).
- Since 18 g < 20 g, your process can *potentially* fit inside the customer's tolerance — your process is **capable**.

Now, if the customer tightens the window to **496 g – 504 g** (only 8 g wide), your $6\sigma = 18$ g no longer fits. Your $C_p$ drops below 1.0, and the process is **not capable**, regardless of how perfectly it is centered. The oven is just too inconsistent for the new, tighter requirements.

This is the essence of process capability: it is the **geometric marriage** between what the process *naturally does* and what the design *needs it to do*.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"Spread vs Tolerance"* — Capability is essentially the ratio *Tolerance ÷ Process Spread*. Higher value = more capable process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Capability Logic Chain

To compute any capability index, the KTU examiner expects you to follow this exact logical sequence:

1. **Establish Statistical Control (Stability)** — Use a control chart (X̄-R or I-MR) to confirm the process is in-control. If a process is not stable, capability indices are mathematically meaningless.
2. **Estimate the Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)** — Either from a control chart ($\bar{R}/d_2$) or directly from sample data ($\sigma = s$).
3. **Compute the Process Mean ($\mu$)** — Average of all individual measurements.
4. **Compare Process Spread to Specification Spread** — Using $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, etc.

## 2.2 The Two Pillars: $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$

### $C_p$ — Process Capability Index (Spread Only)
The $C_p$ index measures the **potential capability** — what the process *could* achieve if it were perfectly centered. It ignores centering entirely.

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Interpretation Rules:**
- $C_p \geq 1.00$ → Process spread fits inside specification (marginally capable).
- $C_p = 1.33$ → Industry minimum acceptable capability.
- $C_p = 1.50$ → Good capability for new processes.
- $C_p = 1.67$ → Excellent capability (historically required for automotive QS-9000).
- $C_p \geq 2.00$ → **Six Sigma** world-class capability.

### $C_{pk}$ — Process Capability Index (Spread + Centering)
The $C_{pk}$ index measures the **actual capability** — accounting for both spread *and* centering of the process relative to the target.

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

The two components are often written as:
- **$C_{PU}$ (Upper Capability)** = $(USL - \mu) / 3\sigma$
- **$C_{PL}$ (Lower Capability)** = $(\mu - LSL) / 3\sigma$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$ Gap:** If $C_p \neq C_{pk}$, the process is capable in *spread* but not *centered*. A re-centering of the mean can dramatically improve $C_{pk}$ without changing $C_p$.

### $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ — Process Performance Indices
These indices use the **overall standard deviation $s$** (computed from all individual data points over a long period) rather than the within-subgroup $\sigma$.

$$P_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6s}$$

$$P_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3s},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3s}\right)$$

They capture **long-term process performance**, including shifts, drifts, and assignable causes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | All in same units | Measures potential capability (spread only) |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL})$ | Min of two values | Measures actual capability (spread + centering) |
| 3 | $C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided metric | Distance from mean to upper limit, in $\sigma$ units |
| 4 | $C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided metric | Distance from mean to lower limit, in $\sigma$ units |
| 5 | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | $s$ = overall std. dev. | Long-term performance index |
| 6 | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3s},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Long-term indices | Actual long-term capability |
| 7 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | $d_2$ depends on $n$ | Estimate $\sigma$ from control chart range |
| 8 | $Z_{USL} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standard normal variable | Distance to USL in $\sigma$ units |
| 9 | $Z_{LSL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}$ | Standard normal variable | Distance to LSL in $\sigma$ units |
| 10 | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million opportunities | Motorola Six Sigma metric |
| 11 | $\text{Yield \%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^6}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage | % of conforming units |
| 12 | $\text{Sigma Level} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{2\sigma} + 1.5\sigma$ | Six Sigma with 1.5$\sigma$ shift | Motorola Six Sigma methodology |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (Automotive, Aerospace):** Supplier quality audits universally require $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$. Critical characteristics (safety items) often require $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$.
- **Semiconductor Industry:** Six Sigma demands $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$, equivalent to $3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
- **Healthcare/Pharmaceutical:** Process capability indices are part of FDA Process Validation guidelines.
- **Service Industries:** Adapted for call center wait times, transaction processing accuracy, and delivery timeliness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $C_p$ from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define the Process Distribution
Assume the process output $X$ follows a normal distribution:

$$X \sim N(\mu,\ \sigma^2)$$

By the empirical rule ($68.2-95.4-99.73$ rule), nearly all observations lie within:

$$\mu \pm 3\sigma$$

This $6\sigma$ band is called the **natural process spread** or **Voice of the Process (VOP)**.

### Step 2 — Define the Specification Window
The customer defines:

$$\text{USL} = \text{Upper Specification Limit}, \quad \text{LSL} = \text{Lower Specification Limit}$$

This band, $\text{USL} - \text{LSL}$, is the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — the tolerance window the design requires.

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio
The capability index is the ratio of allowable spread to actual spread:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{VOC}}{\text{VOP}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

This derivation is intentionally simple — the elegance of $C_p$ lies in its dimensionless nature (it cancels any unit system).

## 3.2 Derivation of $C_{pk}$

### Step 1 — Recognize Centering Failure
$C_p$ assumes $\mu$ is exactly at the midpoint of the specification:

$$\mu_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}$$

When the mean drifts, $C_p$ still reports the same value — *the spread has not changed* — even though defect rates explode. To fix this, we introduce one-sided indices.

### Step 2 — Upper One-Sided Capability
The distance from the process mean to the upper specification, expressed in units of $3\sigma$:

$$C_{PU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

### Step 3 — Lower One-Sided Capability
The distance from the lower specification to the process mean, in $3\sigma$ units:

$$C_{PL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

### Step 4 — Take the Worst Case
The true capability is limited by the *nearer* specification limit:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL}) \\[4pt]
&= \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Relationship to $C_p$
A useful identity:

$$C_{pk} = C_p \cdot (1 - k)$$

where $k$ is the **centering index**:

$$k = \frac{\vert \text{Target} - \mu \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2}$$

Note: All vertical bars are written using `\vert` syntax to maintain LaTeX/markdown safety.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Exhaustive Solution)

### Problem Statement
A CNC turning process produces shafts with the following data:
- $USL = 24.50$ mm, $LSL = 23.50$ mm
- Process mean $\mu = 24.10$ mm
- Within-subgroup standard deviation $\sigma = 0.10$ mm
- Subgroup size $n = 5$, with sample range $\bar{R} = 0.20$ mm

**Required:** Compute $C_p$, $C_{PU}$, $C_{PL}$, $C_{pk}$, and interpret.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify $\sigma$ via control chart constant**
For $n = 5$, the standard $d_2 = 2.326$:

$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.20}{2.326} = 0.0860\ \text{mm}$$

The problem provides $\sigma = 0.10$ mm — we use this value as the official estimate.

**Step 2 — Compute Specification Spread**

$$USL - LSL = 24.50 - 23.50 = 1.00\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_p$**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.00}{6 \times 0.10} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.00}{0.60} \\[4pt]
&= 1.667
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute One-Sided Indices**

$$C_{PU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.50 - 24.10}{3 \times 0.10} = \frac{0.40}{0.30} = 1.333$$

$$C_{PL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.10 - 23.50}{3 \times 0.10} = \frac{0.60}{0.30} = 2.000$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{pk}$**

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL}) = \min(1.333,\ 2.000) = 1.333$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**
- $C_p = 1.667$ → Process spread is excellent (well above $1.33$ minimum).
- $C_{pk} = 1.333$ → Process meets industry minimum, but is **off-center toward USL**.
- The limiting factor is the upper specification — the mean is closer to $USL$ than to $LSL$.
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process downward to $\mu \approx 24.00$ mm; $C_{pk}$ would then rise toward $C_p = 1.667$.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ | Diagnosis | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1.667$ | $1.667$ | Capable and centered | Maintain control |
| 2 | $1.667$ | $1.333$ | Capable, off-center toward USL | Re-center downward |
| 3 | $1.667$ | $0.833$ | Capable, severely off-center | Re-center urgently |
| 4 | $1.000$ | $1.000$ | Marginal capability, centered | Reduce variation |
| 5 | $0.800$ | $0.500$ | Not capable, off-center | Redesign process |
| 6 | $0.800$ | $0.800$ | Not capable, centered | Major variation reduction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Capability Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Capability Study] --> B[Step 1 Collect Data]
    B --> C[Step 2 Verify Stability Control Chart]
    C --> D{Process In Control?}
    D -- No --> E[Remove Assignable Causes SPC]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Step 3 Estimate sigma within subgroup]
    F --> G[Step 4 Compute Mean mu]
    G --> H[Step 5 Calculate Cp]
    H --> I[Step 6 Calculate Cpk]
    I --> J{Cpk is 1.33 or greater}
    J -- Yes --> K[Process Capable Document]
    J -- No --> L[Step 7 Diagnose Centering or Spread]
    L --> M{Re centering Possible}
    M -- Yes --> N[Adjust Mean]
    M -- No --> O[Step 8 Reduce Variation DMAIC]
    O --> F
    N --> F
    K --> P[Step 9 Calculate DPMO and Yield]
    P --> Q[End Report Capability]
```

## 4.2 Capability Scenarios Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Scenario A Centered and Capable]
        A1[mu centered Cp high Cpk equals Cp]
    end
    subgraph S2[Scenario B Off Center Capable]
        B1[mu shifted toward USL Cp high Cpk less than Cp]
    end
    subgraph S3[Scenario C Not Capable Centered]
        C1[sigma too large Cp less than 1 Cpk equals Cp]
    end
    subgraph S4[Scenario D Not Capable Off Center]
        D1[Both problems Cp less than 1 Cpk less than Cp]
    end
    S1 --> X[Outcome Acceptable]
    S2 --> Y[Re center]
    S3 --> Z[Reduce Variation]
    S4 --> W[Redesign Process]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Mapping (Input → Process → Output → Capability)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In1[Raw Material] --> Proc[Machining Process]
    In2[Machine Settings] --> Proc
    In3[Operator Skill] --> Proc
    In4[Environmental Conditions] --> Proc
    Proc --> Out1[Output Distribution]
    Out1 --> Stat[Compute Mean and sigma]
    Stat --> Comp[Compare with USL and LSL]
    Comp --> Cp[Cp Index]
    Comp --> Cpk[Cpk Index]
    Comp --> DPMO[Defects per Million]
    Cp --> Dec[Capability Decision]
    Cpk --> Dec
    DPMO --> Dec
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each scenario in the block diagram above maps directly to a row in the analytical decision matrix from Section 3.4. Students should be able to draw and label these scenarios in the examination for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Process Capability. Explain the significance of $C_{pk}$ over $C_p$.**

**Model Answer:**
Process capability is a statistical measure of the ability of a stable process to produce output within the customer's specification limits. It is quantified using capability indices.

$C_p$ measures only the **potential capability** — the ratio of specification spread to natural process spread. It assumes the process is perfectly centered and ignores any mean shift.

$C_{pk}$ measures the **actual capability** — accounting for both the process spread *and* the location of the process mean. It is calculated as the minimum of the one-sided upper and lower capabilities. Therefore, $C_{pk}$ provides a more realistic and conservative picture of process performance, making it the preferred index in industry and KTU examinations.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between $C_p / C_{pk}$ and $P_p / P_{pk}$?**

**Model Answer:**
The indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ use the **within-subgroup standard deviation** ($\sigma$, often estimated via $\bar{R}/d_2$ from control charts), reflecting **short-term inherent capability** of a stable process.

In contrast, $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ use the **overall standard deviation** ($s$, computed from all individual data points over time), capturing **long-term performance** that includes process shifts, drifts, and assignable causes.

In practice, $P_{pk}$ is always $\leq C_{pk}$. If $C_{pk} \gg P_{pk}$, the process is statistically stable but drifting over the long term — a signal that SPC control charts need reinforcement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a)** A packaging machine fills bags with nominal 500 g of rice. The specification limits are $USL = 515$ g and $LSL = 485$ g. From a stable in-control process, 25 samples of size 5 each were collected. The grand average is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 502$ g and the average range is $\bar{R} = 4.0$ g. For $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{PU}$, $C_{PL}$, and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the capability. (7 marks)

**b)** Explain the concept of Six Sigma with reference to DPMO. A process operates at a $4.5\sigma$ quality level. Calculate the DPMO assuming a 1.5$\sigma$ shift, and the corresponding rolled throughput yield for 4 sequential processes. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Estimate $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{4.0}{2.326} = 1.720\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{515 - 485}{6 \times 1.720} = \frac{30}{10.32} = 2.907$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{PU}$:**

$$C_{PU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{515 - 502}{3 \times 1.720} = \frac{13}{5.16} = 2.519$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{PL}$:**

$$C_{PL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{502 - 485}{3 \times 1.720} = \frac{17}{5.16} = 3.295$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL}) = \min(2.519,\ 3.295) = 2.519$$

**Comment:** The process exhibits excellent capability ($C_p = 2.907$). However, it is slightly off-center toward the USL ($C_{PU} = 2.519$ is the limiting value). The process is capable but a small downward re-centering (target $\mu = 500$ g) would maximize $C_{pk}$.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Six Sigma Definition:**
Six Sigma is a quality management methodology that seeks to reduce process defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It is equivalent to a $C_{pk}$ of $2.00$ and corresponds to a process operating at $6\sigma$ with a $1.5\sigma$ allowance for long-term mean shift.

**Step 2 — DPMO at $4.5\sigma$:**
At $4.5\sigma$ with a $1.5\sigma$ shift, the effective distance to the nearest specification is $4.5 - 1.5 = 3.0\sigma$.

From standard normal tables, the area beyond $3.0\sigma$ (one tail) $\approx 0.00135$, or $1350$ ppm. For two-sided:

$$DPMO \approx 2 \times 1350 = 2700\ \text{defects per million opportunities}$$

**Step 3 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for 4 Processes:**
Each process has yield $Y_i = 1 - 2700/10^6 = 0.99730$.

$$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{4} Y_i = (0.99730)^4 = 0.98923 = 98.92\%$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the difference between $C_p = 1.00$, $C_p = 1.33$, and $C_p = 1.67$. (7 marks)

**b)** A precision grinding process has $USL = 30.020$ mm, $LSL = 29.970$ mm. Twenty-five subgroups of size 4 gave $\bar{\bar{X}} = 29.995$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.012$ mm. For $n = 4$, $d_2 = 2.059$. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. If the process mean shifts to $30.005$ mm due to tool wear, calculate the new $C_{pk}$ and DPMO. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to (a)

**Sketch Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

Three normal-distribution curves overlaid on the same specification window:

1. **$C_p = 1.00$:** The $6\sigma$ spread exactly equals $USL - LSL$. Curves just touch both limits. **Marginal capability** — any shift produces defects.

2. **$C_p = 1.33$:** The $6\sigma$ spread occupies $75\%$ of the specification window ($6\sigma / 8\sigma = 0.75$). There is a $1\sigma$ buffer on each side. **Industry minimum acceptable** capability.

3. **$C_p = 1.67$:** The $6\sigma$ spread occupies only $60\%$ of the specification window ($6\sigma / 10\sigma = 0.60$). There is a $2\sigma$ buffer on each side. **Preferred** capability for new processes; required for safety-critical characteristics.

**Verbal explanation:** $C_p$ increases as the natural process variation decreases relative to the specification width. Higher $C_p$ implies greater robustness against process shifts and is the hallmark of a well-designed, mature process.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Estimate $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.012}{2.059} = 0.005828\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_p$ (mean = 29.995 mm):**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{30.020 - 29.970}{6 \times 0.005828} = \frac{0.050}{0.03497} = 1.430$$

**Step 3 — Compute one-sided indices:**

$$C_{PU} = \frac{30.020 - 29.995}{3 \times 0.005828} = \frac{0.025}{0.01748} = 1.430$$

$$C_{PL} = \frac{29.995 - 29.970}{3 \times 0.005828} = \frac{0.025}{0.01748} = 1.430$$

(Process is well-centered; $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.430$.)

**Step 4 — New $C_{pk}$ with mean shift to $30.005$ mm:**

$$C_{PU}^{\text{new}} = \frac{30.020 - 30.005}{0.01748} = \frac{0.015}{0.01748} = 0.858$$

$$C_{PL}^{\text{new}} = \frac{30.005 - 29.970}{0.01748} = \frac{0.035}{0.01748} = 2.002$$

$$C_{pk}^{\text{new}} = \min(0.858,\ 2.002) = 0.858$$

**Step 5 — DPMO Calculation:**

The limiting side is now the upper specification. Compute the $Z$-score:

$$Z_{USL} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{30.020 - 30.005}{0.005828} = 2.574$$

From standard normal tables, area beyond $Z = 2.574$ (one tail) $\approx 0.00503$.

$$\text{DPMO} = 2 \times 0.00503 \times 10^6 \approx 10{,}060\ \text{defects per million}$$

(Note: Assuming two-sided risk; if the question expects only the upper tail, DPMO $\approx 5{,}030$.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $d_2$:** When $\sigma$ is not given directly, examiners expect $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$. Marks are deducted for using $\bar{R}$ as if it were $\sigma$.
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing mm with cm or grams with kg in $C_p$ calculation is a guaranteed zero. Always verify all quantities are in identical units.
> 3. **Misreporting $C_{pk}$:** Students often compute only $C_{PU}$ or only $C_{PL}$ and call it $C_{pk}$. The full procedure — *both* one-sided values *plus* the minimum — must be shown.
> 4. **Ignoring Stability Precondition:** Capability indices assume statistical control. If the question describes an unstable process, indices must be rejected — this is a 2-mark deduction item in KTU valuations.
> 5. **$\sigma$ vs $s$ Mix-up:** For $C_p / C_{pk}$, use within-subgroup $\sigma$; for $P_p / P_{pk}$, use overall $s$. Confusing the two loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process Capability** quantifies the inherent ability of a process to meet specifications.
- The two foundational indices are $C_p$ (spread only) and $C_{pk}$ (spread + centering).
- $C_p$ is calculated using $USL - LSL$ divided by $6\sigma$, where $\sigma$ is the within-subgroup standard deviation.
- $C_{pk}$ is the minimum of $C_{PU}$ and $C_{PL}$, capturing real-world off-centering.
- $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ use overall standard deviation $s$ for long-term performance evaluation.
- A $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the universally accepted minimum capability benchmark; $C_p \geq 1.67$ is preferred.
- $C_{pk} = C_p$ implies a perfectly centered process; $C_{pk} \ll C_p$ implies an off-center process needing recentering.
- $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$ is the KTU-expected estimator from X̄-R control charts.
- Six Sigma demands $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$, equivalent to $3.4$ DPMO with a $1.5\sigma$ long-term shift allowance.
- Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) is the product of yields across multiple sequential process stages.
- Capability analysis must always follow a stability check (control chart) — capability is undefined for unstable processes.
- For $C_p$ calculations, ensure all numerical values share identical units before substitution.
- The Six Sigma 1.5$\sigma$ shift is a Motorola convention, not a statistical theorem — examiners may ask its rationale.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Productive Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Total Productive Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is a **holistic, company-wide, proactive and preventive maintenance philosophy** that aims to maximize **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** by eliminating breakdowns, defects, accidents, and waste through the **participation of all employees** — from top management to shop-floor operators.

TPM was first formalized in **1971** by **Seiichi Nakajima** at **Nippondenso** (a Toyota Group company). It evolved from **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** into a system that integrates maintenance with manufacturing, quality, safety, and administration to create a **"Zero Loss"** culture.

The **objective** of TPM can be summarized in five zeros (the **5-Zero Goal**):

| Zero Loss Target | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Zero Defects** | No defective products leave the process |
| **Zero Breakdown** | No unplanned equipment stoppages |
| **Zero Accidents** | Safe working environment for all |
| **Zero Pollution** | No environmental contamination |
| **Zero Waste** | Optimal use of materials, time, and energy |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine your body is a piece of industrial equipment.

- You brush your teeth daily, eat healthy food, and exercise — these are **preventive self-maintenance** (similar to **Autonomous Maintenance / Jishu Hozen** in TPM).
- You visit a doctor for annual check-ups — this is **Planned Maintenance**.
- If your back pains daily because your chair is broken, you fix the chair instead of taking painkillers — this is **Root-Cause Improvement** (the philosophy of *Poka-Yoke* and *Why-Why Analysis* in TPM).

> [!NOTE]
> **In TPM, the operator owns the machine.** Just as you know your own body better than anyone else, the operator who runs the machine daily knows its "voice," vibration, and behaviour best. TPM empowers that operator to clean, inspect, lubricate, and tighten (the **CILT** activities) before a technician is even called.

## 1.3 Why TPM Matters in KTU Context

Under the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, this topic directly supports:

- **CO4:** *Apply modern maintenance and quality tools for industrial performance improvement.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Big Losses (核心の六大損失)

TPM classifies every production loss into **Six Big Losses**, which are grouped under three macro-categories that form the **OEE multiplier chain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Six Big Losses** are the backbone of any OEE-related KTU numerical question. Memorize the mapping between loss type and OEE component.

| OEE Component | Six Big Loss Category | Loss Type | Typical Cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Availability** | 1. **Breakdown Loss** | Equipment Failure | Component wear, sudden breakdown |
| **Availability** | 2. **Setup / Adjustment Loss** | Changeover | Die change, model changeover |
| **Performance** | 3. **Small Stops / Idling Loss** | Minor Stoppages | Sensor jam, temporary blockage |
| **Performance** | 4. **Reduced Speed Loss** | Speed Reduction | Running below designed cycle time |
| **Quality** | 5. **Defect / Rework Loss** | Process Defects | Scrap and rework during steady-state |
| **Quality** | 6. **Startup / Yield Loss** | Reduced Yield | Defects during warm-up / start-up |

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of OEE Computation

$$OEE = Availability \times Performance \times Quality$$

Where each factor is bounded by a **world-class benchmark** and a typical industrial value.

$$OEE_{World\text{-}Class} \geq 85\%$$

## 2.3 Reliability & Maintainability Metrics

These two metrics are asked repeatedly in KTU university exams under Module 4.

**Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — measures *reliability*:

$$MTBF = \frac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$$

**Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** — measures *maintainability*:

$$MTTR = \frac{Total\ Downtime\ for\ Repairs}{Number\ of\ Failures}$$

**Inherent Availability (AI)** — the bridge between MTBF and MTTR:

$$A_I = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> For every KTU Part B question on TPM, you must derive at least **one** of the following. Note the use of `\vert` instead of literal pipes so the markdown table does not break.

| \textbf{Symbol / Term} | \textbf{Formula} | \textbf{Units / Range} | \textbf{Engineering Meaning} |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Availability (A) | $\dfrac{Operating\ Time}{Planned\ Production\ Time} \times 100$ | \% (0–100) | Time the equipment is ready vs. scheduled |
| Performance (P) | $\dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Pieces}{Operating\ Time} \times 100$ | \% (0–100) | Speed efficiency vs. design speed |
| Quality (Q) | $\dfrac{Good\ Pieces}{Total\ Pieces} \times 100$ | \% (0–100) | First-pass yield |
| OEE | $A \times P \times Q \div 10000$ | \% (0–100) | Overall Equipment Effectiveness |
| MTBF | $\dfrac{Total\ Uptime}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Reliability indicator |
| MTTR | $\dfrac{Total\ Repair\ Time}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Maintainability indicator |
| Inherent Availability | $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | ratio (0–1) | Intrinsic uptime potential |
| TEEP | $OEE \times \dfrac{Loading\ Time}{Calendar\ Time}$ | \% | Total Effective Equipment Performance |

## 2.5 The 8 Pillars of TPM (八大支柱)

TPM is implemented through **8 Pillars**, each addressing a specific loss domain:

1. **Pillar 1 – Jishu Hozen (Autonomous Maintenance):** Operators do daily CILT — *Cleaning, Inspection, Lubrication, Tightening*.
2. **Pillar 2 – Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement):** Cross-functional teams eliminate specific big losses using **DMAIC / PDCA**.
3. **Pillar 3 – Planned Maintenance:** Time-based, condition-based, and predictive maintenance schedules.
4. **Pillar 4 – Quality Maintenance:** *Poka-Yoke* (mistake-proofing), *5-Why*, root-cause analysis to achieve **Zero Defects**.
5. **Pillar 5 – Early Equipment Management:** Design new equipment for *easy maintenance, safety, and reliability* from Day 1.
6. **Pillar 6 – Training & Education:** Build multi-skilled operators and engineers.
7. **Pillar 7 – Safety, Health & Environment:** Eliminate unsafe conditions; link directly to **5-Zero Goal**.
8. **Pillar 8 – TPM in Administration:** Extend TPM philosophy to office, stores, and supply chain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — OEE Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Most Module 4 numericals are *OEE + MTBF/MTTR* combined questions worth 7–14 marks. Always show **units** and **final % conversion**.

### **Problem Statement**

A CNC machine in a Kerala-based precision components factory has the following data for one shift (8 hours = 480 minutes):

- Planned production time = 480 min
- Two breakdowns: 35 min and 25 min
- One changeover: 20 min
- Ideal cycle time per piece = 1.20 min
- Total pieces produced = 320
- Defective pieces = 18

Calculate: **(a) Availability, (b) Performance, (c) Quality, (d) OEE, (e) Comment on world-class status.**

### **Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step**

**Step 1: Compute Operating Time (T_op).**

Operating Time = Planned Production Time − All Downtime (breakdowns + changeovers)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{op} &= 480 - (35 + 25 + 20) \\
T_{op} &= 480 - 80 \\
T_{op} &= 400 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Availability (A).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{T_{op}}{Planned\ Production\ Time} \times 100 \\
A &= \frac{400}{480} \times 100 \\
A &= 83.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Performance (P).**

Ideal production at design speed = Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ideal} &= 1.20 \times 320 \\
T_{ideal} &= 384 \text{ minutes} \\
P &= \frac{T_{ideal}}{T_{op}} \times 100 \\
P &= \frac{384}{400} \times 100 \\
P &= 96.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Quality (Q).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{Good\ Pieces}{Total\ Pieces} \times 100 \\
Q &= \frac{320 - 18}{320} \times 100 \\
Q &= \frac{302}{320} \times 100 \\
Q &= 94.38\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute OEE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \div 10000 \\
OEE &= \frac{83.33 \times 96.00 \times 94.38}{10000} \times 100 \\
OEE &= 75.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **World-Class OEE = 85%.** This machine is operating at **75.50%**, indicating substantial improvement potential. The lowest pillar is **Availability (83.33%)**, suggesting that **breakdown and changeover losses** must be attacked via Pillar 2 (Kobetsu Kaizen) and Pillar 3 (Planned Maintenance).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — MTBF, MTTR, and Inherent Availability

A packaging machine operated for **5,000 hours** in one quarter and experienced **6 failures**, with cumulative repair time of **30 hours**.

### **Solution**

**Step 1: MTBF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures} \\
MTBF &= \frac{5000}{6} \\
MTBF &= 833.33 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: MTTR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \frac{Total\ Repair\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures} \\
MTTR &= \frac{30}{6} \\
MTTR &= 5.00 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Inherent Availability (AI).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_I &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
A_I &= \frac{833.33}{833.33 + 5.00} \\
A_I &= \frac{833.33}{838.33} \\
A_I &= 0.9940 \text{ or } 99.40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** High MTBF (833 hrs) and low MTTR (5 hrs) indicate the equipment is both **highly reliable** and **easily maintainable**. The very high AI of 99.40% reflects excellent design + maintenance culture.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right TPM Pillar

| Symptom Observed on Shop-Floor | Primary Pillar to Deploy | Supporting Tool |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequent unplanned stoppages | Pillar 3 – Planned Maintenance | MTBF, MTTR tracking |
| Operators wait for technicians | Pillar 1 – Jishu Hozen | CILT, Tag boards |
| High scrap at start-up of shift | Pillar 4 – Quality Maintenance | Poka-Yoke, 5-Why |
| Slow model changeovers (SMED) | Pillar 2 – Kobetsu Kaizen | SMED, VSM |
| New machine design is hard to maintain | Pillar 5 – Early Equipment Mgmt. | Maintainability indices |
| Operators lack technical depth | Pillar 6 – Training & Education | OJT matrix |
| Unsafe working conditions | Pillar 7 – SHE | Risk assessment |
| Office delays bottleneck production | Pillar 8 – Admin TPM | Value Stream Mapping |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Diagram — The 8 Pillars of TPM Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the **hierarchical block-level architecture** of TPM, showing how each pillar links to either *Equipment Effectiveness* (OEE) or *Zero-Defect / Safe Culture*.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Total Productive Maintenance TPM] --> goal1[Five Zero Goals]
    rootA --> goal2[Maximum OEE]
    rootA --> goal3[Operator Ownership]

    pilA --> toolA2[Tag Board and One Point Lessons]

    toolC2 -.Uses.-> predA[Vibration Analysis]
    toolC2 -.Uses.-> predB[Thermal Imaging]

```

## 4.2 The Six Big Losses — Loss-to-Pillar Mapping

```mermaid

    oeeRoot[OEE Multiplier Chain] --> avL[Availability]
    oeeRoot --> pfL[Performance]
    oeeRoot --> qaL[Quality]

    avL --> lossA2[Loss 2 Setup and Adjustment]

    pfL --> lossP1[Loss 3 Small Stops and Idling]

    qaL --> lossQ1[Loss 5 Defect and Rework]
    qaL --> lossQ2[Loss 6 Startup and Reduced Yield]

    lossA1 --> actA1[Deploy Pillar 3 Planned Maintenance]

    lossP2 --> actP2[Deploy Pillar 5 Early Equipment Management]

    lossQ2 --> actQ2[Deploy Pillar 6 Training and Standard Work]
```

    step1[Step 1 Initial Cleaning] --> step2[Step 2 Counter Measures for Sources]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Tentative TPM Standards]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 General Inspection]

    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Autonomous Maintenance Management]
