
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) and list its 5 Zero goals.**

**Model Answer:**
TPM is a company-wide, proactive maintenance philosophy that maximizes OEE by involving **all employees** from operators to top management in maintaining equipment. The **5 Zero Goals** are:
1. **Zero** Defects (Quality)
2. **Zero** Breakdowns (Equipment)
3. **Zero** Accidents (Safety)
4. **Zero** Pollution (Environment)
5. **Zero** Waste (Efficiency)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "less defects" or "minimum breakdowns." Use the **exact word "Zero"** — examiners award the mark only when zero-loss language is used.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Total Productive Maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scope | Equipment only | Entire organization (including office) |
| Participation | Maintenance department only | All employees (operators included) |
| Focus | Avoiding breakdown | Maximizing OEE and achieving 5 Zero |
| Tools | Schedule, lubrication | CILT, OEE, Poka-Yoke, SMED |
| Origin | US Industry | Japan, 1971 (Nippondenso) |

---

## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme regulation**, every Module Part B offers **internal choice**. Attempt ANY ONE of the two options.

---

### ✅ **OPTION A — 14 Marks**

#### **Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the concept of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). Derive the formula and identify its three sub-components, classifying the Six Big Losses into each.**

**Model Solution:**

OEE is a **composite metric** that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized, relative to its true potential. It was popularized within the TPM framework by Seiichi Nakajima.

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= Availability \times Performance \times Quality \\
    &= A \times P \times Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification of Six Big Losses into OEE components:**

- **Availability Losses:** (1) Equipment Failure / Breakdown Loss, (2) Setup & Adjustment Loss.
- **Performance Losses:** (3) Small Stops / Idling, (4) Reduced Speed Loss.
- **Quality Losses:** (5) Defect / Rework Loss, (6) Startup / Reduced Yield Loss.

**World-Class Benchmark:** $OEE \geq 85\%$ — corresponding to $A \geq 90\%$, $P \geq 95\%$, $Q \geq 99\%$.

---

#### **Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]**
**A bottling plant operates two shifts of 8 hours each. Planned downtime = 1 hour per shift. The line produced 14,000 bottles in the day. Ideal cycle time = 3.6 seconds. Defective bottles = 350. Compute Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE. State whether it is world-class.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Planned Production Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{plan} &= (8 \times 2 \times 60) - (1 \times 2 \times 60) \\
         &= 960 - 120 \\
         &= 840 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Performance factor (no break/changeover specified → assume operating time = 840 min for maximum marks, state assumption).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ideal} &= 14{,}000 \times 3.6\ \text{sec} \\
         &= 50{,}400\ \text{sec} \\
         &= 840 \text{ minutes} \\
P &= \frac{T_{ideal}}{T_{op}} \times 100 \\
P &= \frac{840}{840} \times 100 \\
P &= 100.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*(If exam gives any unplanned downtime, deduct from 840 min accordingly.)*

**Step 3 — Quality.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{14{,}000 - 350}{14{,}000} \times 100 \\
Q &= \frac{13{,}650}{14{,}000} \times 100 \\
Q &= 97.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — OEE (assuming A = 100% if no breakdown data).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= 100 \times 100 \times 97.50 \div 10{,}000 \\
OEE &= 97.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The line is **world-class** since $OEE \geq 85\%$. However, Quality must be improved (target $\geq 99\%$) to attain *true* world-class status.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Trap):**
> 1. Many students forget to convert **seconds → minutes** for ideal cycle time. Award 0 if units are inconsistent.
> 2. Always **state your assumption** ("assuming zero breakdowns") — examiners reward it.
> 3. Do NOT write "it is good." Write the **numeric comparison** with the 85% benchmark.

---

### ✅ **OPTION B — 14 Marks**

#### **Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]**
**Define MTBF and MTTR. A machine was operated for 6,000 hours and failed 8 times. Total downtime for repair was 56 hours. Calculate MTBF, MTTR, and Inherent Availability. Comment on the maintenance effectiveness.**

**Model Solution:**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average time the equipment performs its intended function between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time taken to restore the equipment to working condition after a failure.

**Step 1 — MTBF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures} \\
     &= \frac{6000}{8} \\
     &= 750\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — MTTR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \frac{Total\ Repair\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures} \\
     &= \frac{56}{8} \\
     &= 7\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Inherent Availability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_I &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
    &= \frac{750}{750 + 7} \\
    &= \frac{750}{757} \\
    &= 0.9908 \\
    &\approx 99.08\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** An AI of **99.08%** is excellent. However, **MTTR of 7 hours** suggests the maintenance crew can improve further through **Predictive Maintenance (Pillar 3)** and **Training (Pillar 6)**. The breakdown frequency is also a concern — focus on **root-cause analysis (5-Why)** under **Pillar 4**.

---

#### **Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the 8 Pillars of TPM with focus on Jishu Hozen and Kobetsu Kaizen.**

**Model Solution:**

TPM is implemented through **8 interconnected pillars**. The **foundation pillars** (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7) and the **extension pillar** (8) together drive the **5 Zero Goals**.

**1. Jishu Hozen (Pillar 1 – Autonomous Maintenance):**
Empowers the **operator** to own the equipment. Activities follow the famous **CILT** framework:
- **C**lean the machine
- **I**nspect while cleaning
- **L**ubricate as per standard
- **T**ighten loose parts

Implemented in **7 standardized steps** — initial cleaning → countermeasures → TPM standards → general inspection → autonomous inspection standards → management → rollout.

**2. Kobetsu Kaizen (Pillar 2 – Focused Improvement):**
Cross-functional teams use **PDCA / DMAIC** to eliminate the *single biggest loss* identified through OEE analysis. A classic tool is **SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die)** to attack Setup Losses. This pillar directly improves **Performance × Quality**.

**Remaining 6 Pillars:** Planned Maintenance (3), Quality Maintenance (4), Early Equipment Mgmt (5), Training (6), SHE (7), and Admin TPM (8).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list the pillars but **fail to explain the linkage with OEE components**. Always map at least **2 pillars** to the specific OEE loss they attack. Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for this linkage.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam (Module 4).**

- ⭐ **TPM Origin:** Nippon Denso, 1971, **Seiichi Nakajima**, evolved from Preventive Maintenance.
- ⭐ **5 Zero Goals:** Defects, Breakdown, Accidents, Pollution, Waste.
- ⭐ **OEE Formula:** $A \times P \times Q$, world-class = **85%** (i.e., $A \ge 90\%$, $P \ge 95\%$, $Q \ge 99\%$).
- ⭐ **Six Big Losses:** Breakdown, Setup, Small Stops, Reduced Speed, Defects, Startup Yield — map to A, A, P, P, Q, Q respectively.
- ⭐ **MTBF** = Uptime / Failures; **MTTR** = Repair Time / Failures; **AI** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- ⭐ **8 Pillars:** Jishu Hozen, Kobetsu Kaizen, Planned Maintenance, Quality Maintenance, Early Equipment Mgmt, Training, SHE, Admin TPM.
- ⭐ **CILT** = Cleaning, Inspection, Lubrication, Tightening — the operator's daily ritual.
- ⭐ **Poka-Yoke** and **5-Why Analysis** are the core *Quality Maintenance* (Pillar 4) tools.
- ⭐ **SMED** = Single Minute Exchange of Die — used to reduce Setup Losses under Kobetsu Kaizen.
- ⭐ Always **state assumptions** and **convert units** in numericals — failing to do so costs 2–3 marks.
- ⭐ **TEEP** (Total Effective Equipment Performance) considers calendar time and is even stricter than OEE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive, and structured group technique used to identify and evaluate the potential failure modes of a product, process, or service, and to determine the relative impact (effect) of each failure on system performance, with the objective of prioritizing corrective actions to reduce risk.

The methodology was first formally documented in the **U.S. military procedure MIL-P-1629 (1949)** and is now standardized globally through **AIAG-VDA (2019)**, **IEC 60812**, and **SAE J1739**. In the KTU 2024 scheme, FMEA is positioned as a cornerstone of the *Prevention* pillar of quality management, complementing tools like SPC, Pareto analysis, and Ishikawa diagrams.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Failure Mode** | The manner in which a component, subsystem, or process could potentially fail to perform its intended function. |
| **Effect** | The consequence of the failure mode on the system, customer, or downstream operation. |
| **Cause** | The root reason the failure mode occurs (mechanism of failure). |
| **Severity (S)** | A numerical rating of the seriousness of the effect of a failure mode to the next assembly, the end user, or the environment. |
| **Occurrence (O)** | A numerical rating of the likelihood that the failure mode will occur. |
| **Detection (D)** | A numerical rating of the ability of the current process control to detect the failure mode before it reaches the customer. |
| **RPN** | Risk Priority Number — a composite metric ranking the relative risk of each failure mode. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pre-Flight Checklist" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine an airline pilot preparing for takeoff. Before the flight, the pilot does not *wait* for an engine to fail; instead, the pilot and ground engineers proactively review every system — hydraulics, fuel, navigation, communication — asking: *"What could go wrong here? How bad would it be? How likely is it? Would we even notice before takeoff?"* This is exactly the philosophy of FMEA.

FMEA does not "fix" a failure; it **anticipates**, **quantifies**, and **ranks** the failure so that engineering resources are deployed where they yield the maximum reduction in customer dissatisfaction. It is the difference between **firefighting** (reactive) and **fire-prevention engineering** (proactive).

### 1.4 Types of FMEA

| Type | Focus | Application Stage |
|------|-------|-------------------|
| **System FMEA (SFMEA)** | Overall system architecture, interfaces, and integration risks | Concept / Feasibility stage |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Design of components and subsystems | Product Development |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing and assembly operations | Production / Process Planning |
| **Service FMEA** | Field use, maintenance, and service operations | Post-delivery phase |

### 1.5 Standard Rating Scale Ranges

- **Severity (S):** 1 to 10 — where **10 = Hazardous (loss of life/catastrophic)** and **1 = None**
- **Occurrence (O):** 1 to 10 — where **10 = Very High (≥ 1 in 2)** and **1 = Remote (failure unlikely)**
- **Detection (D):** 1 to 10 — where **10 = Almost Impossible to detect** and **1 = Almost Certain detection**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher RPN values indicate greater risk.** A failure mode with RPN = 1000 demands immediate, top-priority action, whereas RPN values below 30 may be acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of FMEA — Underlying Logic Chain

The FMEA logic is built on **six logical steps** before any number is calculated:

1. **Define the System Boundary** — What is in scope? What is out? (Avoids infinite regression of causes.)
2. **Identify Functions** — What is the item/process *supposed* to do? (Failure is defined relative to a *function*, not an opinion.)
3. **Enumerate Failure Modes** — The complete *manner* in which the function can be lost.
4. **Determine Effects** — The *consequence* to the customer, system, or regulation.
5. **Identify Causes** — The *mechanism* of failure (e.g., vibration, corrosion, operator error).
6. **Identify Current Controls** — What prevents the cause or detects the failure today?

Only **after** the qualitative analysis is complete do we apply the quantitative ratings.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the definitive reference for all KTU numerical problems on FMEA.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit / Range | Engineering Interpretation |
|------------------|------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Dimensionless index, 1 to 1000 | Composite risk score; higher = more urgent action needed |
| **Severity Rating (S)** | $S \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10\}$ | Scale 1–10 | Magnitude of harm to customer |
| **Occurrence Rating (O)** | $O \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10\}$ | Scale 1–10 | Probability / frequency of failure |
| **Detection Rating (D)** | $D \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10\}$ | Scale 1–10 | Inverse measure of detectability |
| **Severity Index Ratio** | $S_{ratio} = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{S_{max}} = \dfrac{S}{10}$ | Normalized 0–1 | Useful for benchmarking across product lines |
| **Percentage Risk Reduction** | $\Delta RPN \% = \dfrac{RPN_{before} - RPN_{after}}{RPN_{before}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Measures effectiveness of corrective action |
| **Action Priority (AP) — AIAG-VDA** | $AP = f(S, O, D)$ qualitative matrix | H / M / L | Modern replacement for RPN, recommended from 2019 |
| **Cumulative RPN (ΣRPN)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} RPN_i$ | Sum of all RPNs | Tracks total project/process risk over time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Update:** The **AIAG-VDA (2019)** harmonized FMEA handbook has replaced the pure RPN approach with the **Action Priority (AP)** method. AP classifies each failure mode as **H (High)**, **M (Medium)**, or **L (Low)** based on a logic matrix of S, O, and D. However, for KTU numerical problems, RPN remains the **primary scoring method** unless explicitly stated.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

FMEA is mandatory in the following industries and is a KTU-favoured exam topic due to its high industrial relevance:

- **Automotive** — Mandatory for ISO/IATF 16949 compliance (OEMs like Tata, Maruti, Bosch).
- **Aerospace & Defense** — AS9100-compliant risk analysis (HAL, DRDO labs).
- **Medical Devices** — FDA Design Controls (21 CFR 820.30).
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing** — Used at Intel, TSMC for yield improvement.
- **Hospitality & Service Sector** — Failure-proofing of check-in, billing, and housekeeping workflows.

### 2.4 Threshold and Decision Rules (KTU Board-Standard)

| RPN Range | Recommended Action |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **1 – 30** | Acceptable — Monitor periodically |
| **31 – 100** | Document — Consider low-cost improvements |
| **101 – 300** | Action Required — Plan corrective action within timeline |
| **301 – 1000** | Critical — Immediate engineering intervention mandatory |

> [!NOTE]
> The thresholds above are typical industry defaults. KTU problems may specify custom thresholds. **Always read the question stem carefully.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Seven-Stage FMEA Procedure (Expanded)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical 7-step procedure** expected in KTU 14-mark descriptive answers. Memorize and reproduce the logical flow.

**Step 1: Assemble a Cross-Functional Team**
The team must include members from **Design, Manufacturing, Quality, Service, Suppliers, and the Customer-Voice function**. A single-discipline FMEA has an average **35% lower detection capability** for cross-functional failure modes.

**Step 2: Define Scope, Boundaries, and Assumptions**
Use a **P-diagram** (Parameter diagram) to identify Noise factors, Error states, and Control inputs. This prevents scope creep.

**Step 3: Identify Failure Modes**
Use brainstorming, the 4M framework (Man, Machine, Material, Method), and historical data from warranty claims or field service reports.

**Step 4: Determine Effects and Assign Severity (S)**
Trace the effect forward through the system hierarchy. Use the **standardized severity table** (refer Section 2.2).

**Step 5: Determine Causes and Assign Occurrence (O)**
For each failure mode, list all potential causes and assign the **O-rating of the most likely cause**.

**Step 6: Identify Current Controls and Assign Detection (D)**
Detection rating reflects the **worst-case detection scenario** (i.e., the highest D rating among the existing controls).

**Step 7: Calculate RPN, Prioritize, and Re-evaluate**
After implementing corrective actions, the RPN is recalculated. The cycle repeats until risk is **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)**.

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — RPN Computation for an Automotive Brake System

Let us work through a **complete numerical problem** that mirrors KTU exam style.

**Problem Statement (Worked Example):**
> A leading automotive supplier analyses the **disc-brake assembly** of a passenger car. The R&D team identifies three potential failure modes during road-test simulation. The data is given below. **Compute the RPN for each failure mode, identify the highest-risk mode, and recommend a corrective action.**

| S. No. | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause | S | O | D |
|--------|--------------|--------|-------|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake-pad wear > 5 mm | Reduced braking efficiency | Driver overuse, poor pad material | 8 | 6 | 4 |
| 2 | Hydraulic fluid leak | Complete brake failure | Seal fatigue, corrosion | 10 | 3 | 7 |
| 3 | Rotor surface scoring | Steering vibration, noise | Debris contamination | 5 | 5 | 6 |

**Step 1: Apply the RPN formula**

$$RPN_1 = S_1 \times O_1 \times D_1 = 8 \times 6 \times 4$$

Evaluating the product:

$$RPN_1 = 48 \times 4 = 192$$

**Step 2: Compute RPN for Mode 2**

$$RPN_2 = S_2 \times O_2 \times D_2 = 10 \times 3 \times 7$$

$$RPN_2 = 30 \times 7 = 210$$

**Step 3: Compute RPN for Mode 3**

$$RPN_3 = S_3 \times O_3 \times D_3 = 5 \times 5 \times 6$$

$$RPN_3 = 25 \times 6 = 150$$

**Step 4: Rank the Failure Modes by Risk**

$$\boxed{RPN_2 \,(210) \;>\; RPN_1 \,(192) \;>\; RPN_3 \,(150)}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation and Action Mapping**

| Mode | RPN | Severity Tier | Recommendation |
|------|-----|---------------|----------------|
| **Hydraulic fluid leak** | 210 | High (S=10) | Top-priority: redesign seal geometry, switch to EPDM rubber, install leak-pressure sensor (D target ≤ 3) |
| **Brake-pad wear** | 192 | Medium-High | Schedule maintenance indicator, improve pad material (target O ≤ 4) |
| **Rotor scoring** | 150 | Medium | Add rotor cover, improve dust filtration (D target ≤ 4) |

**Step 6: Post-Action RPN Recalculation (Verification)**

Suppose for the hydraulic-leak mode, the team introduces a **leak-pressure sensor** (improves detection) and **upgrades to EPDM seal** (reduces occurrence):

- New $S_2' = 10$ (severity of complete brake failure cannot be redesigned down)
- New $O_2' = 1$ (seal material upgraded, occurrence now remote)
- New $D_2' = 2$ (sensor reliably detects the leak)

$$RPN_2' = S_2' \times O_2' \times D_2' = 10 \times 1 \times 2 = 20$$

**Step 7: Percentage Risk Reduction**

$$\Delta RPN \% = \frac{RPN_2 - RPN_2'}{RPN_2} \times 100$$

$$\Delta RPN \% = \frac{210 - 20}{210} \times 100$$

$$\Delta RPN \% = \frac{190}{210} \times 100 \approx 90.48\%$$

This represents a **90.48% reduction in risk**, comfortably below the typical industry ALARP threshold of 80%.

### 3.3 Critical Comparison Matrix — S vs. O vs. D

| Parameter | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| **What it measures** | Consequence | Probability | Test/Ctrl efficacy |
| **Can it be reduced by design?** | Limited (often fixed by safety regs) | Yes | Yes (more inspections/sensors) |
| **Direction of reduction** | Lower is safer | Lower is safer | Lower is safer (easier to detect) |
| **Source of data** | Customer-impact analysis | Field data, MTBF | Process FMEA, test records |
| **Mandatory reduction?** | Always (if S ≥ 9) | Always (if O ≥ 8) | Optional (D < 4 is ideal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A frequent examiner trick is to ask *"If you had to reduce RPN with a limited budget, which parameter would you target first?"* The answer is **Occurrence (O)** — because it is the most cost-effective to address through design changes. Detection improvements (sensors, gauges) are *expensive* and only valid when design is locked.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master FMEA Process Flow (7-Stage Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: FMEA Project] --> B1[Step 1: Assemble Cross-Functional Team]
    B1 --> B2[Step 2: Define Scope and Boundaries]
    B2 --> B3[Step 3: Identify Failure Modes]
    B3 --> B4[Step 4: Determine Effects and Rate Severity S]
    B4 --> B5[Step 5: Identify Causes and Rate Occurrence O]
    B5 --> B6[Step 6: Identify Controls and Rate Detection D]
    B6 --> C1[Step 7: Calculate RPN = S times O times D]
    C1 --> D1{RPN Greater than 100?}
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[Plan Corrective Action]
    E1 --> E2[Implement Action and Redesign]
    E2 --> E3[Recalculate RPN]
    E3 --> C1
    D1 -- No --> F1[Document and Monitor]
    F1 --> G1[Close FMEA Item]
    G1 --> H1[End]
```

### 4.2 Risk Classification Matrix (RPN Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Failure Mode Identified] --> Seval{Severity Rating}
    Seval -- S Greater than or Equal To 9 --> Crit[CRITICAL: Mandatory Redesign]
    Seval -- S Between 5 and 8 --> Smed[Major: Action Required]
    Seval -- S Less than 5 --> Slow[Minor: Document Only]

    Crit --> Oeval{Occurrence Rating}
    Oeval -- O Greater than 6 --> OveryHigh[VERY HIGH RISK: Halt Production]
    Oeval -- O Between 4 and 6 --> Ohigh[HIGH RISK: Schedule Corrective Action]
    Oeval -- O Less than 4 --> Omod[MODERATE: Monitor]

    Smed --> Deval{Detection Rating}
    Deval -- D Greater than 7 --> Dlow[Poor Detection: Add Sensors]
    Deval -- D Between 4 and 7 --> Dmod[Moderate Detection: Improve Inspections]
    Deval -- D Less than 4 --> Dgood[Good Detection: Acceptable]
```

### 4.3 FMEA Worksheet Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputPhase["INPUT DATA BLOCK"]
        I1[Item or Function]
        I2[Failure Mode]
        I3[Effect Description]
        I4[Potential Cause]
        I5[Current Control]
    end

    subgraph RatingPhase["QUANTITATIVE RATING BLOCK"]
        R1[S Rating 1 to 10]
        R2[O Rating 1 to 10]
        R3[D Rating 1 to 10]
    end

    subgraph OutputPhase["RISK OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        O1[RPN Computed]
        O2[Action Priority AP]
        O3[Recommended Action]
        O4[Responsibility and Target Date]
    end

    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5
    I5 --> R1
    I5 --> R2
    I5 --> R3
    R1 --> O1
    R2 --> O1
    R3 --> O1
    R1 --> O2
    R2 --> O2
    R3 --> O2
    O1 --> O3 --> O4
```

### 4.4 RPN Computation Flow (Mathematical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Assign Severity S] --> Q1[Multiply]
    P2[Assign Occurrence O] --> Q1
    P3[Assign Detection D] --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Compute RPN]
    R1 --> S1{RPN Threshold Exceeded?}
    S1 -- Yes --> T1[Trigger Corrective Action]
    S1 -- No --> T2[Archive in FMEA Register]
    T1 --> U1[Reassess After Action]
    U1 --> R1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** and explain how it is calculated in a Design FMEA.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a quantitative metric used in Failure Mode and Effect Analysis to rank the relative risk of each potential failure mode. It is calculated as the product of three ratings:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

where **S** = Severity (1–10), **O** = Occurrence (1–10), and **D** = Detection (1–10). The RPN ranges from 1 to 1000. Higher RPN values indicate greater risk and demand prioritized corrective action. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for range and interpretation).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Severity**, **Occurrence**, and **Detection** in FMEA.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Severity (S):** Measures the seriousness of the *consequence* of a failure mode to the customer or end-user. (1 mark)
- **Occurrence (O):** Measures the *likelihood or frequency* with which the failure mode will happen. (1 mark)
- **Detection (D):** Measures the *ability of the current control system* to detect the failure before it reaches the customer. (1 mark)

All three are rated on a 1–10 scale. Severity is often treated as non-negotiable, while Occurrence and Detection can be reduced by design and process changes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

> **(a) Explain the seven-step procedure of Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA) in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Assemble a Cross-Functional Team (1 mark):** Includes members from Design, Manufacturing, Quality, Service, and Supplier functions. A multidisciplinary team improves detection of failure modes by an average of 35%.

2. **Define Scope, Boundaries, and Assumptions (1 mark):** Specify the system under analysis, the design life, environmental conditions, and interfaces. Use a P-diagram to identify noise factors.

3. **Identify Failure Modes (1 mark):** For each component and function, list all possible ways the item could fail to deliver its intended function. Methods include brainstorming, Ishikawa (4M) analysis, and historical field-failure data.

4. **Determine Effects and Assign Severity (S) (1 mark):** Trace each failure forward to the next assembly, the system, and the end customer. Assign the S-rating from the standard 1–10 table. The effect on safety, function, and regulations must be considered.

5. **Identify Causes and Assign Occurrence (O) (1 mark):** List all potential causes for each failure mode. Assign the O-rating of the **most likely** cause, based on field data, MTBF, and supplier history.

6. **Identify Current Controls and Assign Detection (D) (1 mark):** For each cause, identify the existing prevention and detection controls (e.g., SPC, gauge, visual inspection). Assign the D-rating reflecting the **worst-case** (highest) D among the controls.

7. **Calculate RPN, Prioritize, and Re-evaluate (1 mark):** Compute $RPN = S \times O \times D$. Sort failure modes in descending RPN. Implement corrective actions for high-RPN modes, then recalculate. The cycle continues until risk is **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable).

> **(b) The following failure modes were identified in a coffee vending machine. Calculate the RPN for each, rank them, and recommend corrective action for the highest-risk mode. (7 Marks)**

| S. No. | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D |
|--------|--------------|--------|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heating element burnout | Cold beverage dispensed | 7 | 4 | 5 |
| 2 | Coin sensor jam | Machine rejects valid payment | 6 | 7 | 3 |
| 3 | Water pump failure | No liquid dispensed | 8 | 3 | 8 |
| 4 | Display screen flicker | Customer confusion | 3 | 5 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute RPN for each mode**

$$RPN_1 = 7 \times 4 \times 5 = 140$$

$$RPN_2 = 6 \times 7 \times 3 = 126$$

$$RPN_3 = 8 \times 3 \times 8 = 192$$

$$RPN_4 = 3 \times 5 \times 4 = 60$$

**Step 2: Rank in descending order**

$$\boxed{RPN_3 \,(192) \;>\; RPN_1 \,(140) \;>\; RPN_2 \,(126) \;>\; RPN_4 \,(60)}$$

**Step 3: Corrective Action for the Highest-Risk Mode (Water Pump Failure):**

- **Design change:** Install a **dual-pump parallel configuration** with automatic switchover. (Addresses Occurrence)
- **Detection improvement:** Add a **flow-rate sensor** with audible alarm and a 30-day predictive maintenance alert. (Addresses Detection)
- **Recommended Targets:** New $O' \leq 2$, new $D' \leq 3$. Severity (S = 8) cannot be reduced without a complete product redesign.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **rank** the failure modes in descending order before recommending action. Marks (1 mark) are specifically reserved for the **prioritization step** in 14-mark problems. Also, many students recommend actions for *all* modes — this is unrealistic in industry. Focus only on the **top 1–2 RPN modes**, as that is what an experienced FMEA facilitator would do.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

> **(a) Explain the different types of FMEA. Differentiate between DFMEA and PFMEA. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major types of FMEA are (1 mark each, plus 1 mark for synthesis):

1. **System FMEA (SFMEA):** Conducted at the conceptual and architectural level. Evaluates the **integration of subsystems** and the impact of failures at the system level. Used during feasibility and system-design stages.

2. **Design FMEA (DFMEA):** Focuses on the **design of the product itself**. It examines failure modes arising from design choices (geometry, material, tolerances). Conducted during product development, before tooling release.

3. **Process FMEA (PFMEA):** Focuses on the **manufacturing and assembly process**. It examines failure modes arising from human error, machine variability, method errors, and material inconsistencies. Conducted before the start of production (SOP) and updated for any process change.

4. **Service FMEA:** Applied to **field-use, maintenance, and warranty scenarios**. Identifies failures that may not appear in-house but occur in customer environments (e.g., extreme temperature, misuse, transportation damage).

**Differentiation Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DFMEA | PFMEA |
|--------|-------|-------|
| **Focus** | Product design | Manufacturing process |
| **Stage** | Product development | Production planning / Pre-SOP |
| **Customer** | End user of the product | Internal manufacturing line / assembler |
| **Causes** | Design weaknesses, material choices | Operator error, machine variation, method gaps |
| **Controls** | Design verification, simulation, prototype tests | SPC, gauge control, mistake-proofing (poka-yoke) |
| **Severity source** | Impact on end customer | Impact on next operation / final inspection |

> **(b) A printed circuit board (PCB) has the following failure modes after thermal cycling. Compute RPN and recommend which mode should be tackled first. Justify using a percentage-risk-reduction argument. (7 Marks)**

| S. No. | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D |
|--------|--------------|--------|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solder joint crack | Open circuit, board failure | 9 | 5 | 6 |
| 2 | Capacitor delamination | Capacitance drop, performance loss | 7 | 4 | 4 |
| 3 | Trace oxidation | Intermittent connectivity | 6 | 6 | 7 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute RPN**

$$RPN_1 = 9 \times 5 \times 6 = 270$$

$$RPN_2 = 7 \times 4 \times 4 = 112$$

$$RPN_3 = 6 \times 6 \times 7 = 252$$

**Step 2: Rank and Identify Highest-Risk Mode**

$$\boxed{RPN_1 \,(270) \;>\; RPN_3 \,(252) \;>\; RPN_2 \,(112)}$$

The **Solder joint crack (Mode 1)** must be tackled first.

**Step 3: Corrective Action and Post-Action RPN**

- **Action:** Switch from Sn-Pb solder to **SAC305 lead-free alloy** with **conformal coating**. (Addresses Occurrence: $O$ drops from 5 to 2.)
- **Add X-ray inspection** post-reflow to detect hidden cracks. (Addresses Detection: $D$ drops from 6 to 3.)

**Step 4: Percentage Risk Reduction**

$$RPN_1' = 9 \times 2 \times 3 = 54$$

$$\Delta RPN \% = \frac{270 - 54}{270} \times 100 = \frac{216}{270} \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Step 5: Justification**

The corrective action delivers an **80% risk reduction**, surpassing the industry benchmark of 70% for "actionable" FMEA items. Severity (S = 9) cannot be reduced because PCB failure at the customer end is a hard-failure event that the design must prevent, not mitigate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit of RPN:** RPN is a *dimensionless index*, not a percentage. Do not write "RPN is 80%."
> 2. **Confusing Detection with Occurrence:** Detection is the ability to *find* the failure; Occurrence is the *probability* of the failure happening.
> 3. **Adding rows of actions without recomputing RPN:** Always show the **post-action RPN** to demonstrate measurable improvement — this is worth 2 full marks in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Skipping the "rank in descending order" step:** Many students compute RPN correctly but lose 1 mark for not writing it as a ranked list.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FMEA**

- [ ] **FMEA = Proactive + Structured + Cross-Functional.** Not a one-time document; a **living document** updated with every design or process change.
- [ ] **Core formula:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$, range 1 to 1000.
- [ ] **Three ratings, three distinct meanings:** S = consequence, O = probability, D = catchability.
- [ ] **All scales are 1–10.** Higher = worse, except in interpretation where the "ideal" S/O/D are all 1.
- [ ] **Severity (S) is often fixed** by safety regulation; it cannot be redesigned down easily. Focus on O and D for quick wins.
- [ ] **Action Priority (AP)** — H / M / L from the AIAG-VDA 2019 method — is the modern KTU-recognised alternative. Always mention it in 7+ mark theory answers for extra credit.
- [ ] **RPN thresholds (industry default):** <30 Accept, 31–100 Monitor, 101–300 Action, >300 Critical.
- [ ] **The 7-step procedure:** Team → Scope → Failure Modes → Effects+S → Causes+O → Controls+D → RPN + Recalculate.
- [ ] **FMEA does not fix the problem; it identifies and prioritises the problems to fix.** Corrective action is the next step in the PDCA cycle.
- [ ] **Re-evaluate after every corrective action.** RPN reduction ≥ 70% is the typical "actionable" target.
- [ ] **DFMEA ≠ PFMEA.** Remember: design is for the *customer*, process is for the *manufacturing line*.
- [ ] **Common FMEA outputs:** RPN, AP, recommended action, responsibility, target completion date, evidence of effectiveness.

> **End of KTU-Premier-Engine V10 Notes — Failure Mode and Effect Analysis**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Systems: ISO 9000 Family of Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET612 - QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Systems: ISO 9000 Family of Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ISO 9000 Family of Standards** refers to a globally recognized, comprehensive set of international standards developed and published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** that govern the establishment, implementation, maintenance, and continual improvement of a **Quality Management System (QMS)** within any organization, irrespective of its size, type, or the nature of products and services it delivers.

The ISO 9000 family provides a **framework** rather than a one-size-fits-all recipe, allowing organizations to define and document their own quality policies, objectives, and processes while conforming to universally accepted quality assurance principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** "The ISO 9000 family of standards addresses various aspects of quality management and contains some of ISO's best-known standards. It provides guidance and tools for companies and organizations who want to ensure that their products and services consistently meet customer's requirements, and that quality is consistently improved."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Global Passport" Analogy** 🛂
>
> Imagine you run a small restaurant in Kerala. A tourist from Germany wants to eat at your restaurant but cannot judge its hygiene, ingredient quality, or service reliability just by looking. Now, suppose there is an **internationally recognized body** that awards a "trust seal" to restaurants that follow certain hygiene, sourcing, and service standards — verified through regular independent inspections. That seal (say, ISO 9001 certified) becomes a **quality passport** recognized worldwide. The tourist can now confidently dine at your restaurant.
>
> In the same way, ISO 9000 standards act as a **universal language of quality** — they do not tell you *what* to cook, but they tell you *how* to run your kitchen systematically so that consistent quality is delivered every time.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **ISO** | International Organization for Standardization — a non-governmental, independent body with 168+ national standards bodies as members, headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland. |
| **QMS** | Quality Management System — the formalized system that documents processes, procedures, and responsibilities for achieving quality policies and objectives. |
| **Certification** | A third-party written assurance (issued by a Certification Body) that an organization's QMS conforms to a specific standard (e.g., ISO 9001:2015). |
| **Accreditation** | Formal recognition by an authoritative body (usually a national body like NABCB in India) that a Certification Body is competent to perform specific certification activities. |
| **Audit** | A systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining audit evidence and evaluating it objectively to determine the extent to which audit criteria are fulfilled. |
| **Conformity** | Fulfillment of a requirement. |
| **Process Approach** | Managing activities as interrelated processes that function as a coherent system (Clause 4.4 of ISO 9001:2015). |

### 1.4 Historical Evolution (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ISO 9000 family was **first published in 1987**. It was derived from the **BS 5750** standards (British Standards), which themselves had roots in **NATO defense procurement standards** (AQAP — Allied Quality Assurance Publications) developed in the late 1950s and 1960s.
>
> **Major Revisions:**
> - **ISO 9001:1987** — First issue (3 standards: 9001, 9002, 9003)
> - **ISO 9001:1994** — Clarifications
> - **ISO 9001:2000** — Major structural overhaul; introduced **process model** and **8 QMPs**
> - **ISO 9001:2008** — Minor clarifications
> - **ISO 9001:2015** — Current version; introduced **7 QMPs**, **risk-based thinking**, and the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** / Annex SL framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Table

### 2.1 The Four Core Standards of the ISO 9000 Family

The family is not a single document but a coordinated set of standards. The four cornerstone standards are:

#### A. ISO 9000:2015 — *Quality Management Systems — Fundamentals and Vocabulary*
- Establishes the **definitions** and **fundamental concepts** (terminology) used across the family.
- Explains the **7 Quality Management Principles (QMPs)**.
- Describes the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** and the **process model**.
- Acts as the **dictionary** of the family.

#### B. ISO 9001:2015 — *Quality Management Systems — Requirements*
- This is the **only standard** in the family against which an organization can be **certified**.
- Specifies the **requirements** a QMS must meet.
- Uses the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** — **10 clauses**.
- Applicable to **any organization** regardless of type, size, or industry (10-clause Annex SL structure).

#### C. ISO 9004:2018 — *Quality Management — Quality of an Organization — Guidance to Achieve Sustained Success*
- Goes **beyond ISO 9001**.
- Provides guidance on achieving **sustained success** by addressing both **effectiveness** and **efficiency**.
- **Not used for certification** — purely advisory.
- Introduces the concept of the **mature QMS** and self-assessment methodology.

#### D. ISO 19011:2018 — *Guidelines for Auditing Management Systems*
- Provides **guidelines** on the **management of audit programs**, the conduct of internal and external audits, and the **competence of auditors**.
- Replaced the older ISO 10011 series and harmonizes auditing across multiple management systems (e.g., ISO 14001, ISO 45001).

### 2.2 The Seven Quality Management Principles (QMPs) — *High-Yield KTU Topic*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These 7 principles were introduced in **ISO 9000:2015** and **ISO 9001:2015** (replacing the 8 principles of the 2000 version by merging related concepts).

| # | Principle | Core Idea | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Primary focus is meeting customer needs and exceeding expectations. | A bakery tracking repeat customer feedback on cake sweetness. |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Leaders establish unity of purpose and engage people. | A CEO visibly championing a "zero-defect" vision. |
| 3 | **Engagement of People** | Competent, empowered, and engaged people at all levels enhance the organization's capability. | Frontline staff stopping a defective batch without manager approval. |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | Consistent and predictable results are achieved more effectively when activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes. | Defining the "order-to-delivery" workflow as a linked process. |
| 5 | **Improvement** | Successful organizations have an ongoing focus on improvement. | Kaizen suggestions from employees reviewed monthly. |
| 6 | **Evidence-Based Decision Making** | Decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data are more likely to produce desired results. | Using SPC charts instead of intuition to adjust a machine. |
| 7 | **Relationship Management** | For sustained success, an organization manages its relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners, regulators). | Long-term contracts with ethically audited vendors. |

### 2.3 The PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)

The **PDCA cycle** is the operating engine of the ISO 9001 QMS. Every process within the QMS follows this continuous improvement loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Plan your wedding, Do the rituals, Check the photos, Act on the feedback."* 😄

| Stage | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Establish objectives, processes, and procedures necessary to deliver results (risk & opportunity analysis). | Quality Plan, QMS scope, quality objectives. |
| **Do** | Execute the planned processes — implement training, perform work. | Actual product/service, operational records. |
| **Check** | Monitor, measure, and analyze results against objectives (audit & review). | Audit reports, KPIs, performance data. |
| **Act** | Take actions to continually improve performance based on Check results. | Corrective actions, preventive actions, improvements. |

### 2.4 ISO 9001:2015 — High-Level Structure (Annex SL / 10 Clauses)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **High-Level Structure (HLS)** was introduced to align ISO 9001 with other management system standards (ISO 14001, ISO 45001, ISO 27001). The 10 clauses are mandatory for any ISO management standard.

| Clause | Title | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scope** | Defines applicability of the standard. |
| 2 | **Normative References** | References to ISO 9000:2015. |
| 3 | **Terms and Definitions** | Vocabulary (cross-ref to ISO 9000). |
| 4 | **Context of the Organization** | Internal/external issues, scope of QMS, processes. |
| 5 | **Leadership** | Top management commitment, customer focus, policy, roles. |
| 6 | **Planning** | Actions to address risks/opportunities, quality objectives, change planning. |
| 7 | **Support** | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information. |
| 8 | **Operation** | Operational planning and control, product/service requirements. |
| 9 | **Performance Evaluation** | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review. |
| 10 | **Improvement** | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement. |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Summary Table — ISO 9000 Family Standards

| Standard | Purpose | Used for Certification? | Mandatory for QMS? |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| **ISO 9000:2015** | Vocabulary & Fundamental concepts | ❌ No | 📖 Reference only |
| **ISO 9001:2015** | QMS Requirements | ✅ **Yes** | ✅ **Mandatory** |
| **ISO 9004:2018** | Guidance for sustained success | ❌ No | 📖 Advisory only |
| **ISO 19011:2018** | Auditing guidelines | ❌ No | 📖 Advisory only |
| **ISO 17021-1** | Requirements for certification bodies | ❌ (for certification bodies) | N/A for end-orgs |

### 2.6 Benefits of Implementing ISO 9001 (KTU 14-Mark Favorites)

1. **Enhanced Customer Confidence** — Globally recognized quality assurance.
2. **Operational Efficiency** — Reduced waste, rework, and operational costs.
3. **Improved Risk Management** — Risk-based thinking (Clause 6.1).
4. **Market Access** — Often a prerequisite for tenders (especially in aerospace, defense, automotive).
5. **Employee Engagement** — Clear roles, responsibilities, and processes.
6. **Legal & Regulatory Compliance** — Systematic framework ensures adherence.
7. **Continual Improvement** — Built-in PDCA ensures the QMS evolves.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Application

> [!NOTE]
> In an **automotive component manufacturing unit in Kerala**, ISO 9001:2015 certification is essential to supply to OEMs like Bosch or Tata Motors. The QMS ensures that every batch of brake pads is produced under controlled conditions (Clause 8), tested against specifications (Clause 9), and that any deviation triggers corrective action (Clause 10). The PDCA cycle ensures that every defect analyzed leads to a process refinement — embodying the spirit of "Quality is not an act, it is a habit."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Walkthrough

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Old vs. New ISO 9001 Revisions

> This analytical breakdown is **critical** for KTU 14-mark questions that ask for differences between editions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISO 9001:2000/2008} &\xrightarrow{\text{Major Revision}} \text{ISO 9001:2015} \\[6pt]
\text{8 QMPs} &\rightarrow \text{7 QMPs (merged)} \\[4pt]
\text{No risk-based thinking} &\rightarrow \text{Risk-based thinking throughout (Clause 6.1)} \\[4pt]
\text{Procedural emphasis} &\rightarrow \text{Process approach + PDCA explicit} \\[4pt]
\text{Prescriptive documentation} &\rightarrow \text{\textquotedblleft Documented Information\textquotedblright (flexible)} \\[4pt]
\text{Separate standards} &\rightarrow \text{High-Level Structure (Annex SL) aligned with ISO 14001, 45001} \\[4pt]
\text{Preventive action clause} &\rightarrow \text{Absorbed into \textquotedblleft risks and opportunities\textquotedblright}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Process of ISO 9001 Implementation (For 14-Mark "Discuss the Implementation of ISO 9001" type questions)

#### Step 1: Top Management Commitment
- The leadership issues a **quality policy** aligned with strategic direction.
- Assigns a **Management Representative (MR)** and a cross-functional implementation team.
- **Output:** Signed quality policy, MR appointment letter.

#### Step 2: Define Scope of QMS
- Identify boundaries and applicability.
- Justify any exclusions (Clause 4.3).
- **Output:** QMS Scope Statement (e.g., "Design and manufacture of precision gears for the automotive industry, Bangalore plant only").

#### Step 3: Context Analysis (Clause 4)
- Identify **internal issues** (culture, performance, resources).
- Identify **external issues** (market trends, regulations, competition).
- Identify **interested parties** (customers, regulators, suppliers, employees) and their relevant requirements.
- **Output:** SWOT Analysis, PESTLE Analysis, Interested Party Matrix.

#### Step 4: Risk & Opportunity Assessment (Clause 6.1)
- For each process, identify risks (what could go wrong) and opportunities (what could go better).
- Use tools: **FMEA, Risk Register, Failure Mode Matrix**.
- **Output:** Risk Register with severity, likelihood, and mitigation plans.

#### Step 5: Establish Processes & Process Map
- Document **every process** in terms of:
  - Inputs
  - Outputs
  - Sequence and interaction
  - Performance indicators (KPIs)
  - Responsible owner
  - Risks and opportunities
- **Output:** Process Map (a visual flowchart of all core, support, and management processes).

#### Step 6: Define Documented Information
- The 2015 version uses the term **"documented information"** instead of "documents/records".
- Mandatory documented information:
  - Quality policy (5.2)
  - Quality objectives (6.2)
  - QMS scope (4.3)
  - Evidence of competence (7.2)
  - Internal audit results (9.2)
  - Management review results (9.3)
  - Nature of nonconformities and corrective actions (10.2)

#### Step 7: Operational Implementation (Clause 8)
- Execute operational planning and control.
- Implement **change control** procedures.
- Establish **product/service requirements review** mechanism.

#### Step 8: Training & Awareness (Clause 7.2 & 7.3)
- Conduct competency gap analysis.
- Conduct training programs.
- Document effectiveness evaluation.

#### Step 9: Internal Audits (Clause 9.2)
- Plan, conduct, and document internal audits as per **ISO 19011:2018**.
- Select competent auditors (independent of the audited area).
- Report findings; ensure top management reviews results.

#### Step 10: Management Review (Clause 9.3)
- Conducted at planned intervals (typically every 6 months).
- Inputs: Audit results, customer feedback, process performance, KPI status, risk treatment, follow-ups, opportunities for improvement.
- **Output:** Decisions for continual improvement, resource allocation, change plans.

#### Step 11: Certification Audit
- **Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review):** Certification Body reviews QMS documentation for conformance to ISO 9001:2015.
- **Stage 2 Audit (On-site Audit):** Verifies effective implementation at the site.
- If compliant, **certificate is issued** (valid for 3 years, with surveillance audits annually).

#### Step 12: Continual Improvement (Clause 10)
- Analyze **nonconformities** — apply **Root Cause Analysis (5-Why, Fishbone)**.
- Implement **corrective actions** and verify effectiveness.
- Document **lessons learned**.

### 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — PDCA Applied to a Single Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Process: "Customer Complaint Handling"**

| Stage | Detailed Activity | Documented Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Define objective: "Reduce complaint resolution time to ≤ 48 hours". Identify input: customer email. Identify output: resolved complaint. Owner: Customer Service Lead. KPI: resolution time, recurrence rate. | SOP-CSP-01, KPI tracker |
| **DO** | Train staff. Log every complaint in CRM. Assign ticket. Investigate root cause. Communicate with customer. | CRM logs, training records, RCA sheets |
| **CHECK** | Weekly review of resolution time. Monthly audit of 10% cases. Calculate Pareto of complaint types. | Weekly reports, audit reports |
| **ACT** | If KPI met → standardize. If not → corrective action (revise SOP, re-train, escalate supplier issue). Update risk register. | Corrective Action Report, revised SOP |

### 3.4 Mathematical Risk Prioritization (KTU Numerical Appliqué)

For **risk-based thinking** (Clause 6.1), organizations often compute a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where **S** = Severity (1–10), **O** = Occurrence (1–10), **D** = Detection difficulty (1–10).

| Risk | S | O | D | RPN | Action |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|---|
| Bearing failure in gearbox | 9 | 4 | 3 | 108 | High priority — redesign |
| Color mismatch on packaging | 2 | 5 | 6 | 60 | Medium — improve QC training |
| Label print smudge | 1 | 3 | 4 | 12 | Low — monitor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Risks with **RPN > 100** must have **documented mitigation strategies** before ISO 9001 audit.

### 3.5 Difference Matrix — Certification vs. Accreditation (Common KTU 2-Mark Question)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Certification:} &\quad \text{CB} \rightarrow \text{Organization (e.g., ISO 9001:2015)} \\
\textbf{Accreditation:} &\quad \text{AB} \rightarrow \text{Certification Body} \\
\text{Example in India:} &\quad \text{NABCB (National Accreditation Board for Certification Bodies)}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Aspect | Certification | Accreditation |
|---|---|---|
| **Granted by** | Certification Body (CB) | Accreditation Body (AB) |
| **Granted to** | An organization's QMS | A Certification Body |
| **Recognizes** | Conformance to a standard | Competence to certify |
| **Example** | TÜV SÜD certifies Infosys Ltd. | NABCB accredits TÜV SÜD |
| **Hierarchy Level** | Level 3 (organization level) | Level 2 (body level) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Architecture of the ISO 9000 Family

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["ISO 9000 FAMILY OF STANDARDS"] --> nodeB["ISO 9000 2015"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["ISO 9001 2015"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["BEYOND ISO 9001 GUIDANCE"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["SUSTAINED SUCCESS APPROACH"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["NOT FOR CERTIFICATION"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["AUDIT PROGRAM MANAGEMENT"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["AUDITOR COMPETENCE"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["INTERNAL AND EXTERNAL AUDITS"]

    nodeF --> nodeF1["ISO 10001 CUSTOMER SATISFACTION"]

```



<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define ISO 9000 family of standards. List any four standards in this family.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Approx. 100 words):**

The **ISO 9000 family** is a coordinated set of international standards published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** that address quality management systems. It provides a common framework, vocabulary, and requirements for organizations to ensure they consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements.

**Four core standards:**
1. **ISO 9000:2015** – Quality management systems – Fundamentals and vocabulary.
2. **ISO 9001:2015** – Quality management systems – Requirements (the only certifiable standard).
3. **ISO 9004:2018** – Guidance to achieve sustained success.
4. **ISO 19011:2018** – Guidelines for auditing management systems.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Certification and Accreditation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Form Recommended):**

| Aspect | Certification | Accreditation |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A third-party written assurance that a product/process/system conforms to a specified standard. | A third-party attestation that a conformity assessment body (e.g., certification body) is competent to carry out specific certification activities. |
| Granted to | An organization's QMS | A Certification Body (CB) |
| Granted by | Certification Body | Accreditation Body (e.g., NABCB in India) |
| Example | Infosys gets ISO 9001 certified by TÜV | NABCB accredits TÜV SÜD India |
| Purpose | Demonstrates conformance | Demonstrates competence |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — WITH INTERNAL CHOICE

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer ANY ONE out of the two questions (A or B). Each has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** of **ISO 9001:2015** with all **10 clauses**. Highlight the significance of the HLS framework.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Seven Quality Management Principles (QMPs)** of ISO 9000:2015 with one real-world example for each principle.

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **High-Level Structure (HLS)**, also known as **Annex SL**, is a mandatory framework structure adopted by ISO in 2012 to ensure uniformity across all management system standards (ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001, ISO 27001, etc.). It eliminates ambiguity and allows easy integration of multiple management systems.

**The 10 Clauses of ISO 9001:2015 (HLS):**

| Clause | Title | Core Content |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scope | Applicability of the standard. |
| 2 | Normative References | Reference to ISO 9000:2015. |
| 3 | Terms and Definitions | Vocabulary (cross-reference to ISO 9000). |
| 4 | Context of the Organization | Internal/external issues, scope, processes. |
| 5 | Leadership | Top management commitment, policy, roles. |
| 6 | Planning | Risks/opportunities, quality objectives, change planning. |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented info. |
| 8 | Operation | Operational planning, control of production/service provision. |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, internal audit, management review. |
| 10 | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement. |

**Significance of HLS (1.5 Marks dedicated answer):**
- **Common structure** across all ISO management system standards.
- Enables **integrated audits** (e.g., QMS + EMS + OHSMS together).
- Reduces **duplication** of documentation for organizations adopting multiple standards.
- Simplifies **auditor training** and **cross-standard migration**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiner gives 0.5 marks per clause identification (10 × 0.5 = 5) + 2 marks for the table representation/significance explanation = **7 marks**.

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 Marks

The **Seven Quality Management Principles (QMPs)** were introduced in ISO 9000:2015, replacing the 8 principles of ISO 9000:2000. They serve as the philosophical foundation of the standard.

| # | Principle | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Amazon India collecting post-delivery feedback to refine the delivery experience. |
| 2 | **Leadership** | A hospital Director personally auditing surgical outcomes and addressing gaps. |
| 3 | **Engagement of People** | Toyota's "Andon cord" — any worker can stop the assembly line on detecting a defect. |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | A bank defining the "loan approval process" with inputs (application), activities (verification), outputs (decision), KPIs (TAT). |
| 5 | **Improvement** | A software firm running bi-weekly retrospectives to enhance sprint velocity. |
| 6 | **Evidence-Based Decision Making** | A manufacturing plant using **SPC charts** to decide machine calibration schedules rather than relying on gut feel. |
| 7 | **Relationship Management** | Apple maintaining a multi-decade strategic partnership with TSMC for chip fabrication. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Each principle with its example = 1 mark × 7 = 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **benefits** and **limitations** of implementing an ISO 9001 QMS in an engineering organization.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **flow diagram**, explain the **PDCA cycle** as applied to **ISO 9001:2015** with one engineering example.

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Benefits of ISO 9001 Implementation (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Global Recognition & Market Access:** Many public tenders and OEMs (Bosch, Tata Motors) require ISO 9001 certification.
2. **Customer Satisfaction:** Systematic complaint handling and customer feedback loops.
3. **Operational Efficiency:** Reduced rework, scrap, and warranty costs (typical saving: 10–30%).
4. **Risk Management:** Built-in risk-based thinking (Clause 6.1).
5. **Employee Engagement:** Clear roles, responsibilities, and competency frameworks.
6. **Legal Compliance:** Systematic control over regulatory requirements.
7. **Continual Improvement:** PDCA ensures ongoing evolution.

**Limitations of ISO 9001 Implementation (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Documentation Overhead:** SMEs may find documented information requirements burdensome.
2. **Cost:** Certification and maintenance costs (audits, training, consultants) — typically ₹2–10 lakhs for SMEs.
3. **Time-Consuming:** Full implementation typically takes 12–18 months.
4. **Not a Product Quality Guarantee:** ISO 9001 certifies the **management system**, not the product itself — this is a common misconception.
5. **Generic Application:** Standards may not address industry-specific nuances (e.g., aerospace often requires AS9100).
6. **Bureaucracy Risk:** Over-formalization may stifle innovation and agility.
7. **Surveillance Burden:** Annual surveillance audits and triennial re-certifications require sustained effort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** 7 benefits × 0.5 mark = 3.5 marks + 7 limitations × 0.5 mark = 3.5 marks = **7 marks**. Examiner also accepts fewer points with deeper explanation (e.g., 3 points × 1.2 marks each).

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 Marks

The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle, also known as the **Deming Cycle**, is the operational backbone of the ISO 9001:2015 QMS.

**Application to a B.Tech Engineering Scenario:**
> *"Reducing surface roughness defects in CNC-machined aluminium alloy components."*

| Stage | Detailed Activity | Engineering Output |
|---|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Set target roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 µm. Identify inputs (cutting speed, feed, tool condition). Identify risks (tool wear). KPI: % components meeting Ra spec. | Quality Plan, SOP, Risk Register |
| **DO** | Apply new cutting parameters (SPC charts). Train operator. Log every batch. | Production logs, training records, SPC data |
| **CHECK** | Measure every 10th component. Compare against Ra target. Audit process weekly. | Measurement reports, Pareto charts |
| **ACT** | If Ra ≤ 0.8 µm achieved → standardize SOP. If not → analyze root cause (tool wear? vibration?) → adjust process and replan. | Corrective Action Report, Revised SOP |

**Flow Diagram (Hand-drawn / Mermaid equivalent):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["PLAN\nSet Ra objective\nIdentify processes"] --> nodeD["DO\nApply new CNC parameters\nTrain operator"]
    nodeD --> nodeC["CHECK\nMeasure surface roughness\nAudit weekly"]
    nodeC --> nodeA["ACT\nStandardize or Correct\nUpdate SOP"]
    nodeA --> nodeP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Tabular breakdown = 4 marks + flow diagram = 2 marks + engineering relevance explanation = 1 mark = **7 marks**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where Students Typically Lose Marks in ISO 9000 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ISO 9000 with ISO 9001.** ISO 9000 is the vocabulary standard; ISO 9001 is the requirements/certification standard. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this error.
>
> 2. **Listing the OLD 8 QMPs instead of the NEW 7 QMPs.** Since the 2015 revision, there are exactly 7. Mentioning 8 (the old 2000 version) loses **up to 3 marks** in a 7-mark question.
>
> 3. **Missing "Risk-Based Thinking."** Clause 6.1 is a **mandatory** part of any modern ISO 9001 discussion. Students often skip this → **−2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Stating "ISO 9001 ensures product quality."** WRONG. ISO 9001 certifies the **management system's ability to produce quality consistently**, not the product itself. This is a **favorite trick question** by KTU examiners.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Certification vs. Accreditation.** Examiner expects NABCB / National Accreditation Board as the example for accreditation in India.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to draw the PDCA diagram** in 7-mark questions. The flow diagram is **mandatory** — missing it costs **2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 ISO 9000 Family — Quick-Fire Recall List:**
>
> ✅ The ISO 9000 family was **first published in 1987** (derived from BS 5750).
>
> ✅ **Current version of ISO 9001:2015** uses the **High-Level Structure (Annex SL)** with **10 clauses**.
>
> ✅ **Only ISO 9001** is used for **certification**. ISO 9000, 9004, 19011 are **not certifiable**.
>
> ✅ The **7 Quality Management Principles (QMPs)** are: Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement of People, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decision Making, Relationship Management.
>
> ✅ **PDCA cycle** = Plan, Do, Check, Act — applied throughout ISO 9001.
>
> ✅ **Risk-based thinking** (Clause 6.1) replaces the older "preventive action" clause.
>
> ✅ **Documented Information** (2015) replaces the older terms "documents" and "records" (2008).
>
> ✅ **ISO 19011:2018** governs **audit programs** and **auditor competence**.
>
> ✅ **ISO 9004:2018** provides guidance for **sustained success** — not certifiable.
>
> ✅ **Certification Body** is accredited by an **Accreditation Body** (e.g., NABCB, India).
>
> ✅ **Internal audits** must be conducted by **competent, independent auditors** (as per ISO 19011).
>
> ✅ **Management Review** (Clause 9.3) is held at planned intervals (typically **every 6 months**).
>
> ✅ ISO 9001 certificates are valid for **3 years** with **annual surveillance audits**.
>
> ✅ **Cost of implementation** for an SME typically ranges from **₹2–10 lakhs** (keep handy for 7-mark questions).
>
> ✅ **Common misconception:** ISO 9001 does **NOT certify the product** — it certifies the **management system**.

---

### 5.5 Suggested Mnemonic for Clause Recall

> [!NOTE]
> 🧠 **"Silly Students, Carefully Thoughtful Lectures Provide Strong Improvements"**
> → **S**cope, **S**ystem Terms, **C**ontext, **T**erms (Clauses 1–4)
> → **L**eadership, **P**lanning, **S**upport, **O**peration, **P**erformance Evaluation, **I**mprovement (Clauses 5–10).

---

> **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 Notes — Quality Systems: ISO 9000 Family of Standards**
